field_semantics.unit_index: 1-based, matches the workbook's U<N> sheets and the generator's g5-2627-<subject>-u<N> slugs
field_semantics.cell_text: cleaned cell value; multi-row subject blocks are joined with a blank line in row order (see source_rows)
field_semantics.label_note: extra text the teacher typed into the row-label cell (column A) beyond the subject keyword
field_semantics.merged_span: [first_day, last_day] — at least one source cell was merged across these day columns; the text applies to the whole span, and no separate records exist for the covered days unless another source row supplied them
field_semantics.sections_detected: Literacy only: inline ELA section headings found in cell_text (advisory; full text is authoritative)

week_notes.wed:
Explore: (moved from Day 2)
Class Discussion (10 mins):
(Link to Writing aspect of Literacy - Research)
Define home country (where someone is from) and host country (where someone might live temporarily).
Ask: "Do you think someone from another country might see Qatar as their host country?"
Comparison Activity (15 mins):
Pair students and assign each pair a country (e.g., India, Philippines, UK).
Use pictures and facts to compare Qatar with the assigned country (e.g., traditions, weather, buildings).
Collage Introduction (15 mins):
Divide students into groups: one group focuses on Qatar (home country), while others represent assigned host countries.
Start planning their country collages: trace the shape of the country onto cardboard and plan what materials to use (sandpaper for deserts, blue tissue for water, etc.).
Reflection (5 mins):
Discuss: "What are some things that make Qatar different or similar to other countries?"

Grammar: Review Nouns
Nouns are things we can see/touch, they name something, and special/specific things like places. Orally and informally assess their knowledge by asking students to name nouns or you name words to allow them to stand if it's a noun or st if it’s not a noun. Avoid abstract nouns unless they are ready for them.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Classification Activity
For this activity, prepare a tray with 20 unrelated items—for instance, a spool of thread, an eraser, a juice box, etc. Alternatively, create a document with 20 images of items to put up on the screen. Divide your class into even groups. Set a timer and have each group divide the 20 items into four categories that make sense to them. For example, they may put an earring, a glove, a headset, a sock, and a smile into the category “things you wear.” Have groups work quietly so that their ideas are kept secret. When each group is finished, give each one time to present their categories and their rationale behind each category.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Balloon walking
Have your students pair up side-by-side (and hold hands - they may not want to hold hands, this can be optional). Then place a balloon in between the shoulders of each pair. The object of the activity is for the entire class to walk in a line without any of the balloons popping or falling to the ground. It’s heaps of fun!

(Get your balloon in advance)

9:40- The whole of 5th Grade - Mystery Game (LaCondria)

📐Unit 1: Math (Place Value and Operations) (padlet.com)

Lesson 1:
Read Numbers to Millions
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.NBT.A.2: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.NBT.A.1: Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right.

meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

Comparing Numbers to the Millions

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.NBT.A.2: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.NBT.A.2: Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.

High:

Teacher will model the process of writing a personal narrative for the whole group. I would suggest writing about something you all have in common. First day of school, the School Day yesterday, or a field trip from last year.
Focus on what to do if you get to a word that’s hard to spell by clapping syllables. Focus on Capital letters and full stops. Focus on getting one complete sentence for each section. Then allow time for the students to work on the same. Once or if time allows, then move to adding 3 sentences per section from First-Finally.

High:

Students should have been practicing orally what to say already.
 They should also know from your whole group example.

Students take A3 paper and fold it into eights. Write the following in each rectangle:
title, orientation, first, then, next after that, finally, I felt.

This is the structure for the Personal Narrative. For the orientation, please help students create the sentence. help them by asking when, who, and what. (example: Last Thursday, my family and I went to the cinema.)

Each section of the draft paper should now have only 1 sentence. (Example: First we got into the car.) Continue to tell what happened in each section using only 1 sentence.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
This website has lots of matching games for the human body
Dyslexic friendly powerpoint with yellow background:
Brainwave - The Brain.pptx

Powerpoint with white background for printing:
Brainwave wht background - The Brain.pptx

Put students into small groups/whole class to discuss points listed below after explaining what they are (see powerpoint for the points listed):
Ask the groups/class to sort the statements into whether they believe the statement to be true or false. Some of us are ‘left-brained’ and some are ‘right-brained,’ and this helps explain differences in how we learn. We only use 10% of our brain. Every child learns in a particular way - visual, auditory or kinaesthetic. The brain has finished developing by the time children reach their teenage years (puberty). Learning is due to the addition of new cells to the brain. When we sleep, our brain stops working. Everyone can multitask. Playing brain games makes you smarter.

Explain to the class that these statements are all false and are called neuromyths.
 “Neuromyth” is a compound word that joins: neuro, as in the cells related to the nervous system and the brain, with myth, as in a “widely held but false belief or idea”
Debunk the neuromyths and you can go through the explanations (see powerpoint)

Get each student to label a simple brain.

Brain SeeSaw
Label Brain/Facts

Left/Right Brain

meta.objectives: How the brain works
meta.assessment: Label the brain
meta.differentiation:
Label without the provided labeled image
Label using the provided labeled image
Label with the provided labeled image & adult support

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Explain to the students that they will be learning about neuroeducation
science. See if the children can give any ideas what they think this may be. Explain that neuroscience is the study of the brain and the nervous system and how it works. Then explain that the science of neuroeducation science combines the science of the brain and how we use our brain for learning.
Making Connections:
Sit students in a circle and give each child a word. Make sure all of the words are visible to everyone sitting in the circle. Select one child to start. They need to look for connections between the words. When they have made a connection, they pass the wool/string to the person with the connected word. Make sure that every time the ball or wool/string is passed the child also holds onto it. Then that person needs to look for another word which has a connection to their second word in the chain. It is ok if the same word receives multiple connections. The string will cross the circle a number of times in different places, trying to include every child. This represents a web of connectivity; our brains learn more successfully when connections are made.
.After learners have completed this activity, explain that this process of making connections is exactly what our brain does. Show the children a diagram of a neuron. (see powerpoint)

elastic brain

meta.objectives: Know about neuroeducation science
meta.assessment: Draw, label & record ideas on neurons
meta.differentiation:
Drawing, labeled with details written
Drawing with labels
Drawing only (with some adult support)

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Ask the students to share what they think they know about memory and how our brains process information.

Explain that there are 3 main parts of the brain that deal with memories. Scientists have
examined brains, collecting data when they are working to better understand learning. (Show a diagram of
the brain with the hippocampus, neocortex, amygdala and prefrontal cortex highlighted. - see powerpoint)
Set up a Memory lab for learners to take part in a variety of memory games and activities. Each game is
designed to use a different strategy that can be used to support memory in learning. Use instructions to allow students to rotate through the activities.
There are 4 different activities (see powerpoint).

meta.objectives:
To identify when a strategy to reduce stress and improve wellbeing is needed and act
accordingly
meta.assessment: Talk about differences in strategies
meta.differentiation:
Can name, describe and explain a strategy
Can name and describe a strategy
Can name a strategy

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Write the terms ‘growth mindset’ and ‘fixed mindset’ on the whiteboard.
Explore the children’s ideas for what each term might mean. and what they already know.
Explain that a growth mindset takes the view that we can always better ourselves. Everyone is different –
People have different skills and strengths, but if we practice and challenge ourselves, we can improve.
Learning is about change, we see this when we improve, but change happens inside our bodies and
brains too.
Someone with a growth mindset, takes on challenges as a chance to learn. They might not succeed, they
might fail and make mistakes, but we can learn from those mistakes and the repeated practice.
Someone with a fixed mindset takes a more rigid view of learning, that intelligence is fixed at birth, and
they are either natural experts at something or not. Fixed mindset individuals are more likely to only do
things they are good at and less likely to try things they are not so good at, as they believe those things
are ‘not for them’.
Watch both videos or choose between the 2:
Growth Mindset For Kids-Growth Mindset vs. Fixed Mindset-The Power Of Yet-Elementary-Middle School
(5 mins)
Growth Mindset
(2 mins)

Show the children some sentences and ask them to sort them into either a fixed mindset or a growth
Mindset.

Mindset Sorting Activity

Now that the children have an understanding of what a growth mindset is, ask them to share in groups
any examples in school or their home life where they have used a growth mindset. Ask for volunteers to
share. Highlight any stories where the child includes a reference to self-talk such as ‘so I told myself.......’
Explain that self-talk is a metacognitive strategy, by using our inner voice we can review where we have
got to, identify what we think the issue is and talk through in our heads ideas for moving forward. If we
are stressed or frustrated by what we see as failure, self-talk can help us to describe the situation
differently. For example, from this method not working I have learnt that...........so now I could try.............

meta.objectives: Select the most effective metacognitive strategies to improve learning
meta.assessment: Can talk about “mindset”
meta.differentiation:
Can talk about “mindset”, give personal example(s) and use reflective talk on what was used and what to use/do differently to improve on an outcome.
Can talk about “mindset”, give personal example(s) and use reflective talk
Can name the 2 different mindsets

meta.standards:
Reading Foundational Skills
RF.5.3 (CC): Know and apply grade-level phonics and word analysis skills in decoding words.
EE.RF.5.3: Use phonics and word analysis skills to decode words.
RF.5.3a (CC): Use combined knowledge of letter–sound correspondences, syllabication patterns, and morphology (e.g., roots and affixes) to accurately read unfamiliar multisyllabic words in context and out of context.
EE.RF.5.3a / EE.5.RF.3a: Apply phonics and word analysis skills to decode words.
RF.5.3b (CC): Read grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words.
EE.RF.5.3b / EE.5.RF.3b: Recognize common sight words.
Grammar/Language
L.5.1 (CC): Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage when writing or speaking.
L.5.1a (CC): Explain the function of conjunctions, prepositions, and interjections in general and their function in particular sentences.
EE.L.5.1b: Use frequently occurring nouns, verbs, and prepositions.
L.5.1e (CC): Use correlative conjunctions (e.g., either/or, neither/nor).
EE.L.5.1e: Use frequently occurring conjunctions.
Writing
W.5.3b (CC): Use narrative techniques, such as dialogue, description, and pacing, to develop experiences and events or show the responses of characters to situations.
W.5.5 (CC): With guidance and support from peers and adults, develop and strengthen writing as needed by planning, revising, editing, rewriting, or trying a new approach.
EE.W.5.5: With guidance and support, plan by brainstorming and revise own writing.

Being Human Week 5.pptx
records.label_note: Can be done as a centre activity: Get students to create a balanced plate after talking through what makes a healthy diet (diet is not about eating less but what is being spoken about is thinking about the food being put into our body. (see PowerPoint)

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
From previous week:
Class can watch the following video (again):
How Your Heart Works? - The Dr. Binocs Show | Best Learning Videos For Kids | Peekaboo Kidz
In this video Dr. Binocs explains the heart in a simple way.

Watch the video in sections so that the different parts and processes in the circulatory system can be emphasized to the children during the viewing. The path of the blood can be pointed out on the diagram below. (Can be printed so a copy is displayed near the screen).
[see PowerPoint for activity to do]
records.label_note: Can be done as a centre activity: Get students to create a balanced plate after talking through what makes a healthy diet (diet is not about eating less but what is being spoken about is thinking about the food being put into our body. (see PowerPoint)

meta.standards: Can name the body system that involves the heart
meta.assessment: Can you talk about the function of the heart and what happens?
meta.differentiation:
Give the number of syllables to the name of the body system
Support with the initial sound of the body system involving the heart
Knows the body system without support

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Start off with reminding students of what was worked on in the previous session - see PowerPoint. Briefly introduce the idea of fair testing in relation to looking after the heart before moving on to looking after the brain - this put here build up awareness in preparation for Science Fair activities.
 If the brain is an important organ in the human body then we need to find out how we can keep our brain healthy. Ask the students for their ideas. E.g. Drink water to provide hydration and oxygen for the brain’s cells Eat a balanced diet to provide energy – our brains consume a lot of energy Sleep so that the brain can rest and recover.
Go over these points with the class.

** Very interesting article that can be shared with students for next day activity about the food pyramids -  Traditional versus Nutritionally Improved:
Food Pyramid Article
records.label_note: Can be done as a centre activity: Get students to create a balanced plate after talking through what makes a healthy diet (diet is not about eating less but what is being spoken about is thinking about the food being put into our body. (see PowerPoint)

meta.standards: Knows things to do to look after our body systems
meta.assessment: What things can we do to keep our body systems healthy?
meta.differentiation:
Can give 1 idea
Can give 2 ideas
Can give more than 3 ideas

Guided Reading:

Introduction to Story Elements
Play the video: Story Elements  or Use SEESAW lesson

Introduce characters, setting, and plot:
Characters: Who is in the story.
Setting: Where and when the story takes place.
Plot: The sequence of events that happen in the story.
As you read, stop to ask questions like:
Who are the main characters?
Where is the story taking place?
What is happening so far?

Grammar:

Teaching Subjects and Predicates
Start by explaining what a sentence is: "A group of words that makes sense and expresses a complete thought." Discuss complete vs. incomplete sentences (e.g., "The dog" vs. "The dog runs").
Teaching Subjects and Predicates
Define subject: who or what the sentence is about. Define predicate: what the subject is doing or what is happening to the subject. Write simple sentences on the board and have students help you identify the subject and predicate.

Watch Video. Play this video on Brainpop: Subject and Predicates

Writing:
HOOK: Specific Instructions Challenge (Orange Bag with LaCondria)
(You may need two periods for trial and error and discussion)
Students give teacher ‘exact instructions’ on how to make a bowl of cereal. Teacher writes down the instructions they give (exactly as they tell you).
Teacher asks if they think the instructions are ready.
Teacher follows instructions EXACTLY as given.
After completion, the teacher asks students what they think caused the most problems with the task.
Watch this video of how to make cereal to discuss and compare:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sLROExP8VBY
Or this one:
Exact Instructions Challenge - THIS is why my kids hate me. | Josh Darnit (youtube.com)
Exact Instructions:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FN2RM-CHkuI
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sLROExP8VBY
(skip during Evan’s BORING story time 3min - 4:15)
What is Procedural Writing/How-To Writing?
(Grammar: Imperative Command Verbs, Adverbs, Time Connectives)
Use this powerpoint to help them understand what a procedural text is, the elements of a Procedural writing, and tips for writing it: https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/12-nSDWXiWdf4YFF9jHm2WtZrrkIkdPmmf3htNJw4C00/edit?usp=sharing
Reflect on the challenge from yesterday. Discuss as a class:
*When would we use a Procedural/How-To Writing? (recipes, GPS navigation, experiment, etc)
*Why is it important to have detailed/specific instructions? (examples from yesterday’s lesson)
What are the important elements of a procedural text?
[Title with goal (purpose), Materials (equipment), Ingredients (with amount), Method (step by step WITH PICTURES/ILLUSTRATIONS), and Conclusion (advice or pro-tip)]

Guided Reading:

Introduction to Conflict and Resolution
Define conflict: The problem in the story.
Define resolution: How the problem is solved.
Provide examples of common types of conflict (character vs. character, character vs. nature, character vs. self, etc.).
Read Aloud and Discussion
Read another short story (e.g.. .(NonFiction)
After reading, have students identify the conflict and resolution.
Discuss different types of conflict and how they affect the story.

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:

Lesson on Simple vs. Complete Subjects and Predicates
Explain the difference:
Simple subject: the main noun.
Complete subject: all words that describe the subject.
Simple predicate: the main verb.
Complete predicate: all words that tell what the subject does.
Write sentences and color-code the simple and complete subjects/predicates.
Distribute sentence strips. Have students work in pairs to identify both the simple and complete subjects and predicates.
Example: "The brown dog with a wagging tail barked loudly at the mailman." (Simple subject: dog, Complete subject: The brown dog with a wagging tail, Simple predicate: barked, Complete predicate: bark ed loudly at the mailman)

Writing:
Procedural/How-To Writing using present tense verbs (command form):
What is a verb?: https://jr.brainpop.com/readingandwriting/word/verbs/
What does verb “tense” mean?:
https://jr.brainpop.com/readingandwriting/word/tenses/
Practice finding the (present tense) verbs with this Seesaw Activity:
https://app.seesaw.me/pages/shared_activity?share_token=KM02CbdGRrOac0zbwfbXQA&prompt_id=prompt.091a2a76-925f-46dc-8a21-1c5f6c60297e
Discuss:
*Why use present tense when you are teaching someone how to do something?
*What would it sound like if you used past tense to teach someone how to do or make something?
Let’s practice past tense by playing this game:
https://www.abcya.com/games/journey_to_past_tense

Bossy Finger Game
Have a list of words ready, some with bossy “r” and some with “r,” but not bossy. Read the words aloud. As you say, give your student a moment to decide if the “r” is bossy or not. If it is, they should point a bossy finger at you. If not, no pointing at all! Here are some bossy “r” words to use on your list:  jar, art, farm, store, corn, orange, dirt, stir, girl, turn, fur, hurt, germ, her.
Guided Reading:

Review of Beginning, Middle, End
Quick review of the terms and examples of beginning, middle, and end.
Introduction to Rising Action, Climax, and Falling Action
Explain:
Rising Action: Events leading up to the most exciting part.
Climax: The turning point or most exciting part of the story.
Falling Action: Events after the climax that lead to the resolution.
Use a plot diagram to show where each fits into the story structure.

📐Unit 2: Math (Measurement and Data) (padlet.com)

Lesson 5: Measuring in Metric System

meta.standards:
Objective: Students will be able to measure length using metric units (millimeters, centimeters, meters, kilometers) and choose the appropriate unit for a given object or distance.
CCSS.Math.Content.4.MD.A.1: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two-column table. For example, express the length of a 4-ft rope in inches. Generate a conversion table for feet and inches listing the number pairs (1, 12), (2, 24), (3, 36), ...

Guided Reading:
Materials:
Short story or excerpt (either from a book being read in class or a provided short text)
Chart or board for class discussion
Lesson:
Introduction (3 minutes):
Define character traits as qualities or characteristics that describe what a character is like on the inside (e.g., brave, selfish, kind).
Ask students to think about someone from their favorite book or movie and describe them using a few traits.
Guided Practice (7 minutes):
Read a short passage featuring a main character from the story (or book) you’re studying.
After reading, ask the class to list the character's traits.
Write these traits on the board and ask students to provide evidence from the text (what the character says, does, or thinks) to support each trait.
Independent Practice (5 minutes):
Have students pick one character from the story and list two of their traits along with a supporting example from the text in their notebooks.
Closing (1 minute):
Recap: Character traits describe who a character is based on their thoughts, actions, and words.

Grammar:

Brainpop: Types of Sentences (brainpop.com) or

BrainpopJr: Types of Sentences
Introduction
Write two sentences on the board:
"The sun is shining."
"Is the sun shining?"
Ask students what is different about each sentence. Lead them to notice that one gives information (declarative), while the other asks a question (interrogative).
Explanation
Declarative Sentences: Explain that these sentences make a statement and end with a period.
Interrogative Sentences: Explain that these ask a question and end with a question mark.
Write a few more examples of each on the board.
Guided Practice
Call on students to say whether a sentence you read aloud is declarative or interrogative.
Example sentences:
"The cat is sleeping."
"Do you have a pencil?"
"She likes to dance."
"Can I have some water?"
Independent Practice (10 minutes):
Ask students to write two declarative and two interrogative sentences in their notebooks.

Create Anchor Chart
Writing:
Procedural Writing/How To’s

Review procedure writing.
Guided Powerpoint

AND/OR
Reflect on the cereal challenge.
Discuss as a class:
*When would we use a Procedural/How-To Writing? (recipes, GPS navigation, experiment, etc)
*Why is it important to have detailed/specific instructions? (examples from yesterday’s lesson)
What are the important elements of a procedural text?
[Title with goal (purpose), Materials (equipment), Ingredients (with amount), Method (step by step WITH PICTURES/ILLUSTRATIONS), and Conclusion (advice or pro-tip)]

Watch the EGG VIDEO then have students use their materials to complete the task at their desk independently. After they are finished, students will complete the SeeSaw Activity as an informal assessment.

Reading Informational Text

CC: RI.5.3-Explain the relationships or interactions between two or more individuals, events, ideas, or concepts in a text.EE.RI.5.3- Identify how two events or ideas are related in an informational text.

Reading Literature
EE.RL.5.1-Identify details or examples in a story that support the main idea or lesson.
CCSS:  RL.5.2-Determine a theme of a story, drama, or poem from details in the text, including how characters in a story or drama respond to challenges.
CCSS: RL.5.3-Compare and contrast two or more characters, settings, or events in a story or drama, drawing on specific details in the text (e.g., how characters interact).
EE.RL.5.3-Identify characters, settings, and major events in a story.

Writing

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:
Materials:
Same story/excerpt as Day 1
Introduction (3 minutes):
Explain that motivation is what pushes a character to act. It's what a character wants or needs.
Give an example: "If a character steals food, their motivation might be hunger."
Modeling (5 minutes):
Look at a scene from the story and ask, "Why did the character do this?" For example, if a character helped someone, their motivation might be kindness or wanting to make a new friend.
Guided Practice (5 minutes):
Read another short passage. Ask students to identify the character's action and discuss why they think the character acted this way. Encourage them to support their answers with evidence from the text.
Closing (2 minutes):
Summarize how understanding a character's motivation helps us understand their actions and the story.

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Review (5 minutes):
Quickly review declarative and interrogative sentences.
Ask students for one example of each.
Introduction to Imperative Sentences (10 minutes):
Write the following sentence on the board: "Please close the door."
Ask students if this is a question or a statement. Discuss how this is a command or a request. Explain that sentences that give orders, requests, or directions are called imperative sentences and usually end with a period (or occasionally an exclamation mark if it's urgent).
Share a few more examples:
"Take out your books."
"Listen carefully."
Guided Practice (10 minutes):
Ask students to give examples of commands or requests they hear every day (e.g., "Stand up," "Clean your room").
Write their examples on the board and highlight that these are imperative sentences.
Independent Practice (10 minutes):
Have students write/tell three imperative sentences of their own. (Think-Pair-Share)

Writing:
Complete the How to Make Eggs in a Mug writing.

Bossy Finger Game
Have a list of words ready, some with bossy “r” and some with “r,” but not bossy. Read the words aloud. As you say, give your student a moment to decide if the “r” is bossy or not. If it is, they should point a bossy finger at you. If not, no pointing at all! Here are some bossy “r” words to use on your list:  jar, art, farm, store, corn, orange, dirt, stir, girl, turn, fur, hurt, germ, her.

Guided Reading:
Materials:
Same story/excerpt as previous days
Introduction (3 minutes):
Explain that characters have relationships with other characters, and these relationships can help us understand the characters better.
Give examples of types of relationships (friends, family, enemies) and how they might affect a story.
Modeling (5 minutes):
Pick two characters from the story and explain their relationship. For example, "These two characters are friends, and they help each other during the story. This shows they are loyal."
Show how the relationship affects the story’s events.
Guided Practice (5 minutes):
Ask students to think of how two characters in the story interact with each other and how their relationship affects the plot. Have them share their ideas with the class.
Closing (2 minutes):
Summarize that understanding relationships helps us see how characters grow and how they affect the plot.

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Review (5 minutes):
Quickly review declarative, interrogative, and imperative sentences. Ask students to give one example of each.
Introduction to Exclamatory Sentences (10 minutes):
Write on the board: "What a beautiful day!"
Ask students what emotion this sentence shows. Explain that exclamatory sentences express strong emotions (like excitement, surprise, or anger) and always end with an exclamation mark.
Share additional examples:
"I can't believe it!"
"Wow, that’s amazing!"
"Help!"
Guided Practice (10 minutes):
Call on students to share exciting or surprising moments from their lives, and write them down as exclamatory sentences on the board.
Independent Practice (10 minutes):
Have students write/say two exclamatory sentences. (think-pair-share)

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:
Materials:
Same story/excerpt as previous days
Lesson (15 minutes):
Introduction (3 minutes):
Explain that as a story progresses, characters can change or learn something new. This is called character development.
Explain that the character’s growth often connects to the theme, or the message, of the story.
Modeling (5 minutes):
Discuss how the character changed from the beginning to the end of the story.
Example: “At first, the character was afraid of the dark, but by the end, they were brave. The story’s message might be about overcoming fear.”
Guided Practice (5 minutes):
Ask students to think about how the main character in the story has changed and what the character learned. Guide them to connect this change to the theme or message of the story.
Closing (2 minutes):
Summarize that character development is important to the story because it helps convey the message or theme

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:

Quick Review (10 minutes):
Write one example of each type of sentence (declarative, interrogative, imperative, exclamatory) on the board.
Ask students to identify each one.
Interactive Activity (15 minutes):
Play a game where students take turns coming up with sentences, and the class has to identify which type it is.
Example: One student says, "Do you like pizza?" and the class responds, "Interrogative!"
Sentence Writing (10 minutes):
Ask them to write one sentence of each type (declarative, interrogative, imperative, exclamatory).

Writing:
Procedural Writings isn’t just recipes. It could be teaching someone how to do something.
Think of a game you like to play. What are the directions of playing that game? (Use this template or same template in a seesaw activity:

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
This week we are going to find out about transforming materials.
Today we are going to look at Physical Changes
Looking at ice, chocolate and butter - using a scale, 3 beakers, a timer and an electric skillet.

Observe.
1. Which solid will melt the fastest? Predict the results.
2. Place each solid in a separate beaker and put in the skillet. Observe what happens.
3. Note the temperature on the skillet - 100 degrees centigrade is the boiling point of water.
4. Measure the time it takes for each solid to melt.
5. Order the results in a graph.
6. What will happen next? Are the changes permanent?
7. How can you turn them back to a solid?

Students can try and change the material back to a solid, how might that work?

Examples of Physical and Chemical Changes ( Updated )

Guided Reading:

Materials: “The Tortoise and the Hare” (Aesop’s Fable)
Lesson:
Hook (3 mins): Ask the class, “Has anyone ever had a disagreement with a friend or sibling? How did you feel?” Allow a few students to share briefly.
Teach (5 mins):
Explain that just like in real life, stories have problems called conflicts that characters face.
Write "Conflict" on the board and explain that it is what makes the story interesting. Without conflict, there’s no excitement.
Introduce the three types of conflict:
Character vs. Character
Character vs. Nature
Character vs. Self
Mentor Text (5 mins): Read “The Tortoise and the Hare” aloud. Ask, “What is the conflict in this story? Who is the conflict between?” Guide students to identify it as Character vs. Character (the tortoise and the hare competing against each other

Grammar:
MINTS- Slides for Capitalization with Practice

AND/OR

Warm-up (2-3 min):Ask students if they know when to use capital letters. Write a few examples on the board:
my name is sarah.
monday is my favorite day.
we went to the park yesterday.
Read the sentences aloud and ask students what's wrong. Discuss capitalization as a way to help readers understand the important parts of a sentence.
Explicit Instruction (5 min):Introduce key capitalization rules:
Capitalize the first word in every sentence.
Capitalize proper nouns (names, cities, countries, etc.).
Capitalize days of the week and months of the year.
Always capitalize the pronoun “I.”
Write each rule on chart paper or the board with examples:
My dog is cute. (First word)
Sarah lives in New York. (Proper noun)
I will visit on Monday. (Day of the week)
My birthday is in May. (Month)
I love ice cream. (Pronoun "I")
Guided Practice (5 min):Provide sentences with capitalization errors for students to correct as a class. Examples:
tom went to chicago in august.
yesterday was tuesday and i went to school.

Writing:
Procedural Writing/How To’s

Review procedure writing.
Guided Powerpoint

Review procedure writing.
Guided Powerpoint

AND/OR
Reflect on the cereal challenge and the Egg in a Mug How To. .
Discuss as a class:
*When would we use a Procedural/How-To Writing? (recipes, GPS navigation, experiment, etc)
*Why is it important to have detailed/specific instructions? (examples from yesterday’s lesson)
What are the important elements of a procedural text?
[Title with goal (purpose), Materials (equipment), Ingredients (with amount), Method (step by step WITH PICTURES/ILLUSTRATIONS), and Conclusion (advice or pro-tip)]

Guided Reading:
Materials: “Cinderella” (a simplified version)
Lesson:
Hook (3 mins): Ask, “Can anyone name a favorite fairy tale?” Write a few student suggestions on the board.
Teach (4 mins):
Remind students that conflicts in stories are the problems that the characters face.
Explain that conflicts can happen between characters (Character vs. Character), or between a character and something else, like nature or themselves.
Mentor Text (6 mins): Read a brief retelling of “Cinderella”. Ask:
“What is the conflict in this story?”
Guide students to identify Character vs. Character (Cinderella and her stepmother/stepsisters).
Ask, “Do you think there could be other conflicts in the story?” (Possible answers: Character vs. Self - Cinderella’s feelings of sadness, or Character vs. Society - Cinderella not being allowed to go to the ball.)

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Warm-up (2-3 min):Review yesterday’s rules. Ask, “What do we call words like Sarah, Chicago, and Tuesday?” (Proper nouns) Remind students that these are always capitalized.
Explicit Instruction (4 min):Discuss proper nouns more in-depth. Explain that proper nouns name specific people, places, and things, and these always need capital letters. Examples:
Name: Sarah
Place: Paris
Event: Qatar National Day
Guided Practice (6 min):
Play a flashcard game for the whole group. Capitalized or Not.
Have students come up to the board and choose.
Writing:
Complete the How to Make a Pizza in a Mug writing.

Guided Reading:
Materials: “The Ugly Duckling” by Hans Christian Andersen (simplified version)
Lesson:
Hook (3 mins): Say, “Yesterday we learned about conflicts in stories. What happens when the problem is solved?” Guide students to the word resolution.
Teach (4 mins):
Write “Resolution” on the board and explain that it is how the conflict is solved or fixed.
Explain that every conflict needs a resolution for the story to have a satisfying ending.
Mentor Text (6 mins): Read a simplified version of “The Ugly Duckling.” Ask:
“What was the conflict?” (The duckling is teased and rejected by others – Character vs. Character.)
“How did the duckling’s problem get solved?” (The ugly duckling grows into a beautiful swan and is accepted.)
“What was the resolution?” (The duckling finds where he truly belongs and is happy.)

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Warm-up (3 min):Review proper nouns from the previous day. Then, ask Play a quick "Proper Noun Flashcard" game. students if we capitalize the word “day” in Monday or “month” in October (no). Point out that we only capitalize the specific day or month.

Explicit Instruction (4 min):Introduce capitalization of days of the week and months:
Monday, Tuesday, October, January
Also, introduce titles like Mr., Mrs., and Dr.
Explain that both the title and the name following it are capitalized.
Guided Practice (6 min):Hand out sentences that contain errors in capitalization. Have students work in pairs to fix them.
Examples:
i went to the doctor on friday. (Correction: I went to the doctor on Friday.)
dr. smith lives on maple street. (Correction: Dr. Smith lives on Maple Street.)

Writing:
Procedural Writings aren't just recipes. It could be teaching someone how to do something. Write a how to on how to do a 4 digit addition problem.

  - 4658

Guided Reading:
Materials: “The Lion and the Mouse” (Aesop’s Fable)
Lesson:
Hook (2 mins): Say, “Now that we’ve learned about conflict and resolution, it’s time to create our own stories!”
Teach (4 mins):
Briefly review that a story needs a conflict (problem) and a resolution (solution).
Write on the board: Beginning (introduce characters), Conflict (the problem), Resolution (solving the problem).
Mentor Text (5 mins): Read “The Lion and the Mouse”. Ask:
“What is the conflict in this story?” (The lion is trapped in the net – Character vs. Nature.)
“How is the conflict resolved?” (The mouse helps the lion by chewing through the net.)
Activity (6 mins):
Ask students to brainstorm a simple story. It could be about two friends having an argument (Character vs. Character) or a kid overcoming fear (Character vs. Self).
Give them 5 minutes to outline or write their story on paper. It should include:
Who are the characters?
What is the problem (conflict)?
How is the problem solved (resolution)?

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:

Warm-up (2 min):Write several sentences on the board and ask students to correct the capitalization errors, especially focusing on the pronoun “I.” Examples:
i went to the park.
my friend and i are going to the movies.
Explicit Instruction (3 min):Explain that "I" is always capitalized, no matter where it is in the sentence. Ask students to explain why this is important (it shows self-importance or respect for oneself). (Use ones from list)

Guided Practice (7 min):
Hand out a worksheet with sentences that include errors. Students will correct the sentences individually.
Example sentences:
i saw my friend sarah yesterday.
my birthday is in february, and i love valentine’s day.
Writing:
Procedural Writings aren't just recipes. It could be teaching someone how to do something.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
MOVED FROM PREVIOUS WEEK - Grade 5 asked to go on a trip.

This week we are going to find out about transforming materials.
Today we are going to look at Physical Changes
Looking at ice, chocolate and butter - using a scale, 3 beakers, a timer and an electric skillet.

Observe.
1. Which solid will melt the fastest? Predict the results.
2. Place each solid in a separate beaker and put in the skillet. Observe what happens.
3. Note the temperature on the skillet - 100 degrees centigrade is the boiling point of water.
4. Measure the time it takes for each solid to melt.
5. Order the results in a graph.
6. What will happen next? Are the changes permanent?
7. How can you turn them back to a solid?

Students can try and change the material back to a solid, how might that work?

Examples of Physical and Chemical Changes ( Updated )
records.label_note: Day 1 & 2 can be combined - doing as much as can be done. Day 4 & 5 can be combined or as much as can be done taken from these 2 sessions Please make use of the cooking sessions during writing to cover some of the concepts being looked at here.

records.cell_text:
Investigating carbonated liquids:

Explain to students that so far for this unit there has been a focus on liquids (freezing), solids (melting and now, this session gasses - talking about reversible and irreversible changes.

Have a look at two bottles of water - Sparkling/Still have students taste and describe the bottles of water - small amounts, still water first then sparkly. Ask what they think has been used to make the difference - give them the name/word: carbonated
Today, most commercial soft drinks, seltzers and sparkling waters are created by “forced” carbonation. This is when manufacturers directly inject carbon dioxide into the beverage under high carbon dioxide pressures. A second common way to introduce carbon dioxide into a liquid is by fermentation.
Talk about examples of other carbonated drinks, e.g. 7-UP, soda water, cola, lemonade, etc. Discuss what happens when these drinks are left to ‘go flat’. Where does the gas go? (It escapes into the air.)
They are going to do an experiment:
* For some useful information on this, click on link - Bicarbonate of Soda Experiment

Mix lemon juice and baking soda. What do they notice happening?
Mix baking soda and vinegar? How is the solution changing? Can you get it back?
Then water with baking soda.
Ask them what happened when the lemon juice was added to the baking soda. The mixture starts to bubble up and fizz. Ask them, what is happening? A chemical reaction is taking place, i.e. the molecules in the lemon juice have reacted with those in the bicarbonate to form a gas. Once this gas escapes we can’t get it back, unless you collect it using special equipment – the change is irreversible, the bicarbonate of soda and lemon juice but thinking about last week, the chocolate change when melted by heating is reversible, in that it could be made solid again by cooling.

meta.assessment: What things happen that lets us know a chemical change has taken place
meta.differentiation:
Can name one of the points that indicates a chemical change
Can name 2 points that shows that a chemical change has occurred
Can name more than 2 things that show that a chemical change has taken place.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Writing up Experiments/Investigations

An experiment (fair test) is a procedure carried out to test a hypothesis, usually involving controlled and uncontrolled environments, while an investigation is an examination or inquiry that aims to uncover facts about a broad topic.

Tell the students that they are going to write up what they did yesterday using the template they will be using for the Science Fair - in padlet:
Write up for Science Fair
From slide 5 - the hypothesis

The hypothesis is an educated guess as to what will happen during your experiment. The hypothesis is often written using the words "IF" and "THEN."

A hypothesis for adding baking soda to lemon juice, vinegar, and water with a focus on the chemical reactions that occur due to the acidity of the liquids:
Hypothesis: If baking soda is added to lemon juice, vinegar, and water, then the lemon juice and vinegar will not act the same compared to water, because they are acidic and will react with the baking soda (a base), while water will not do anything (being neutral, will show little or no reaction).
They can also attempt recording the outcome in a graph.

records.cell_text: Review Types of Graphs: Tally Marks, Frequency Tables, and Line Plots

meta.objectives: Students will be able to organize a data set using tally marks and frequency tables, and create a line plot to display measurement data in fractions of a unit.
meta.standards:
CCSS.3.MD.B.3: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems. (Prerequisite standard)
CCSS.5.MD.B.2: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Use operations on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misconceptions independent practice (e.g., plotting fractions), and a challenge to find the mean, median, or mode from the line plot.

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Brainpop-Point of View
Mentor Text List for Point of View

Warm-Up:
Write two sentences on the board:
"I walked to the store."
"She walked to the store."
Ask students: "What do you notice about these sentences? How are they different?"
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Introduce the concept of Point of View.
Define First-Person Point of View: The narrator is part of the story and uses words like "I," "me," and "we."
Define Third-Person Point of View: The narrator is not part of the story and uses "he," "she," or "they."
Give examples of each type (e.g., First-Person: "I love to read." Third-Person: "She loves to read.").
Activity (5 minutes)
Hand out a short paragraph in first-person and third-person points of view. Have students highlight or underline the words that give away the point of view (e.g., "I" or "she").
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Ask students to explain the difference between first and third person to a partner.

Rotations:

Point of View G3

Grammar:
Brainpop: Punctuation
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Write three sentences on the board:
"The dog ran outside"
"Where is the dog"
"Look at that dog"
Ask students what’s missing from these sentences.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Introduce the three types of end marks:
Period (.): Used to end a statement.
Question mark (?): Used at the end of a question.
Exclamation mark (!): Used to show strong feeling or excitement.
Give examples of each:
"The dog ran outside." (statement)
"Where is the dog?" (question)
"Look at that dog!" (exclamation)
Activity (5 minutes)
Write sentences on the board without end marks, and have students decide which end mark to use:
"It is raining outside"
"Do you like ice cream"
"What a beautiful day"
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Have students share with a partner one sentence using a period, one using a question mark, and one using an exclamation mark.

Writing:
Procedural Writing/How To’s

Review procedure writing.
Guided Powerpoint

Review procedure writing.
Guided Powerpoint

AND/OR
Reflect on the cereal challenge and the Egg in a Mug How To. .
Discuss as a class:
*When would we use a Procedural/How-To Writing? (recipes, GPS navigation, experiment, etc)
*Why is it important to have detailed/specific instructions? (examples from yesterday’s lesson)
What are the important elements of a procedural text?
[Title with goal (purpose), Materials (equipment), Ingredients (with amount), Method (step by step WITH PICTURES/ILLUSTRATIONS), and Conclusion (advice or pro-tip)]

Watch the BROWNIE IN A MUG VIDEO then have students use their materials to complete the task at their desk independently. After they are finished, students will complete the SeeSaw Activity as an informal assessment.

This personal narrative helps students understand emotions and experiences told from a first-person perspective.
The Pain and the Great One by Judy Blume
The story alternates between a brother and sister’s first-person perspectives, allowing readers to see each character’s point of view.
Henry's Freedom Box by Ellen Levine
A touching first-person account of a boy escaping slavery, rich with emotional depth.
The One and Only Ivan by Katherine Applegate
Ivan, the gorilla, narrates his own story, offering a deeply reflective first-person narrative.

Third-Person Point of View
The Day the Crayons Quit by Drew Daywalt
Though each crayon shares its own story, the narrator remains in third-person, giving a fun and unique perspective.
Charlotte’s Web by E.B. White
A timeless third-person omniscient narration, where readers can see into the thoughts and feelings of multiple characters.
Click, Clack, Moo: Cows That Type by Doreen Cronin
A third-person narrative that humorously explores the communication between cows and their farmer.
Because of Winn-Dixie by Kate DiCamillo
A third-person narrative that focuses on the emotional journey of the main character, Opal.
How to Train Your Dragon by Cressida Cowell
This third-person narration follows the adventures of Hiccup as he navigates the world of dragons and Vikings.

Books with Multiple Perspectives
Voices in the Park by Anthony Browne
A story told from four different points of view, showing how each character experiences the same event differently.
Two Bad Ants by Chris Van Allsburg
Alternating between the ants’ perspective and the narrator’s point of view, offering students a dual perspective on the world.
Hey, Little Ant by Phillip Hoose and Hannah Hoose
A conversation between a boy and an ant, offering two contrasting perspectives on the same situation.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Warm-Up:
Review first-person point of view. Ask a few students to give an example of a first-person sentence.

Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Read a short passage from a first-person narrator (e.g., a short excerpt from a children’s book like Diary of a Wimpy Kid or The One and Only Ivan or any book from the mentor text list below) .
Discuss how the story feels more personal because we are inside the character’s head.
Activity (5 minutes)
Have students write a 3-4 sentence story from the first-person perspective. Start with a prompt like: "I was walking through the park when..."
After writing, students can share their stories with a partner.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Ask students: "How does it feel to write from your character's perspective? How do you know it's first person?"

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Review the definition of a period. Ask students to give examples of sentences that end with a period.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Explain that periods are used at the end of statements or commands.
Give examples: "She is my friend." "Please close the door."
Point out that every sentence must have an end mark, and statements need a period.
Activity (5 minutes)
Write several statements on the board without periods, and ask students to copy them into their notebooks, adding periods at the end:
"The cat is sleeping"
"We went to the park"
"My brother is five years old"
Ask volunteers to share their sentences with the correct punctuation.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Have students write one original statement using a period and share with the class.

Writing:
Complete the How to Make a Brownie in a Mug writing.

Guided Reading:
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Review third-person point of view. Ask students to give an example of a third-person sentence.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Read a short passage written in third-person from a book like Charlotte's Web or Harry Potter or any book from the mentor text list below. Discuss how the story feels more like an observer watching the characters.
Talk about how we can know different characters' feelings through third-person narration.
Activity (5 minutes)
Have students rewrite their Day 2 story from a third-person perspective. Start with a prompt like: "She was walking through the park when..."
After writing, students can share their stories with a partner.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Ask students: "What did you notice when you changed your story to third-person? How do you know it's third person?"

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Review the definition of a question mark. Ask students to give examples of questions they might ask their friends.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Explain that question marks are used at the end of questions.
Give examples: "Where are we going?" "Do you like pizza?"
Highlight that a question always asks something, and we signal that with a question mark.
Activity (5 minutes)
Write several questions on the board without question marks, and have students copy them, adding question marks at the end:
"What time is it"
"Can we play outside"
"Where is your backpack"
Ask volunteers to read their sentences with the correct end marks.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Have students write one original question using a question mark and share with a partner.

Writing:
Brainstorm ideas for writing their own Procedural text (What do you want to teach someone?)

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Warm-up (3 minutes)
Ask students to recall the two points of view they’ve learned about. Write "First Person" and "Third Person" on the board.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Show a simple story told from both perspectives. For example: or  any book from the mentor text list below
First Person: "I found a treasure chest."
Third Person: "She found a treasure chest."
Ask students how the two perspectives feel different.
Activity (5 minutes)
In pairs, students will take turns retelling a story they know (fairy tale, personal story, etc.) in first and third person. One student tells the story in first person, and the other retells the same story in third person.
Afterward, discuss how changing the point of view affects the story.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Ask: "Which point of view do you like writing from? Why?"

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Review the definition of an exclamation mark. Ask students to share situations where they might feel excited or surprised.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Explain that exclamation marks are used to show strong feelings like excitement, surprise, or anger.
Give examples: "Wow, look at that!" "I can't believe it!"
Point out that we don’t use exclamation marks all the time, only when we want to show strong emotion.
Activity (5 minutes)
Write several sentences on the board without end marks, and have students decide which should end with an exclamation mark:
"That’s amazing"
"I won the game"
"Watch out for that car"
Ask students to copy and correct the sentences with the correct end marks.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Have students write one original sentence using an exclamation mark and share with the class.
Writing:
Procedural Writings aren't just recipes. It could be teaching someone how to do something.
Think of a game you like to play. What are the directions of playing that game? (Use this template or same template in a seesaw activity:

records.cell_text:
The next few weeks will look at an investigation to start off the week in continuing to expose students to scientific knowledge/experiences in order to be successful in the task completed for the final task as outlined in the rubric:

Students will create a science investigation: that relates to states of matter OR the change from one state to another in order to create a new material. (copied & pasted directly from the Making New Materials Exit Rubric sheet)

Hence the investigation for this week is linked to Electrical Conductors - this is to build upon what students found out from the Thermal Conductor investigation.

Watch this video

Conductors and Insulators -Animation for kids
This covers Thermal and Electrical Conductors.

meta.standards: 3.02d Be able to explain and justify their predictions, investigations, findings, and conclusions
meta.assessment: What will happen if..?
meta.differentiation:
Can name parts of a simple circuit
Can talk about what is happening when the circuit is connected
Can talk about, predict, and explain what is happening when the circuit is connected - making predictions about what would happen when changes are made to the circuit e.g adding another battery

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Students continue with the investigation for this week linked to Electrical Conductors - this is to build upon what students found out from the Thermal Conductor investigation & knowledge gained from looking at circuits from the previous day.

Students start off with putting in batteries for a torch to work - one torch between 2. This to be followed by investigating and completing electric circuits on rotation. (see image below)
What happens when an electric current flows through a conductor?
When current passes through a conductor, like a wire, all of the electrons which were flowing randomly in various directions, all get together and begin to move in the same direction. Electrons flow from the negative - through the conductor to the positive +. They are repelled by the negative terminal and attracted by the positive terminal.
Conductors – a conductor is a material that has electrons moving freely. This means that, when electricity is passed through, the electrons move, and the electricity passes through the material.
Insulators – an electrical insulator is a material that does not have electrons flowing freely, making it almost impossible to conduct electricity.

Sheet to be completed for circuit 5 to finish off the session.
Whole class can check their prediction on what happens when there are 2 bulbs in a circuit.

meta.standards: 3.02d Be able to explain and justify their predictions, investigations, findings, and conclusions
meta.assessment: What will happen if..?
meta.differentiation:
Can name parts of a simple circuit
Can talk about what is happening when the circuit is connected
Can talk about, predict,  and explain what is happening when the circuit is connected - making predictions about what would happen when changes are made to the circuit e.g adding another battery

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Review Types of Graphs: Tally Marks, Frequency Tables, and Line Plots

meta.objectives: Students will be able to organize a data set using tally marks and frequency tables, and create a line plot to display measurement data in fractions of a unit.
meta.standards:
CCSS.3.MD.B.3: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems. (Prerequisite standard)
CCSS.5.MD.B.2: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Use operations on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misconceptions independent practice (e.g., plotting fractions), and a challenge to find the mean, median, or mode from the line plot.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Read and Form Pictographs and Bar Graphs from Data

meta.objectives: Students will be able to draw a scaled pictograph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set, and solve one- and two-step problems using the graphs.
meta.standards:
CCSS.3.MD.B.3: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems. (Prerequisite standard)
CCSS.3.MD.B.3: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems. (Prerequisite standard)
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misconceptions independent practice (e.g., importance of a key/scale), and a challenge to create a graph with a multi-unit scale (e.g., each symbol = 5).

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Read and Form Line Graphs and Circle Graphs from Data

meta.objectives: Students will be able to interpret line graphs to analyze change over time and understand how a circle graph (pie chart) represents parts of a whole (fractions/percentages).
meta.standards:
CCSS.3.MD.B.3: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems. (Prerequisite standard)
CCSS.5.G.A.2: Represent real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation. (Applies to line graphs)
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misconceptions independent practice (e.g., reading x- and y-axis), and a challenge to create a simple circle graph from data with easy fractions (e.g., 1/2, 1/4).

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Interpreting Graphs for Real World Decisions

meta.objectives: Students will be able to analyze and compare data presented in various graph formats to solve multi-step, real-world problems and make informed decisions.
meta.standards:
CCSS.3.MD.B.3: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems. (Prerequisite standard)
CCSS.5.MD.B.2: Use operations on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots. (e.g., find the total, average, or difference).
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misconceptions independent practice (e.g., comparing two data sets), and a challenge to write their own real-world problems based on a given graph.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Review/Assess

meta.objectives: Students will demonstrate mastery in reading, creating, and interpreting data from various graphs (line plots, bar graphs, line graphs).
meta.standards: Formative assessment covering 5.MD.B.2, 5.G.A.2, and prerequisite data concepts.
meta.differentiation: Assessment provides options for students (e.g., create a graph vs. interpret a graph). Challenge questions require multi-step analysis or comparing different data representations.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Brainpop-Point of View
Mentor Text List for Point of View

Warm-Up:
Write two sentences on the board:
"I walked to the store."
"She walked to the store."
Ask students: "What do you notice about these sentences? How are they different?"
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Introduce the concept of Point of View.
Define First-Person Point of View: The narrator is part of the story and uses words like "I," "me," and "we."
Define Third-Person Point of View: The narrator is not part of the story and uses "he," "she," or "they."
Give examples of each type (e.g., First-Person: "I love to read." Third-Person: "She loves to read.").
Activity (5 minutes)
Hand out a short paragraph in first-person and third-person points of view. Have students highlight or underline the words that give away the point of view (e.g., "I" or "she").
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Ask students to explain the difference between first and third person to a partner.

Rotations:

5 C POV Lesson for any book
Guided/Independent POV Lesson
G4 POV Activity with some Reading
POV Gr 4-6

 IXL

Point of View G3

Grammar:
Brainpop: Punctuation
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Write three sentences on the board:
"The dog ran outside"
"Where is the dog"
"Look at that dog"
Ask students what’s missing from these sentences.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Introduce the three types of end marks:
Period (.): Used to end a statement.
Question mark (?): Used at the end of a question.
Exclamation mark (!): Used to show strong feeling or excitement.
Give examples of each:
"The dog ran outside." (statement)
"Where is the dog?" (question)
"Look at that dog!" (exclamation)
Activity (5 minutes)
Write sentences on the board without end marks, and have students decide which end mark to use:
"It is raining outside"
"Do you like ice cream"
"What a beautiful day"
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Have students share with a partner one sentence using a period, one using a question mark, and one using an exclamation mark.

Writing:
Procedural Writing/How To’s

Review procedure writing.
Guided Powerpoint

Review procedure writing.
Guided Powerpoint

AND/OR
Reflect on the cereal challenge and the Egg in a Mug How To. .
Discuss as a class:
*When would we use a Procedural/How-To Writing? (recipes, GPS navigation, experiment, etc)
*Why is it important to have detailed/specific instructions? (examples from yesterday’s lesson)
What are the important elements of a procedural text?
[Title with goal (purpose), Materials (equipment), Ingredients (with amount), Method (step by step WITH PICTURES/ILLUSTRATIONS), and Conclusion (advice or pro-tip)]

Watch the BROWNIE IN A MUG VIDEO then have students use their materials to complete the task at their desk independently. After they are finished, students will complete the SeeSaw Activity as an informal assessment.

Review first-person point of view. Ask a few students to give an example of a first-person sentence.

Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Read a short passage from a first-person narrator (e.g., a short excerpt from a children’s book like Diary of a Wimpy Kid or The One and Only Ivan or any book from the mentor text list below) .
Discuss how the story feels more personal because we are inside the character’s head.
Activity (5 minutes)
Have students write a 3-4 sentence story from the first-person perspective. Start with a prompt like: "I was walking through the park when..."
After writing, students can share their stories with a partner.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Ask students: "How does it feel to write from your character's perspective? How do you know it's first person?"

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Review the definition of a period. Ask students to give examples of sentences that end with a period.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Explain that periods are used at the end of statements or commands.
Give examples: "She is my friend." "Please close the door."
Point out that every sentence must have an end mark, and statements need a period.
Activity (5 minutes)
Write several statements on the board without periods, and ask students to copy them into their notebooks, adding periods at the end:
"The cat is sleeping"
"We went to the park"
"My brother is five years old"
Ask volunteers to share their sentences with the correct punctuation.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Have students write one original statement using a period and share with the class.

Writing:
Complete the How to Make a Brownie in a Mug writing.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Review third-person point of view. Ask students to give an example of a third-person sentence.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Read a short passage written in third-person from a book like Charlotte's Web or Harry Potter or any book from the mentor text list below. Discuss how the story feels more like an observer watching the characters.
Talk about how we can know different characters' feelings through third-person narration.
Activity (5 minutes)
Have students rewrite their Day 2 story from a third-person perspective. Start with a prompt like: "She was walking through the park when..."
After writing, students can share their stories with a partner.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Ask students: "What did you notice when you changed your story to third-person? How do you know it's third person?"

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Review the definition of a question mark. Ask students to give examples of questions they might ask their friends.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Explain that question marks are used at the end of questions.
Give examples: "Where are we going?" "Do you like pizza?"
Highlight that a question always asks something, and we signal that with a question mark.
Activity (5 minutes)
Write several questions on the board without question marks, and have students copy them, adding question marks at the end:
"What time is it"
"Can we play outside"
"Where is your backpack"
Ask volunteers to read their sentences with the correct end marks.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Have students write one original question using a question mark and share with a partner.

Writing:
Brainstorm ideas for writing their own Procedural text (What do you want to teach someone?)

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Warm-up (3 minutes)
Ask students to recall the two points of view they’ve learned about. Write "First Person" and "Third Person" on the board.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Show a simple story told from both perspectives. For example: or  any book from the mentor text list below
First Person: "I found a treasure chest."
Third Person: "She found a treasure chest."
Ask students how the two perspectives feel different.
Activity (5 minutes)
In pairs, students will take turns retelling a story they know (fairy tale, personal story, etc.) in first and third person. One student tells the story in first person, and the other retells the same story in third person.
Afterward, discuss how changing the point of view affects the story.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Ask: "Which point of view do you like writing from? Why?"

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:
Warm-up (3 minutes)
Review the definition of an exclamation mark. Ask students to share situations where they might feel excited or surprised.
Mini-Lesson (5 minutes)
Explain that exclamation marks are used to show strong feelings like excitement, surprise, or anger.
Give examples: "Wow, look at that!" "I can't believe it!"
Point out that we don’t use exclamation marks all the time, only when we want to show strong emotion.
Activity (5 minutes)
Write several sentences on the board without end marks, and have students decide which should end with an exclamation mark:
"That’s amazing"
"I won the game"
"Watch out for that car"
Ask students to copy and correct the sentences with the correct end marks.
Wrap-up (2 minutes)
Have students write one original sentence using an exclamation mark and share with the class.
Writing:
Procedural Writings aren't just recipes. It could be teaching someone how to do something.
Think of a game you like to play. What are the directions of playing that game? (Use this template or same template in a seesaw activity:

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
The next few weeks will look at an investigation to start off the week in continuing to expose students to scientific knowledge/experiences in order to be successful in the task completed for the final task as outlined in the rubric:

Students will create a science investigation: that relates to states of matter OR the change from one state to another in order to create a new material. (copied & pasted directly from the Making New Materials Exit Rubric sheet)

Hence the investigation for this week is linked to Electrical Conductors - this is to build upon what students found out from the Thermal Conductor investigation.

Watch this video

Conductors and Insulators -Animation for kids
This covers Thermal and Electrical Conductors.

meta.standards: 3.02d Be able to explain and justify their predictions, investigations, findings, and conclusions
meta.assessment: What will happen if..?
meta.differentiation:
Can name parts of a simple circuit
Can talk about what is happening when the circuit is connected
Can talk about, predict, and explain what is happening when the circuit is connected - making predictions about what would happen when changes are made to the circuit e.g adding another battery

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Students continue with the investigation for this week linked to Electrical Conductors - this is to build upon what students found out from the Thermal Conductor investigation & knowledge gained from looking at circuits from the previous day.

Students start off with putting in batteries for a torch to work - one torch between 2. This to be followed by investigating and completing electric circuits on rotation. (see image below)
What happens when an electric current flows through a conductor?
When current passes through a conductor, like a wire, all of the electrons which were flowing randomly in various directions, all get together and begin to move in the same direction. Electrons flow from the negative - through the conductor to the positive +. They are repelled by the negative terminal and attracted by the positive terminal.
Conductors – a conductor is a material that has electrons moving freely. This means that, when electricity is passed through, the electrons move, and the electricity passes through the material.
Insulators – an electrical insulator is a material that does not have electrons flowing freely, making it almost impossible to conduct electricity.

Sheet to be completed for circuit 5 to finish off the session.
Whole class can check their prediction on what happens when there are 2 bulbs in a circuit.

meta.standards: 3.02d Be able to explain and justify their predictions, investigations, findings, and conclusions
meta.assessment: What will happen if..?
meta.differentiation:
Can name parts of a simple circuit
Can talk about what is happening when the circuit is connected
Can talk about, predict,  and explain what is happening when the circuit is connected - making predictions about what would happen when changes are made to the circuit e.g adding another battery

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 1: Numerical Patterns / Input & Output Tables

meta.objectives:
Objective: Students will be able to identify, describe, and extend numerical patterns. They will use input-output tables to find and apply a rule (e.g., add 3, multiply by 2) that connects a set of inputs to a set of outputs.
Description: This lesson focuses on algebraic reasoning through patterns. Students will analyze sequences of numbers and values in "In-Out" tables to determine the operation (add, subtract, multiply, or divide) that defines the pattern's rule. They will then use this rule to find missing values.
CCSS: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.OA.B.3: Generate two numerical patterns using two given rules. Identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms. Form ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from the two patterns, and graph the ordered pairs on a coordinate plane.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 2: Coordinate Plane (Quadrant 1) & Plotting

meta.objectives:
Objective: Students will be able to identify the x-axis, y-axis, and origin. They will accurately plot and identify ordered pairs (x, y) in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane.
Description: This lesson introduces the basic components of the coordinate plane. Students will learn that an ordered pair provides a precise location, with the first number (x-coordinate) indicating horizontal movement and the second number (y-coordinate) indicating vertical movement. All practice will be in Quadrant 1, using only positive numbers.
CCSS: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.A.1: Use a pair of perpendicular number lines, called axes, to define a coordinate system... Understand that the first number indicates how far to travel from the origin in the direction of one axis, and the second number indicates how far to travel in the direction of the second axis...
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 3: The Coordinate Plane (All 4 Quadrants)

meta.objectives:
Objective: Students will be able to identify all four quadrants and plot ordered pairs using positive and negative integers. They will apply their plotting skills to complete engaging activities like creating a mystery picture or breaking a code.
Description: This lesson expands the coordinate plane to all four quadrants, introducing negative numbers. Students will learn how the signs (+/-) of the coordinates determine the point's quadrant. They will practice plotting points in all four quadrants through structured notes and fun, reinforcing activities.
CCSS: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.6.NS.C.6.B: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 4: Distance in All 4 Quadrants

meta.objectives:
Objective: Students will be able to find the vertical or horizontal distance between two points on the coordinate plane (in all four quadrants) by counting units or using absolute value.
Description: This lesson applies knowledge of all four quadrants to a new skill: finding distance. Students will focus on finding the length between two points that share the same x-coordinate (a vertical line) or the same y-coordinate (a horizontal line). They will learn to count the units on the grid or use the absolute value of the coordinates to find the distance.
CCSS: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.6.NS.C.8: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Mixed Review and Assessment

meta.objectives:
Objective: Students will demonstrate their understanding of numerical patterns, coordinate plane components (all 4 quadrants), plotting ordered pairs, and finding horizontal/vertical distances.
Description: A summative assessment (quiz or test) that reviews all the concepts covered during the week. This allows the teacher to check for mastery and identify any areas needing re-teaching.
CCSS: 5.OA.B.3, 5.G.A.1, 6.NS.C.6.B, 6.NS.C.8
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.
