Brainpop- Figurative Language-Simile and Metaphors

Introduction (3 mins):
Start with a brief explanation of similes. A simile compares two different things using “like” or “as” (e.g., "as brave as a lion").

Video (3 mins):Show the similes video. Ask students to listen for examples they recognize.
Create Anchor chart. (Only Similes side)

Brainpop: Personal Pronouns
Pronouns (3min 12sec)
Pronoun Song

Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Write a simple sentence on the board, such as "Sarah went to the store because Sarah wanted milk."
Ask students if they notice anything repetitive and introduce the concept of pronouns to replace repetitive nouns.
Main Activity (10 minutes)
Discuss common pronouns (he, she, it, they, we, you, I) and explain that pronouns replace nouns.
Hand out small index cards with a mix of nouns and pronouns written on them. In pairs, students will find a matching pronoun for each noun card (e.g., “Sarah” and “she”).
Wrap-Up (2 minutes)
Ask a few students to share their noun and pronoun pairs.

Introduction (3 mins):Define personification, where non-human things are given human qualities (e.g., “The wind whispered through the trees”).
Song Video

Grammar: Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Write sentences on the board like “This is ___ book.” Ask students to fill in the blank with a possessive pronoun (e.g., my, your, his, her, our, their).

Main Activity (10 minutes)
Discuss possessive pronouns, explaining they show ownership.
Students will work in pairs to rewrite sentences on a worksheet, replacing possessive nouns with possessive pronouns (e.g., "That is the boy's book" to "That is his book").

Wrap-Up (2 minutes)
Review one or two worksheet sentences as a class.
Add some student examples of possessive pronouns to the anchor chart.

BrainpopJr.- Compare and Contrast
Warm-Up (3 min):
Ask students: What is a theme? Write down responses.
Share a definition: A theme is the big idea or lesson in a story.
Lesson (9 min):
Read "The Tortoise and the Hare" aloud.
Ask students: What lesson can we learn from this story? Guide them to identify the theme (e.g., perseverance, hard work).
Write the theme on the board and discuss how the events in the story support it.
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Have students share one other story or movie they know and its theme (e.g., "Cinderella" = kindness is rewarded).

AND/OR

Chart or slide with FANBOYS (For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So)
Warm-Up (3 min):
Write two short sentences on the board (e.g., I like apples. I like oranges.).
Ask: How can we combine these sentences into one?
Introduce the term "conjunctions" as words that join words, phrases, or sentences.
Lesson (9 min):
Introduce the FANBOYS acronym and explain each conjunction briefly.
Show example sentences with conjunctions, emphasizing how they join ideas (e.g., I like apples and oranges.).
Practice as a class: Underline the conjunction in sample sentences.
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Quick question: What is your favorite conjunction, and why?

(e.g., "The Tortoise and the Hare" and "The Lion and the Mouse") Text in the FOLDER
Warm-Up (3 min):
Review: Ask, What is a theme? and list examples from yesterday.
Introduce today’s goal: We will compare themes in two stories.
Lesson (9 min):
Briefly read "The Lion and the Mouse" aloud.
Identify its theme (e.g., kindness is powerful, even small help can make a difference).
Compare it with "The Tortoise and the Hare" using a Venn diagram.
Differences: Themes are perseverance vs. kindness.
Similarities: Both teach life lessons and involve animals.
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Ask: Can two different stories have the same theme? Why or why not?

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:

Warm-Up (3 min):
Review FANBOYS with a quick game: Call out a conjunction, and students give an example sentence using it.
Lesson (9 min):
Provide sentence fragments (e.g., I like to read. I like to write.).
Guide students to combine the sentences using conjunctions (e.g., I like to read and write.).
Independent or pair activity: Students complete sentences using the correct conjunction from a word bank.
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Share a few completed sentences.
Ask: What happens if we don’t use conjunctions in writing?

Warm-Up (3 min):

Read "The Boy Who Cried Wolf" and discuss its theme (e.g., honesty is important).
Contrast it with "The Ant and the Grasshopper" (theme: hard work pays off).
Guide students to write or share contrasting sentences using the sentence stems.
Sentence stems for contrasting (e.g., "The theme of ___ is different from ___ because...").

Ask: Why do you think authors write stories with different themes?

Grammar: Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Write sentences on the board like “This is ___ book.” Ask students to fill in the blank with a possessive pronoun (e.g., my, your, his, her, our, their).

Main Activity (10 minutes)
Discuss possessive pronouns, explaining they show ownership.
Students will work in pairs to rewrite sentences on a worksheet, replacing possessive nouns with possessive pronouns (e.g., "That is the boy's book" to "That is his book").

Wrap-Up (2 minutes)
Review one or two worksheet sentences as a class.
Add some student examples of possessive pronouns to the anchor chart.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text:
Lesson: What is Area/Perimeter? - An Introduction Covering Surfaces: Understanding Area

📐Unit 2: Math (Measurement and Data)

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students will be able to define area as the amount of space a two-dimensional shape covers. They will be able to measure the area of shapes using non-standard units and understand that the area is measured in square units.

Description: This introductory lesson focuses on the concept of area. Students will use non-standard square units (e.g., square tiles, paper squares) to cover different shapes and determine how many of these units are needed to cover each shape. They will compare the area of shapes and understand that area is measured in square units. This is a hands-on discovery and an introduction to the definition of the word "area".

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3.A: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text:
Lesson: Area/Perimeter of Rectangles - Rectangle Roundup: Area with Length and Width

📐Unit 2: Math (Measurement and Data)

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students will be able to understand that the area of a rectangle can be found using the formula: Area = length × width, and be able to use this formula to calculate the area of rectangles.

Description: This lesson builds off of the concept of area and will explore how to find the area of rectangles using a formula (Area=Length x Width). Students will use manipulatives (tiles, graph paper) to discover the relationship between the number of rows, columns, and the total number of squares inside a rectangle. They will practice using the formula to find the area of various rectangles, including word problems and real-world examples.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.MD.A.3: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement. (Though not about area directly, this standard reinforces the concept of measurement)

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3.A: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.

Understand how people, cultures, and nations develop over time and how historical events shape the present.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text:
Lesson: Area/Perimeter of Squares - Square Secrets: Area as a Special Rectangle

📐Unit 2: Math (Measurement and Data)

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students will be able to apply the area formula (A = l x w), to squares and understand that the area of a square can be calculated using the formula: Area = side × side or (Area = side squared).

Description: This lesson will build on the previous lesson to explore how squares are special types of rectangles and how to find their area. Students will analyze squares as a special kind of rectangle and discover the connection between the area of a rectangle and the area of a square. Students will practice finding the area of a variety of squares, and will discover how using the formula Area = Side x Side is the most efficient way to find the area of a square.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.MD.A.3: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.3: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.

Understand how people, cultures, and nations develop over time and how historical events shape the present.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text:
Lesson: Area Explorations/Perimeter - Area Adventures: Real-World Applications

📐Unit 2: Math (Measurement and Data)

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students will be able to apply their understanding of area formulas for squares and rectangles to solve various real-world problems.

Description: In this lesson, students will be given real-world scenarios that will require using their knowledge of the area. Students will explore a variety of situations (e.g., calculating the amount of carpet needed for a room, finding the area of a garden plot, or finding the area of materials needed for a building project) and learn to choose the appropriate shapes and area formula to solve the word problems. Students will also begin to learn how to create their own word problems, which will be shared with the class.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.MD.A.3: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.B.4: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.B.4.B: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.5: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume. (While this standard is about volume, it reinforces concepts of unit calculation that are necessary for area)

Understand how people, cultures, and nations develop over time and how historical events shape the present.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

Guided Reading:

BrainpopJr.- Compare and Contrast
Warm-Up (3 min):
Ask students: What is a theme? Write down responses.
Share a definition: A theme is the big idea or lesson in a story.
Lesson (9 min):
Read "The Tortoise and the Hare" aloud.
Ask students: What lesson can we learn from this story? Guide them to identify the theme (e.g., perseverance, hard work).
Write the theme on the board and discuss how the events in the story support it.
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Have students share one other story or movie they know and its theme (e.g., "Cinderella" = kindness is rewarded).

Chart or slide with FANBOYS (For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So)
Warm-Up (3 min):
Write two short sentences on the board (e.g., I like apples. I like oranges.).
Ask: How can we combine these sentences into one?
Introduce the term "conjunctions" as words that join words, phrases, or sentences.
Lesson (9 min):
Introduce the FANBOYS acronym and explain each conjunction briefly.
Show example sentences with conjunctions, emphasizing how they join ideas (e.g., I like apples and oranges.).
Practice as a class: Underline the conjunction in sample sentences.
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Quick question: What is your favorite conjunction, and why?

Guided Reading:

(e.g., "The Tortoise and the Hare" and "The Lion and the Mouse") Text in the FOLDER
Warm-Up (3 min):
Review: Ask, What is a theme? and list examples from yesterday.
Introduce today’s goal: We will compare themes in two stories.
Lesson (9 min):
Briefly read "The Lion and the Mouse" aloud.
Identify its theme (e.g., kindness is powerful, even small help can make a difference).
Compare it with "The Tortoise and the Hare" using a Venn diagram.
Differences: Themes are perseverance vs. kindness.
Similarities: Both teach life lessons and involve animals.
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Ask: Can two different stories have the same theme? Why or why not?

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar:

Warm-Up (3 min):
Review FANBOYS with a quick game: Call out a conjunction, and students give an example sentence using it.
Lesson (9 min):
Provide sentence fragments (e.g., I like to read. I like to write.).
Guide students to combine the sentences using conjunctions (e.g., I like to read and write.).
Independent or pair activity: Students complete sentences using the correct conjunction from a word bank.
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Share a few completed sentences.
Ask: What happens if we don’t use conjunctions in writing?

Writing:
Brainstorming: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h5HM8GjQwP8&t=4s

Activities:
Watch How to Choose a Research Topic.
Teacher models brainstorming using a mind map.
Students create their own mind maps for their chosen topics.

Guided Reading:

(e.g., "The Boy Who Cried Wolf" and "The Ant and the Grasshopper") or Text in the FOLDER
Warm-Up (3 min):
Review Venn diagram from Day 2. Ask: What do we mean when we contrast something?
Lesson (9 min):
Read "The Boy Who Cried Wolf" and discuss its theme (e.g., honesty is important).
Contrast it with "The Ant and the Grasshopper" (theme: hard work pays off).
Guide students to write or share contrasting sentences using the sentence stems.
Sentence stems for contrasting (e.g., "The theme of ___ is different from ___ because...").
Wrap-Up (3 min):
Ask: Why do you think authors write stories with different themes?

Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar: Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Write sentences on the board like “This is ___ book.” Ask students to fill in the blank with a possessive pronoun (e.g., my, your, his, her, our, their).

Main Activity (10 minutes)
Discuss possessive pronouns, explaining they show ownership.
Students will work in pairs to rewrite sentences on a worksheet, replacing possessive nouns with possessive pronouns (e.g., "That is the boy's book" to "That is his book").

Wrap-Up (2 minutes)
Review one or two worksheet sentences as a class.
Add some student examples of possessive pronouns to the anchor chart.

Writing:
Watch How to Find Reliable Sources for Research.
Class activity: Sort examples of sources into "reliable" and "unreliable" categories.
Discuss what makes a source trustworthy.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Presentation by Ms. Noor from TLC Team - Sharing information about Palestine to be compared and contrasted to Qatar.

Collage Making (25 mins):
Provide materials like tissue paper, magazines, wool, and sandpaper.
Groups work collaboratively to create their collages. For Qatar, include the desert, ocean, capital city, and key landmarks. For other countries, include elements like mountains, rivers, or iconic buildings.
Adding Details (10 mins):
Use small drawings or pictures to add architectural features, people, or native species.
Groups create symbols or captions to explain features in their collage.
Gallery Walk (10 mins):
Groups walk around the classroom to view each other's progress, sharing positive feedback.
records.label_note: The Encarta English Dictionary defines ‘country’ as: 1. separate nation – nation or state that is politically independent 2. homeland – nation or state where somebody was born or is a citizen 3. geographically distinct area – large area of land regarded as distinct from other areas, e.g. because of its natural boundaries or because it is inhabited by a specific group of people

meta.standards: To use creative materials to build a collage representing Qatar and other countries, highlighting key features.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Final Touches (10 mins):
(Remind them about the work just done on their Science Projects)
Groups finalize their collages, adding captions or flags if needed.
Presentations (25 mins):
Each group presents their collage, explaining the features they included and why they are important.
For Qatar, students might highlight the desert terrain, the Pearl-Qatar, and cultural traditions like camel racing.
Discussion: Similarities and Differences (10 mins):
As a class, discuss what they noticed about the similarities and differences between Qatar and the other countries.
Ask: "What surprised you? What do you appreciate more about Qatar after this activity?"
Classroom Display:
Arrange the collages, and accompanying captions into a display corner for parents and visitors.
records.label_note: The Encarta English Dictionary defines ‘country’ as: 1. separate nation – nation or state that is politically independent 2. homeland – nation or state where somebody was born or is a citizen 3. geographically distinct area – large area of land regarded as distinct from other areas, e.g. because of its natural boundaries or because it is inhabited by a specific group of people

meta.standards: To present their country collages, reflecting on what they’ve learned about Qatar and other countries.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Extension Ideas:
Use technology to create a digital presentation or video about Qatar and the other countries.

meta.standards:
Students will identify and create two-dimensional shapes based on given attributes including number of sides and vertices.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.4.G.A.2: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.3: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category. For example, all rectangles have four right angles and squares are rectangles, so all squares have four right angles.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

YouTube.- 5 W’s or Question Words
Warm-Up (3 min) & Lesson (9 min):
(Introduction [10 minutes])
Begin with the question: "Where are you in this big wide world?" Home Country
Show a globe or a large world map. Ask, “Can you find our host country?” (UAE)
Invite a student to locate the host country.
Follow up with, “Who are our neighbors? Which other countries do we have connections with?”
Discuss how people, places, and connections make the world smaller.
Discuss the 5 W questions:
Who lives in the host/home countries?
What do we know about these places?
Where are they located?
When did you or your family travel between these places?
Why might someone travel or move between these countries?
Model comparing and contrasting using a sentence frame:
“The host country is [size/geographic feature], while my home country is [size/geographic feature].”
Activity (15 min):
Students write a short paragraph comparing the host country and their home country using these questions.

Wrap-Up (3 min):
Get students share some ideas from their writing

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

(e.g., "The Boy Who Cried Wolf" and "The Ant and the Grasshopper") or Text in the FOLDER
Warm-Up (3 min) & Lesson (9 min):
Introduction to Researching Skills (15 minutes)
Discuss why research is important when learning about different places.
Teach strategies for finding credible information:
Introduce criteria for evaluating credible sources:
Author: Who wrote it? Are they an expert?
Publication Date: Is the information current?
Bias: Does the source have a particular opinion or agenda?
Purpose: Is the source informative, persuasive, or entertainment-focused?
Guided Research Practice (30 minutes)
Provide a list of recommended kid-friendly resources (print and online).
Assign pairs or small groups to research one of the following:
A geographic feature of the host/home country.
A cultural tradition unique to one of the countries.
Historical connections between the two countries.

Wrap-Up (3 min): Reflect on the research process:
“What was easy or challenging about finding information?”
“How can we make sure our information is trustworthy?”
Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar: Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Write sentences on the board like “This is ___ book.” Ask students to fill in the blank with a possessive pronoun (e.g., my, your, his, her, our, their).

Main Activity (10 minutes)
Discuss possessive pronouns, explaining they show ownership.
Students will work in pairs to rewrite sentences on a worksheet, replacing possessive nouns with possessive pronouns (e.g., "That is the boy's book" to "That is his book").

Wrap-Up (2 minutes)
Review one or two worksheet sentences as a class.
Add some student examples of possessive pronouns to the anchor chart.

Writing:
Watch How to Find Reliable Sources for Research.
Class activity: Sort examples of sources into "reliable" and "unreliable" categories.
Discuss what makes a source trustworthy.

Spelling Padlet:
Padlet for Spelling

Phonics:
Briefly explain that "r" changes the sound of the vowel it follows (e.g., "ar" in "car," "er" in "her").
Show examples on the board.
Read words with r-controlled vowels aloud (e.g., "star," "bird," "fork") and have students repeat after you.
Sort words by r-controlled vowel sounds (e.g., ar, er, or) and practice pronunciation.
Get students to give examples of control-r words that they may have come across of heard of from their personal experiences.

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

YouTube.- 5 W’s or Question Words
Warm-Up (3 min) & Lesson (9 min):
(Introduction [10 minutes])
Begin with the question: "Where are you in this big wide world?" Home Country
Show a globe or a large world map. Ask, “Can you find our host country?” (UAE)
Invite a student to locate the host country.
Follow up with, “Who are our neighbors? Which other countries do we have connections with?”
Discuss how people, places, and connections make the world smaller.
Discuss the 5 W questions:
Who lives in the host/home countries?
What do we know about these places?
Where are they located?
When did you or your family travel between these places?
Why might someone travel or move between these countries?
Model comparing and contrasting using a sentence frame:
“The host country is [size/geographic feature], while my home country is [size/geographic feature].”
Activity (15 min):
Students write a short paragraph comparing the host country and their home country using these questions.

Wrap-Up (3 min):
Get students share some ideas from their writing

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

(e.g., "The Boy Who Cried Wolf" and "The Ant and the Grasshopper") or Text in the FOLDER
Warm-Up (3 min) & Lesson (9 min):
Introduction to Researching Skills (15 minutes)
Discuss why research is important when learning about different places.
Teach strategies for finding credible information:
Introduce criteria for evaluating credible sources:
Author: Who wrote it? Are they an expert?
Publication Date: Is the information current?
Bias: Does the source have a particular opinion or agenda?
Purpose: Is the source informative, persuasive, or entertainment-focused?
Guided Research Practice (30 minutes)
Provide a list of recommended kid-friendly resources (print and online).
Assign pairs or small groups to research one of the following:
A geographic feature of the host/home country.
A cultural tradition unique to one of the countries.
Historical connections between the two countries.

Wrap-Up (3 min): Reflect on the research process:
“What was easy or challenging about finding information?”
“How can we make sure our information is trustworthy?”
Rotations:
See list from Sunday or the differentiation section below.

Grammar: Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Write sentences on the board like “This is ___ book.” Ask students to fill in the blank with a possessive pronoun (e.g., my, your, his, her, our, their).

Main Activity (10 minutes)
Discuss possessive pronouns, explaining they show ownership.
Students will work in pairs to rewrite sentences on a worksheet, replacing possessive nouns with possessive pronouns (e.g., "That is the boy's book" to "That is his book").

Wrap-Up (2 minutes)
Review one or two worksheet sentences as a class.
Add some student examples of possessive pronouns to the anchor chart.

Writing:
Watch How to Find Reliable Sources for Research.
Class activity: Sort examples of sources into "reliable" and "unreliable" categories.
Discuss what makes a source trustworthy.

Spelling:
No New Words

Phonics:
Briefly explain that "r" changes the sound of the vowel it follows (e.g., "ar" in "car," "er" in "her").
Show examples on the board.
Read words with r-controlled vowels aloud (e.g., "star," "bird," "fork") and have students repeat after you.
Sort words by r-controlled vowel sounds (e.g., ar, er, or) and practice pronunciation.
Get students to give examples of control-r words that they may have come across of heard of from their personal experiences.
Extra resources for week:
Digraph write the room

Cut out and fold dice, roll and write digraph game

Underline the digraph SeeSaw

Decodable text hub

Word search

Th and wh crossword

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Lesson: What are 3D Shapes? - An Introduction

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students will be able to identify and describe real-world objects that are three-dimensional (3D), and explain that 3D shapes have faces, edges, and vertices.

Description: In this lesson, we'll become shape detectives! We will explore the world around us to find objects that are not flat, but are instead 3D. We will learn that 3D shapes take up space and have faces, edges, and vertices. We'll sort everyday objects by their 3D shapes to begin to understand the basic attributes of three-dimensional figures.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.3: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Lesson: Prisms and Pyramids - Building and Comparing

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students will be able to identify and describe the characteristics of prisms and pyramids, including their base shapes and the shape of their faces, and differentiate between these two types of 3D shapes.

Description: Get ready to build! In this lesson, we will build prisms and pyramids using materials like straws and clay, or even nets. We will learn the difference between these two types of shapes, focusing on how their bases and faces are different. We'll identify these shapes in real-world objects.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.3: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.4: Classify two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Lesson: Rolling Shapes: Cylinders, Cones, and Spheres!

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students will be able to describe the attributes of cylinders, cones, and spheres, and will be able to differentiate between these shapes and those of prisms and pyramids.

Description: Let's roll into some new shapes! We will explore cylinders, cones, and spheres in this lesson. We will investigate how these shapes are similar and different from prisms and pyramids. We'll discover how they roll, and we will find examples of these shapes in our world.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.3: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
* CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.4: Classify two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Lesson 4: 3D "Architects at Work: Designing with 3D Shapes!"

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students will be able to combine various 3D shapes to create new structures and will accurately use math vocabulary to describe the attributes of the shapes and the structures they build.

Description: It's time to be architects! Using our knowledge about 3D shapes, we'll design buildings and structures. We will combine different 3D shapes to create structures like towers, pyramids, and houses. We will draw out our designs, discuss how to build them, and use math vocabulary to explain the different shapes we used in our designs.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.3: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.5: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.G.B.4: Classify two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes partner practice, common misperceptions independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

Trigraphs and Digraphs

Review Digraphs and Watch this video: Trigraphs

OR

1. Introduction
Begin with a brief explanation of what digraphs are. Provide examples such as "ch" in "chair," "sh" in "shoe," and "th" in "this."
Show an anchor chart that illustrates common digraphs. See Above and the H digraphs from last week.
2. Video
Watch a YouTube video that explains Trigraphs..
3. Guided Practice
Distribute a worksheet that includes a list of words. Have students underline the digraphs in each word. You can find free worksheets at Education.com or These trigraph worksheets.
4. Independent Practice
Ask students to write five sentences using at least three words that contain digraphs./trigraphs
5. Closing (5 minutes)
Review what digraphs are and ask students to share one new word they learned.

RI.5.9: Integrate information from several texts on the same topic to write or speak about the subject knowledgeably.
Extended Standards (EE)
EE.RI.5.3: Recognize how authors use details to support claims in texts.
EE.RI.5.6: Describe how events or ideas are related in a text.
EE.RI.5.9: Gather information from multiple texts on a topic to increase understanding.
Language Standards
CC: L.5.2.D: Use commas to separate items in a series.
EE.L.5.2.D: Use commas in a list of items.
Writing Standards
RI.5.1: Quote accurately from a text when explaining what it says explicitly and when drawing inferences.
W.5.2b: Develop the topic with facts, definitions, concrete details, quotations, or other information and examples related to the topic.
W.5.9: Draw evidence from literary or informational texts to support analysis, reflection, and research.
Extended Writing Standards (EE)
EE.RI.5.1: Identify specific parts of the text that provide information.
EE.RI.5.2: Summarize the main idea of a text with support.
meta.assessment: Informal- SeeSaw Assignment

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading/Grammar/Writing::
Activities:
Students choose one fun topic related to Qatar (e.g., camel racing, the 2022 World Cup, or dhow boats).
Research using resources like National Geographic Kids or Duckster or teacher-provided texts.
Create a list of facts, organized by subtopic, using commas for lists and dates as needed. (Teacher can assign this mode or students can choose or the teacher can request that all modes of notetaking be included. (list, commas, dates, note taking, graphic organizers)

Fun Qatar Facts to Share:
Qatar is the richest country per capita in the world.
The country's flag, called Al Adaam, is maroon and white.
Qatar’s National Day, December 18, marks the unification of the country.
The Museum of Islamic Art in Doha is designed by architect I.M. Pei, who also designed the Louvre Pyramid.

Grammar:
Brainpop Video- REVIEW Contractions
Activities:
Watch Apostrophes in Contractions for Kids.
Discuss how apostrophes replace letters in contractions (e.g., don’t = do not).
Qatar Fact: Highlight contractions in a sentence like "Qatar’s National Day isn’t on January 1; it’s on December 18."

Sentences
Qatar's a small country in the Middle East.
The capital's Doha, and it's very modern.
Many people love visiting Qatar's beaches.
The weather's usually hot and sunny.
They’ve got a lot of parks for families.
The food's delicious and full of spices.
People aren’t friendly and don’t like to share.
Qatar’s known for its amazing skyscrapers.
The museum hasn’t got interesting exhibits.
Kids enjoy playing in the warm sand.
(Kids don’t go outside much when it’s very hot)
Answer Key with Contractions
Qatar is a small country in the Middle East.
The capital is Doha, and it is very modern.
Many people love visiting Qatar's beaches.
The weather is usually hot and sunny.
They have got a lot of parks for families.
The food is delicious and full of spices.
People are friendly and like to share their culture.
Qatar is known for its amazing skyscrapers.
The museums have got interesting exhibits.
Kids enjoy playing in the warm sand.
Kids do not go outside much when it is very hot.

Spelling WORDS:
cleaner
cleanest
bigger
biggest
earlier
earliest
quicker
quickest
busier
busiest
rougher
roughest
heavier
heaviest
happier
happiest
lazier
laziest

Spelling Padlet:
Padlet for Spelling

Qatar is known for its beautiful parks and warm weather.
The Emir of Qatar works to improve the country’s development.
Many tourists explore the rich culture and history of Qatar.
The desert in Qatar is home to unique flora and fauna.
Qatar’s markets offer a variety of fresh fruits and spices.
The skyline of Doha features impressive skyscrapers and buildings.
Children in Qatar enjoy playing sports after school.
The Qatari cuisine includes flavorful dishes like biryani and kebabs.
Festivals in Qatar bring together families and friends to celebrate.
The coral reefs near Qatar are perfect for snorkeling and diving.

Key Vocabulary
then, now, past, present, change

Intro (10mins): Go through PPT on places in Qatar
Amazing Places in Qatar.pptx
Ask:
“Is this then or now?”
Invite students to answer. Encourage choral responses for EAL support.
Activity: (20 mins) Explain: Then = long ago; Now = today
Create a T-chart on board for later, after working on the word - pic activity sheet, on which students will state which places are old or new or in between.
Qatar Word - Pic Activity.pdf - linked to Literacy

Wrap-up: (10 mins): Finish with drawing their favorite place on the activity sheet with the name of place and recording why?

meta.standards: Explain how communities change over time (continuity & change).
meta.objectives: Tell the difference between the past and today - Qatar.
meta.assessment: Draw a place and state whether it is from then or now (old or new)
meta.differentiation:
🟢 Draw + label place
🔵 Draw + Write one sentence about the place
🟣Draw +  Write sentence with because… (about the place)

records.subject: math
records.cell_text:
Understanding Volume

Teaching Resource: https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/breakout-link/PkpnqAB5YA9JvD0B-VyDovAEdpxxOvZQL

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: I can explain that volume is how much space a 3D object takes up, and I know we measure it using cubes.

Short Description: This introductory lesson focuses on conceptual understanding of volume. Students will explore the differences between area and volume, and will use hands-on activities and manipulatives (like blocks and unit cubes) to begin to define volume and understand that volume is measured in cubic units.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3.A: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
meta.differentiation:
Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

Provide physical manipulatives (unit cubes)
Start with smaller numbers and simpler shapes
Use step-by-step visual guides
Offer partially completed problems

records.subject: math
records.cell_text:
Volume of Rectangular Prisms

Teaching Resources:
https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/breakout-link/gx604ee8DPl34YGo-VyDovAEdpxxOvZQL

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: I can find the volume of a rectangular prism by counting how many little cubes it takes to fill it up.

Short Description: This lesson focuses on building and visualizing rectangular prisms using unit cubes. Students will construct various rectangular prisms and determine their volume by counting the number of unit cubes they contain. They will begin to discover and describe the patterns found in rectangular prisms as they build and count cubes.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.4: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and improvised units.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3.A: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
meta.differentiation:
Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

Provide physical manipulatives (unit cubes)
Start with smaller numbers and simpler shapes
Use step-by-step visual guides
Offer partially completed problems

records.subject: math
records.cell_text:
Relating Volume to Area

Teaching Resources:
https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/breakout-link/DKrG4nj8007d41y3-VyDovAEdpxxOvZQL

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: Students can see how the area of the base of a prism helps them figure out its volume, and students can use that to find the total volume.

Description: This lesson focuses on connecting prior knowledge of area to the concept of volume. Students will explore how rectangular prisms are composed of layers of unit cubes and how the area of the base layer can be used to find the total volume. They will make connections between the area of a base of a rectangular prism and the total volume of the prism by exploring these layers, and will make connections between volume and area using manipulatives and real world examples.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.4: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and improvised units.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.5: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.
meta.differentiation:
Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

Provide physical manipulatives (unit cubes)
Start with smaller numbers and simpler shapes
Use step-by-step visual guides
Offer partially completed problems

records.subject: math
records.cell_text:
Using Volume Formula

Teaching Resources:
https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/breakout-link/JZaQvQZRx6JK4nYK-VyDovAEdpxxOvZQL

meta.standards:
Learning Objective: I can use a formula to find volume: Volume = Length x Width x Height.

Description: This lesson introduces the standard formula for finding the volume of a rectangular prism. Students will connect what they have learned about layers, bases, and unit cubes to the formula. They will practice applying the formula to solve for the volume of various rectangular prisms, including those with fractional side lengths and real-world situations.

Common Core Standards:

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.5: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.5.A: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.MD.C.5.B: Apply the formulas V = l × w × h and V = b × h for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole-number edge lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems.

CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.B.4: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
meta.differentiation:
Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

Provide physical manipulatives (unit cubes)
Start with smaller numbers and simpler shapes
Use step-by-step visual guides
Offer partially completed problems

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Key vocabulary
evidence, picture, change

Then vs Now (Compare & Explain)
Intro (10mins): Reading pictures as evidence
 It means using pictures to learn about the past
 A picture can give us clues about how people lived
Those clues are called evidence
Students are learning to:
Look carefully at a picture notice details
use those details to explain then vs now.
Tell students:
When we “read pictures as evidence,” we:
*look closely at what we see
*think about what the picture shows us
*explain how the picture helps us understand life in the past or present
Pictures help us answer questions like:
*What was life like before oil?
*How did life change after oil?
*What is different now?
Look at the first slide from previous day to demonstrate the skill of reading pictures -
BEFORE & AFTER COMPARISON (QATAR).pptx
Activity: (20 mins)
Continue looking at the other slides -
Say:
“We are going to continue to read pictures.
We are not reading words — we are reading pictures.”
(Show slide 2: old Qatar OR modern Qatar)
“First, we look carefully.
No talking. Just look.”
(pause 10 seconds)
“Now, what do I see?”
Accept answers:
“I see tents.”
“I see buildings.”
Write answers on the board.
“Good.
Slide 3: What does this picture tell us?”
Guide students:
“Is this before oil or after oil?”
Say clearly:
“This picture is evidence.”
Point to the picture.
“Evidence means proof.
The picture gives us proof.”
Model one full sentence:
“This picture is evidence because I see a car. That means life after oil.”
Repeat with another picture:
“This picture is evidence because I see tall buildings. That means life is after oil.”
End with:
“Pictures help us learn about the past and the present.”
Wrap-up: (10 mins): Get students to complete the Reading Pictures sheet
Reading Pictures - Evidence.pdf

meta.standards: Analyze how evidence shows change over time.
meta.objectives: Explain what pictures show about change in Qatar.
meta.assessment: Answer assessment style questions
meta.differentiation:
🟢 Circle correct answer
🔵 Explain choice
🟣 Use evidence sentence

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Key Vocabulary
source, primary, secondary

Primary and Secondary Sources
Intro (10mins):
Show an old photograph (or drawing).
Tell students:
“This is a primary source.”
“Primary means from the past.”
Explain with examples:
old photo.; old newspaper; object from long ago.

You can use gesture: Point behind you when you say past
Show an information book or point to yourself.
“This is a secondary source.”
“Secondary means it tells us about the past.”
Explain: books, teachers, videos explaining the past, websites
You can gesture: Point to mouth (telling)
Activity: (20 mins)
Anchor Chart - Draw on board:

Go over the intro and invite students to give examples and discuss.
Wrap-up: (10 mins):
Sorting Activity:
Word cards:
Old photo, newspaper, information book, teacher
Two labels:
PRIMARY
SECONDARY

meta.standards: Identify and classify historical sources
meta.objectives: Tell the difference between primary and secondary sources.
meta.assessment: Putting sources in Primary and Secondary categories
meta.differentiation:
🟢✔ pointing
🔵 ✔ short explanations
🟣 ✔ full sentence explanations

Volume Project: Design a City
Lesson Resources: https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/breakout-link/3jXMqplEYa0ZqAzx-ldENvGQnKJp1zjo5

https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/breakout-link/ke9KqzbKLaP9qYQG-ldENvGQnKJp1zjo5

First, review any previous week's lessons on volume including understanding volume, volume of rectangular prisms, and using volume formula.
Second, Complete project: Design a City with Volume, Area, and Perimeter
 Project Steps
Review concepts using IXL
Review directions and rubric
Cut grid paper, buildings, and roads
Construct 3D buildings and city layout
Calculate volume, area, and perimeter
Decorate with details (cars, trees, etc.)
Record measurements in table
Assemble on construction paper

meta.objectives:
Central Learning Outcome
Students will apply their understanding of volume, area, and perimeter to design a functional city layout while meeting specific size requirements and rules.

Supporting Learning Outcomes
Students will demonstrate mastery of volume calculations for rectangular prisms.
Students will apply area and perimeter concepts to create a functional city layout.
Students will develop spatial reasoning and problem-solving skills through hands-on design.
Students will practice following directions and meeting specific criteria for accuracy and creativity.
 Common Core Standards
5.MD.C.3: Understand volume as an attribute of solid figures.
5.MD.C.5: Relate volume to multiplication and addition.
4.MD.A.3: Apply area and perimeter formulas for rectangles.
meta.differentiation:
Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

Provide physical manipulatives (unit cubes)
Start with smaller numbers and simpler shapes
Use step-by-step visual guides
Offer partially completed problems

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Display posters with text features in class for reference.

Introduction to Text Features & Headings

 Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Brainstorm: Ask students: "What helps you know what to expect when you read a book with information in it?" Call on a few for answers, writing key terms on the board. Guide students to items beyond just the core story.

Mini-Lesson: What are Text Features? (10 minutes)
Introduce the Big Idea: "Today, we're starting a journey to become nonfiction navigators! We will be learning about special tools, called text features, that are included in non-fiction books."
Show a print of the title page of the flipbook to gain the students attention.
Text Feature #1: Headings: Refer to page 1 of the flip book.
Ask: “What do you notice about the way the information is organized? How do the words in dark help readers?”
Explain: "Headings are like titles for sections of a book. They tell us what that section will be about."
Model: Show examples of headings in nonfiction books and explain their purpose.

Guided Practice (5 minutes)
Scavenger Hunt Prep: Pass out "Non-Fiction Text Feature Scavenger Hunt" page 1. Model the process of noting a page number and writing the purpose of the feature for one heading in your sample text.
Search: Students, at their table groups, search for one heading in the text at their table and write the page number and the purpose of the heading on their paper. Share their findings in a quick share within their group.

 Closing (2 minutes)
Quick review: "What was the text feature we learned about today?" (Headings)
"Why are they helpful to authors?"

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Captions and Labels
Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Review: "Yesterday, we learned about headings. Who can remind me of what headings tell us in a text?"

Call on students to discuss and recap the purpose of headings. (Formative Assessment)

Mini-Lesson: Captions and Labels (10 minutes)

Refer to page 1 of the flipbook.
Text Feature #2: Captions Ask: "What do you notice about the words next to the pictures?"
Explain: "Captions are the words next to or under a picture, giving us a short explanation of what the picture is about."
Text Feature #3: Labels: Ask: “What do you notice about the words on the diagrams?”
Explain: “Labels point to and name parts of an object”
Model: Show and discuss examples of captions and labels in different nonfiction books.

Guided Practice (5 minutes)
Scavenger Hunt: Students, at their tables, use the same texts as yesterday and now find at least one caption and one label. They record their findings and their purpose on their scavenger hunt sheet.
Quick Share: Have students show and share with their table groups.

Closing (2 minutes)
"Who can remind me what we learned about today?" (Captions and Labels)
“What is different about the purpose of a caption and a label?”

Reading: Text Features Language if Needed:
Table of Contents- Is it found in fiction books? Do we have to read nonfiction in order?
Keywords/Bold Words-How do we identify this in the text? What are the colors keywords can be? Where in a nonfiction text can a bold word or keyword be found?
Glossary-What’s the purpose of a glossary? How are words written in a glossary?
Photographs/Illustrations-Don’t all books have pictures? What’s the difference between pictures in a nonfiction book vs fiction?
Captions-What can captions tell me? Would I necessarily need to read the entire page to understand a photo?
Labels-What's the difference between captions and labels?
Maps-What are some reasons we might find a map in a nonfiction book?
Headings- What is the purpose of a heading? Can it tell what that section is about?
Subheadings- What is a subheading? How does the subheading usually relate to the heading?
Index-How is the index section organized? Why do you think it’s organized in this way? What do the numbers represent? What’s the difference between a glossary and an index?

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Bold Print and Table of Contents

Warm-Up (3 minutes)
Quick Review: Ask students, "What are the text features we’ve discussed so far, and what is the purpose of each?"
Have a few students share, and record student answers on the board.

 Mini-Lesson: Bold Print and Table of Contents (10 minutes)
Text Feature #4: Bold Print: Show examples of bold print from the uploaded material (flip book resource) and a nonfiction book. Ask: "What do you notice about the words in dark, thicker print?" (Bold words point to important words.) Explain: "Bold print helps to make important words and phrases stand out."
Text Feature #5: Table of Contents: Show a variety of tables of contents from different non-fiction texts. Ask: "Where would you look in a book to see what information it contains?" Explain: "The Table of Contents is at the beginning of a book, and it shows you what is in the book and what page it’s on."
Video Clip (2 minutes): Short YouTube video that explains different text features. Example: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1yT55p3W424 (Note: This particular video may need to be adjusted based on your students.) This will provide another avenue for instruction.

 Guided Practice (5 minutes)
Index Cards: Have students use their books to look for examples of bold print and a table of contents. Have them write their examples on an index card. They write the page number and purpose of each on an index card.
Class Share: Have a few students share their findings.

Closing (2 minutes)
Briefly review: "Today, we explored two more important text features. How do bold print and a table of contents help readers?"

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading::

Applying Our Knowledge - Scavenger Hunt

Warm-Up (2 minutes)
Quick review: “Let’s name some of the text features we’ve learned so far.”
Call on students to remind the class about previously discussed text features.

 Mini-Lesson: Putting it all Together (5 minutes)
Connect the features: Students at the tables get 1 minute to look at any of the books and identify any text feature.
Lead a quick discussion: “So, we’ve learned about a lot of text features. How do you think they work together to help readers?” Steer conversation toward how text features help organize and provide clarity in a text.

Independent Practice: Extended Scavenger Hunt (10 minutes)
Pass out: Pass out the "Non-Fiction Text Feature Scavenger Hunt page 2" where students have to write the names of 4 text features and find them in their text. Students should use the knowledge gained during previous days, and search at their table group for examples of the text features in the books at their table. Have students describe what is on the page, identify the text feature, write the page number and purpose.
Provide one sample from one student’s book.

Closing (3 minutes)
Class Discussion: What have you learned this week about text features?
“Now when you see them in books you know their job and how to use them as you read.”

See Sunday’s Options

Guided Reading:

Brainpop: Author's Purpose
Warm-up (5 min)
Ask students: “Why do authors write books? Can you name different reasons?”
Show the PIE anchor chart and explain:
Persuade: convince you of something
Inform: give you facts
Entertain: tell a story for fun
Watch the short video (2 minutes) for a fun explanation.
Practice (7 min)
Read a short passage from The Day the Crayons Quit.
Ask: “What do you think the author’s purpose is? Why?”
Discuss the text’s clues (funny tone, talking crayons, playful story = entertain).
Closing (3 min)
Quick Thumbs Up/Down Game: Teacher says a sentence, and students show thumbs up/down if it persuades, informs, or entertains.
Example:
“Crayons should be used equally, not just blue all the time!” (Persuade)
“Crayons are made from wax and come in many colors.” (Inform)
“The crayons went on strike and wrote letters to their owner!” (Entertain)

Show a short YouTube video explaining quotation marks. Example:  Brainpop Dialogue

AND/OR

Practice (10 min) – Read & Highlight
Read "The Missing Lunchbox" aloud.
Give each student a highlighter. Have them find and highlight all the dialogue in the story.
Discuss: “What do you notice about how the words are written when characters speak?”
Closing (2 min) – Exit Ticket

Writing:
Writing DAY 1 - Opinion Writing
Writing will be linked to IPC this week to strengthen students understanding of the unit
Guiding Questions Throughout:
What do you think?
Why do you think that way?
How can your ideas help others?
What is an Opinion?
Start with a simple definition: "An opinion is what YOU think or feel about something."
Contrast opinions with facts ("The sky is blue" vs. "Blue is the best color.")
Use e.g.: "My favorite lunch is pizza." "I think recess is the best part of the day."
Partner talk: Ask students to share their favorite anything with their partner (favorite animal, color, game, book).
Encourage them to say why they like it (e.g., "I love dogs because they're playful").
Class Discussion:
Gather a few examples from the partners.
Highlight the difference between the opinion (what they like) and the reason (why they like it).
Create a simple chart on the board: Opinion & Reason from the ideas given.
Discussion:
Students choose one favorite thing from the day's discussion or generally in their lives.

Guided Reading:
Warm-up (5 min)
Ask: “Has anyone ever convinced you to buy something? How?”
Watch persuasive writing video (2 minutes).
Practice (7 min)
Read a short advertisement (e.g., “This toy is the best! Everyone loves it! Buy yours today!”).
Ask: “What is the author trying to do?” (Persuade)
Discuss persuasive techniques (opinion words, emotional appeal, facts).
Have students write their own 2-sentence persuasive ad on whiteboards.
Closing (3 min)
Share a few persuasive ads.
Ask: “What words did they use to persuade?”
Mentor Text:
Hey Little Ant by Phillip and Hannah Hoose
Don't Let the Pigeon Drive The Bus by Mo Willems
The Great Kapok Tree by Lynne Cherry
Earrings by Judith Viorst
One Word from Sophia by Jim Averbeck and Yasmeen Ismail

Grammar:
Warm-Up (3 min) – Fix the Sentences
Write three incorrect sentences on the board (e.g., Where are you going said Mom.)
Have students correct them on whiteboards.
Practice (10 min) – Interactive Mini-Lesson
Show a model sentence: “I love ice cream,” said Sarah.
Discuss the placement of quotation marks, commas, and capitalization.
Give students short sentences without quotation marks.
Have them rewrite sentences with correct punctuation in pairs.
Closing (2 min) – Partner Share
Have students read their revised sentences aloud.
Reflect: “What was easy or tricky about using quotation marks?”

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Warm-up (5 min)
Show two books: One nonfiction (Why Do Snakes Shed Their Skin?) and one fiction (There’s a snake in my school)
Ask: “Which one gives facts? How do you know?”
Review informational text features (headings, facts, pictures).
Practice (7 min)
Read a short passage from an article (e.g., about sharks).
Ask: “What is the purpose? What clues tell us this?”
Students find three facts in the passage and share.
Closing (3 min)
Students pick up a nonfiction book and find one fact to share.
Grammar:
Warm-Up (3 min) – Act It Out
Partner students and have them act out a two-line conversation (e.g., “What’s your favorite food?” “Pizza!”).
Ask: “How would we write that on paper?”
Practice (10 min) – Guided Writing
Model writing a short dialogue (3-4 lines) with proper punctuation.
Students write their own mini-conversation between two characters.
Closing (2 min) – Peer Review
Have students swap notebooks and check each other’s quotation marks.
Discuss common mistakes together.

Writing:

Writing DAY 2 - Opinion Writing
Mini-Lesson: Opinions + Reasons + "Because"
Review what an opinion and a reason is from the day before. Introduce the word "because" as a keyword to connect the two.
Example: "I think we should have more library books because I love to read new stories."
Brainstorm: How Can We Make Our School Better?
As a class, brainstorm a list of things that students think could be improved at school (e.g., recess, lunch, library, classrooms, learning, clean spaces etc.). Write these ideas on the board or chart paper.
Think-Pair-Share:
Students choose one of the brainstormed items they feel strongly about.
They think about why they think that way.
They share their opinion and reasons with a partner.
Graphic Organizer: Opinion & Reasons
Provide a simple graphic organizer (like a basic box with "Opinion" at the top and three lines underneath for reasons).
Students write their opinion at the top and then three reasons why they think that way.
Finish Off   Using the graphic organizer, students write 3-4 sentences expressing their opinion and reasons. E.g.: "I think we should have more time during recess. Because playing outside is fun and I need to run around. Also, more recess time can help us focus in class."

Guided Reading:

Warm-up (5 min)
Ask: “What is your favorite story? Why do you like it?”
Introduce entertaining texts: Stories with characters, humor, or drama.
Practice (7 min)
Read a funny passage from Don’t Let the Pigeon Drive the Bus!.
Ask: “Why did the author write this? What makes it fun?”
Students act out a funny part from a story they know.
Closing (3 min)
Quick review game: Teacher names a book, and students say if it is Persuade, Inform, or Entertain.

Grammar:
Warm-Up (3 min) – Quotation Marks Quiz
Quick 5-question quiz (e.g., “Which sentence is punctuated correctly?”).
Practice (10 min) – Creative Writing
Give students a fun prompt: “Imagine you meet a talking animal. What would you say to each other?”
Students write a short dialogue using quotation marks.
Closing (2 min) – Author’s Chair
Invite a few students to share their writing.

Writing:

Writing DAY 3 - Opinion Writing
Mini-Lesson: Polite Listening and Sharing
Review expectations for good listening (eyes on the speaker, quiet mouth).
Discuss how to respond to opinions that are different from yours politely (e.g., "That's an interesting idea," "I see what you mean").
Explain that being good citizens means listening to others and respecting their viewpoints, even if they don't agree.
Partner Share and Feedback:  Students share their writing from the previous day, with a partner. The partner should listen and offer a polite comment or question. They switch roles.
Wrap up: Discuss how we can use our opinions to make positive changes and how being a good listener is part of being a good citizen.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Knowledge Harvest
What does it mean to be a responsible member of a community and a global citizen? Mind map ideas of what it means to be  a member of a responsible community

Introduction (5 minutes)
Start: "What makes a good citizen in our classroom?" (Quick responses, 2-3 examples).
Explain that today, they will think about being a citizen of the whole world, "We're talking about responsible global citizens."

* You can use visuals, quickly to show 1-2 very simple images depicting responsible global actions or state them quickly (e.g., recycling, helping).
Short Group Brainstorm (10 minutes)
Question: "What is one thing a ‘responsible global citizen’ does?" (1 minute of individual thinking).
Pair-Share: Students quickly share their one idea with a partner (3 minutes).

Record Pairs Ideas: As a class go around the groups to quickly note their one chosen idea on chart paper or whiteboard in key words (e.g., "helps others," "cares for earth", "respects differences", etc.) (3 minutes)

 Class Definition Building (10 minutes)
Use pre-written sentence stems to help the class define ‘responsible global citizen’ quickly. Here are a couple you could use on a screen or small piece of paper:
"A responsible global citizen is someone who..." clearly on the chart paper/whiteboard.

"Responsible global citizens care about..."
"Responsible global citizens help by..."

Collaborate & Write: Work together quickly to form a concise definition. Write it
Wrap-up & Reflection (5 minutes)

Quick Examples: Ask a few students to give a very quick example of something a responsible global citizen might do.
Thumbs Up/Down: Ask "Give me a thumbs up if you understand what a responsible global citizen is. Thumbs down if you don't."

meta.objectives: To collaborate to form a common definition for “responsible global citizen”.
meta.assessment: Observe level of student participation
meta.differentiation:
Support: Use the sentence stems directly and provide 1-2 examples of a responsible action, without discussion.

Challenge: Encourage them to consider larger actions that go beyond their personal experience.

The Inference Collection - Once Upon a Picture

Display a picture with a hidden meaning (Example: A boy with a backpack and umbrella looking at the sky).
Ask:
What do you see?

Closing (3 min) – Exit Ticket

Show an excerpt from BrainPOP Jr. Video: "Pronouns" (https://www.brainpop.com/).

Practice (10 min)

Guided Practice: Show sentences on the board, such as:
"Sarah loves ice cream. Sarah eats it every day."

Writing:

Writing DAY 1 - Opinion Writing
Focus: Using previously explored content about Needs and Wants to form an opinion.
(5 mins) Review:
 "Remember, we spoke about Needs/Wants?"
Introduction:: "Should everyone in the world have the same things?"
Write it on the board
(20 mins) Brainstorming Reasons:
 "Why Do You Think That?"
Explain: "We're going to write our opinions about this! An opinion is what you think or believe. It's okay if it's different from what someone else thinks."
Ask: "Raise your hand if you think everyone should have the same things. Raise your hand if you think it's okay for people to have different things." (Gauge the class's general leanings).
Make a list of “Wants & Needs”
Model: "Let's say my opinion is: 'I don't think everyone should have the same things.' Why do I think that? ...because people live in different places and need different clothes.'" (Write this as a model reason on the board).
Now, open it to the class. Say: "Okay, why might someone think everyone should have the same things? What are some reasons?" Record student ideas on the chart paper. e.g., Fairness, Basic Needs are important etc.

(10 mins) Writing Strong Opinions with Reasons:
Distribute the worksheet (as per link)/physical copy.
Sentence Starters: Write these on the board: (on sheet as well)
"In my opinion..."
"I think..."
"I believe..."
"One reason why I think this is..."
"Another reason is..."

Students write ONE sentence expressing their opinion.
Then, they write AT LEAST ONE sentence giving a reason for their opinion.

(5 mins) Share Out:
Invite a few students to share their opinions and reasons. Give positive feedback: "That's a great reason! You explained that really well."

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:
Warm-up (5 min) – Mystery Box
Place an item (e.g., sunglasses) inside a box.
Give clues: "This is used on sunny days. It protects your eyes."
Ask: "What do you infer is inside the box?"
Reveal the item & explain how they made the inference.
Practice (7 min) – Read & Infer
Start and stop throughout the book. Read aloud a short passage from a mentor text (e.g., "The Stranger" by Chris Van Allsburg). or The Arabic Quilt an Immigrant Story
Ask:
What do we already know?
What clues did the author give?
What do we infer?
Closing (3 min) – Think & Share
Turn & Talk: Share one inference from the passage.
Grammar:
Warm-Up (3 min)
Show a Pronoun Picture Cards activity with images of one person vs. multiple people.
Ask: "If you are talking about one person, what pronoun do you use? What if there are two?"
Practice (10 min)
Direct Teaching: Write sentences with missing pronouns on the board:
"Jake loves to play soccer. ___ plays every day." (He)
"Jake and Emma love soccer. ___ play every day." (They)
Class Practice: Call on students to fill in blanks using pronouns.
Group Game: Play “Pronoun Relay” where teams race to match a pronoun to a noun.

Writing:

Writing DAY 2 - Opinion Writing

What's the Most Important Thing to Trade?
(5 mins) Review Imports/Exports & Opinion Prompt:
Quickly review look at the PowerPoint (click on link below): https://gamma.app/docs/Qatar-and-Japans-Trade-Landscape-Exports-and-Imports-jqf67csnev4aks5

Introducing the Opinion Prompt: "What is the most important thing [Qatar/Japan] trades?" Explain "Most Important" can mean different things (helps the most people, makes the most money, etc.).
(20 mins) Brainstorming Reasons:
Link to Importance:
Ask: "Which of these imports or exports do you think is the most important? Why?"
Model: "Let's say I think bananas are the most important import. Why? Maybe because they are a healthy food that many people eat." (Write on the board).
Prompt for different perspectives:
What if you think oil is the most important import? Why? (Economy/energy).
What if you think wheat is the most important export? Why? (Feeding others).

Record student ideas (briefly!) on chart paper under the item: (e.g., Bananas - healthy food, many people eat them).

(10 mins) Opinion Writing with Reasons:
Students to record ideas on sheet as done for Day 1
Students write their opinion (what they think is most important) and give at least one reason explaining why it's important.
Encourage them to link their reason to the benefit for people, the economy, or the country.

(5 mins) Partner Share:
Students share their opinions and reasons with a partner.
Encourage them to ask each other: "Why do you think that's important?"

Guided Reading:
Warm-up (5 min) – Video Clip Predictions
Show a short video, Those Shoes
Pause halfway and ask: "What do you predict will happen next?"
Discuss why they made that prediction.
Practice (7 min) – Guided Reading with Predictions
Read the first few paragraphs of a story (e.g., Owl Moon by Jane Yolen).
Pause at key moments and ask:
What will happen next?
What clues support your prediction?
Students write predictions on whiteboards.
Closing (3 min) – Reflect
Students update or revise their predictions based on new details.

Grammar:
Warm-Up (3 min)
Show YouTube Video: “What are Pronouns?” (Search for kid-friendly animated explanations).
Ask: "Which pronouns do we use for boys? Girls? Animals?"
Practice (10 min)
Mini-Lesson: Write sentences missing pronouns:
"Samantha loves books. ___ reads every day." (She)
"Max has a dog. ___ is very playful." (It)
Pair Work: Hand out sentence strips with a noun and students match it with the correct pronoun.
Interactive Whiteboard Game: Drag and drop correct pronouns into sentences.

Guided Reading:
Warm-up (5 min) – Quick Inferencing Game
Display a sentence with missing information, e.g.,
"Sam was shivering, even though the sun was shining."
Ask: What can we infer?
Practice (7 min) – Writing with Clues
Students write a short mystery scene (3–5 sentences).
The scene should include clues that help readers make inferences.
Pair up and exchange papers. Partners read & infer.
Closing (3 min) – Share & Celebrate
A few students share their mystery sentences with the class.
Class makes inferences & predictions about them.
Grammar:
Warm-Up (3 min)
Quick pronoun review game: Teacher says a noun, and students shout the correct pronoun.

Practice (10 min)
Writing Activity: Students write a 5-sentence short story using at least 3 different pronouns.
Peer Review: Students swap papers and underline each other’s pronouns.

records.cell_text:
(5 mins) What Does “Global” Mean?
"Being global means countries are connected by movement of people, goods, and ideas.
(25 mins) “How do countries get things they don’t have?” Discuss Import/export

Consolidate on trade idea:

Countries sell what they have - export

Countries buy what they need - import

Locate Qatar

Students locate Qatar on a map or globe.

Identify:

Land

Sea

Coast

Teacher prompt:

“What do you notice about where Qatar is?”Say the following to students: Fish, Dates, Oil

Clothes, toys, Takis,  - encourage students to

 put these under the headings of Qatar or

 Other, some may fall under both so place in

 the middle between Qatar and other

countries.. Invite students to name other items

and state where to place these.

Explain: “Countries don’t all have the same
things. They share by buying and selling. This
 is called trade.”
How are these things moved from place to
 Place? Ships and planes/trains and trucks

(10 mins) Wrap-up
"We get/send these things from/to [Summarize a couple of countries]. We're connected to the world through trade!"
Complete sheet:
Draw one way countries are connected
Finish the sentence:
“Global means __________.”

meta.objectives:
To understand that countries share goods

To explain that trade connects countries
meta.assessment: To respond to questions related to session
meta.differentiation:
Teacher-Led: The teacher plays a more active role in guiding discussions and facilitating activities. (generally - as required)

Visual Aids: Use pictures, labels, and visual aids to support understanding.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Key vocabulary
Map, Sea, Coast, Desert

(5mins)
Look at maps from previous session:
Ask students to help you locate:
Their country Qatar
Key questions
Is Qatar big or small?
Is it surrounded by land or water?
What do we call land next to the sea?
Coast

(25 mins) “Why does the coast matter?" Discuss.
Record ideas given by students.

Then look at activities and categorise them into land activity versus sea/ocean activity e.g. fishing, pearl diving, shopping, swimming (both), attending school…

Explain: “Places near the sea can use boats to move people and goods.”

meta.objectives:
To find Qatar on a map.
To describe where Qatar is.
To explain why being near the sea is important.

Writing:

Session 1 - Opinion Writing
Focus: Building upon previously explored content about Needs and Wants to record opinion along with reasons.
 Introduction – Class Discussion (10 minutes)
Ask: "Do we have a responsibility to help people in need?"
Discuss different viewpoints. Encourage students to think about reasons for and against helping others.
Write a list of possible reasons on the board (e.g., For: It is kind, everyone deserves basic needs, we can make a difference. Against: Some people believe everyone should help themselves, we may not always have extra to give).
Model Opinion Writing (10 minutes)
Opinion Statement: "I believe we should help others in need."
Reason 1: "Many people do not have enough food, water, or shelter, and we can help."
Reason 2: "Helping others makes the world a kinder place."
Reason 3: "If we were in need, we would want others to help us too."
Conclusion: "For these reasons, I believe it is important to help those in need."
Discuss the importance of using strong reasons to support opinions.
3. Independent Writing – Opinion Paragraph (15 minutes)Students write their own opinion paragraph using the structure:Opinion Statement -  "I think we should/should not help others in need." - is this different to “I believe…?”Three Strong ReasonsConclusion (Restating their opinion)Provide sentence starters to help students:
"I believe that..."
"One reason is..."
"Another reason is..."
"For these reasons, I think..."
4. Sharing & Reflection (10 minutes)
Wrap up by reinforcing how opinion writing helps us express thoughts clearly.

meta.differentiation:
Reading-
Struggling learners: Provide sentence starters (e.g., "A fact about school is…").
Advanced learners: Challenge them to find two facts and two opinions in a short text.

Grammar- Struggling students: Provide highlighted sentence structure cues.
Advanced students: Ask them to create their own examples.

Writing:
LA: Provide sentence starters for structuring their writing.
Word bank with key vocabulary (e.g., responsibility, helping, needs, wants, fairness).
Allow students to verbally express their opinions before writing them down.
HA: Encourage them to write a longer paragraph or multiple paragraphs.
include examples or personal experiences to strengthen their reasoning.
Can look at how to put in a counter argument: “Some people think we should not help others because… but I believe…”

Session 4 - Opinion Writing - Researching Opinions on Branding

Review & Discuss (10 minutes)
Recap from Session 3: "Why do we like certain brands?"
Ask: "What do we want to find out from other students?"
    Group Work – Creating a Questionnaire (25 minutes)
Divide the class into small groups.
Each group designs 3-5 questions to ask other students (e.g., "Do you prefer brand-name or store-brand food? Why?").
Encourage a mix of multiple-choice and open-ended questions.
Groups practice asking their questions with each other.
    Wrap-up & Next Steps (5 minutes)
Groups share their questions.
Explain: "Next week, we will use this research to help create our own product and brand!"

Coast

Full Text:Luqaimat is a popular snack in Qatar. It is a small, round dumpling that is crispy on the outside and soft on the inside. People make Luqaimat with flour, yeast, sugar, and milk. They fry the dough until it turns golden brown. Then, they cover it with honey or date syrup to make it sweet. Luqaimat is often eaten during Ramadan and special gatherings. It is a favorite treat for many families in Qatar.

Warm-Up (3 min):

Quick review: Watch a short BrainPOP video on nouns and verbs.
Practice (25 min)
Whole Group: Read a short passage (fiction or nonfiction).
Pair Work: Highlight nouns and verbs in the text.
Guided Discussion: What is the main idea of the passage? How do the nouns and verbs help express it?
