Writing:
Session 1: Introduction to Summarizing and Topic/Product Selection
Procedure (40 minutes):
(5 minutes) What is Summarizing?  Begin by asking students what they think "summarizing" means. Discuss examples of when they summarize (e.g., telling a friend about a movie, explaining what they learned in class). Explain that summarizing is taking a lot of information and making it shorter by only including the most important parts.
(10 minutes) Summarizing Practice: Present a sample short text. As a class, brainstorm the most important information from the text. Guide students to condense the information into a single sentence or two. Encouraging students to participate more independently - sheet available
(15 minutes) Product Selection/Design Decision:
(Option 1: Existing Food Product): Show examples of various food products (pictures work well). Discuss where they come from, what they are made of, and why people like them. Have the student choose one product to focus on for their research - linked to going to the shop last week.
(Option 2: New Food Product): Brainstorm different kinds of foods and ingredients. Show examples of unusual ingredients or interesting recipes. Guide the student to decide on a type of food (e.g., a snack, a dessert, a drink) and some initial ingredients they want to explore. Explain that they will need to justify why they are choosing these ingredients/a certain food type.
(5 minutes) Initial Research/Design Thoughts:
(Option 1: Existing Food Product): Students use Google slides to record their initial thoughts and knowledge about the food product they chose. (e.g., "What I already know about apples: They grow on trees, they are healthy.")
(Option 2: New Food Product): Students use the Google slides to record their initial ideas about their new food product. (e.g., "Type of food: A healthy snack. Ingredients I want to use: Blueberries, Oats, Honey. Why: They are healthy and taste good.")
Food Product: ________________________
My initial thoughts: _______________________________________________________________________________________________
What I want to find out: _______________________________________________________________________________________________
(5 minutes) Wrap-up: Review the definition of summarizing and their chosen task (researching an existing product or designing a new one). Remind students to begin gathering information/thinking about their design.

W.5.4 – Produce clear and coherent writing in which the development, organization, and style are appropriate to task, purpose, and audience.
W.5.9 – Draw evidence from literary or informational texts to support analysis,
meta.assessment:
Reading- Turn and talk: Share your summary with a partner.
Ask: How does the SWBST strategy help us summarize? Listen to partner discussions.
Check if students use all parts of SWBST correctly.

Grammar-Exit Ticket: Write a sentence with at least one noun and one verb and underline them.
Reflection: Share how nouns and verbs help us understand what the passage is about.

Writing: Review completed "Summarizing Graphic Organizer" for product selection and initial thoughts.
meta.differentiation:
Reading- Struggling learners: Provide a completed example first.
Advanced learners: Have them try summarizing without the SWBST chart.

Grammar- Struggling students: Use a word bank to help identify nouns/verbs.
Advanced students: Write their own short paragraph using at least five nouns and verbs.

Writing:
Use plenty of visuals (pictures, diagrams, real food items) to keep students engaged.

Guided Reading:
Warm-Up (3 min):
Show a BrainPOP or YouTube video on Paraphrasing vs. Plagiarism.
Discuss: Why can’t we copy exactly? (Elicit responses about using your own words).
Practice (10 min):
Display a short passage from a mentor text.
Model how to paraphrase using synonyms and sentence restructuring.
Group activity: Give students a sentence, and they must rewrite it in their own words.
Sample sentences to tie in UPC:
Qatar imports many products, including cars, electronics, and food items.
The country exports oil and natural gas to various nations around the world.
Many businesses in Qatar help to bring goods from other countries into the nation.
People in Qatar enjoy a wide variety of products that come from different places.
Exporting goods helps Qatar earn money and build relationships with other countries.
Qatar’s ports are busy places where ships arrive and leave with different types of products.
Some imported goods are toys and clothes that people buy in stores.
Local companies in Qatar often sell their products to other countries as exports.
The government of Qatar supports trade to make the economy stronger and more diverse.
Importing and exporting create jobs for many people in Qatar and help the community grow.

Grammar:
Warm-up (10 min)
Game: “Describe It!” – Show a mystery object (or picture) and have students list adjectives and adverbs to describe it.
Quick review: Watch a BrainPOP video on adjectives and adverbs.
Practice (25 min)
Whole Group: Read a passage aloud and underline adjectives and adverbs in different colors.
Small Groups: Find the supporting details—How do adjectives and adverbs enhance the main idea?
Independent Practice: Sentence-building worksheet—Students rewrite dull sentences by adding adjectives and adverbs.

Writing:
Session 2 - Researching/Designing and Identifying a Focus
Procedure (40 minutes):
(5 minutes) Review: Briefly review what was learned about summarizing and their chosen product/design in the previous session.
(20 minutes) Research/Design:
(Option 1: Existing Food Product): Students use their research materials to find out more about their chosen product. They should focus on aspects like where it comes from, how it's made, its nutritional value, its history, or its impact on the environment/society.
(Option 2: New Food Product): Students research their chosen ingredients, looking at nutritional value, flavor combinations, and how they can be prepared. Encourage them to sketch their product design and think about its packaging.

(10 minutes) Identifying a Focus for Opinion Writing: Now, ask students to think about what they've learned.
(Option 1: Existing Food Product): What surprised them? What concerns them? What is interesting or important about this food product?
(Option 2: New Food Product): What are the benefits of their product? What are potential drawbacks? How could they make it even better?
Have students choose ONE question or issue to focus on. Examples:
(Option 1): "Are chocolate chip cookies healthy for you?", "Where do the apples in my grocery store come from?", "Is pizza a good food to eat every day?"
(Option 2): "Is my snack a healthy snack?", "How can I make my snack appeal to children?", "Are the ingredients I'm using good for the planet?"
(5 minutes) Wrap-up: Remind students to reflect on their research and the question they have chosen for the next session. Begin populating their "Summary Writing Checklist" and updating the "Summarizing Graphic Organizer"
Summary Writing Checklist
Have I found out interesting facts about my product?
Have I thought about what I think about my product or creation?
Am I ready to make some decisions?
Does it make sense what I am planning to do?

Discuss: What makes a good paraphrase? Listen to student responses and check if they changed the wording without altering meaning.

Grammar- Exit Ticket: Write a sentence using an adjective and an adverb.
Reflection: How do adjectives and adverbs make writing more interesting?

Advanced learners: Have them paraphrase a full paragraph instead of one sentence.

Grammar- Struggling students: Use sentence stems for support.

Provide sentence starters and graphic organizers with varying levels of scaffolding.

Guided Reading:
Warm-Up/Practice ((3 min):
Quick review: What’s the difference between summarizing and paraphrasing?
Play a sorting game: Give students examples of sentences and have them decide if they are summaries or paraphrases.
Read a short informational passage.
Quick Review/Check:
Step 1: Have students summarize the paragraph in one sentence.
Step 2: Have students paraphrase one sentence from the paragraph.
Compare: How are they different?

Grammar:
Warm-up (10 min)
Game: “Finish My Sentence” – Display half a sentence (e.g., “I went to the park…”) and have students complete it using a preposition, conjunction, or pronoun.
Quick review: Watch a BrainPOP video on prepositions, conjunctions, and pronouns.
Practice (25 min)
Whole Group: Read a nonfiction passage and identify prepositions, conjunctions, and pronouns.
Partner Work: Fill out a graphic organizer (main idea + 3 supporting details).
Independent Practice: Matching activity—Students match sentences with the correct conjunctions or prepositions.

Writing:
Session 3 - Summarizing and Opinion Writing
Procedure (40 minutes):
(5 minutes) Review: Briefly review their chosen product/design and the question they'll be answering.
(10 minutes) Writing the Summary: Students use the information in their "Google slides", to write a short paragraph summarizing their research/design process. Encourage students to use complete sentences and proper grammar.
(15 minutes) Opinion Paragraph Writing: Introduce or reintroduce the "Opinion"  slide and "Opinion Writing Sentence Starters" (same as before). Students use the slides and sentence starters to plan and write an opinion paragraph about their chosen issue, using the information they have gathered.
(5 minutes) Present and Wrap-up: Have one or two students share their opinion paragraphs and, if applicable, describe their food product design. Emphasize that there are no "right" or "wrong" opinions, they support with reasons.

Does the opinion paragraph express a clear opinion with supporting reasons?

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Warm-Up: (whole group)
The teacher will begin the lesson by briefly reviewing the reading and comprehension skills that were learned in previous classes. This may include identifying characters, setting, plot, and understanding the sequence of events in a story.

Practice:
Reading and comprehension activity: The teacher will provide each group of students with a text that is appropriate for their reading level. This may be a short story, a chapter from a book, a poem, or an informational text. The students will read the text in their groups, discussing the characters, setting, plot, and main ideas. The teacher will circulate around the room, offering support and guidance as needed..  (Can also be done in Guided Reading)

Wrap Up:
The teacher will summarize the main points of the lesson, reinforcing the importance of reading comprehension and summarizing as essential skills for understanding a text. The teacher may review the different strategies for summarizing and how they help identify the main ideas of a text.

Session 1: What is Rhyme?

(5 mins) Rhyme Time
Warm-Up: Play a rhyming game. Say a word, and students call out rhyming words. Focus on Ramadan-related words (dates, mosque, prayer, family, star, moon, iftar) - see column 1 for examples
(10mins) Rhyme Exploration:
 Read aloud a short poem with obvious rhyming (e.g., a simple nursery rhyme - see below). Highlight the rhyming words. Discuss what makes words rhyme (same sound at the end). Use visual aids showing words with the same ending to support learners.

What is rhyme.docx

Ramadan Nights
The stars twinkle up high, (A)We say our prayers with devotion, (B)As the blessed month goes by, (A)Showing love and emotion. (B)

(10 mins) Rhyme Sort:
Provide pairs of words. Students sort them into rhyming and non-rhyming pairs.
(10 mins) Rhyme Search:
Give students a short Ramadan-themed poem. They circle all the rhyming words they can find.

The Dates of Ramadan
Sweet dates on a silver plate, We break our fast with thankful hearts, Sharing blessings, sealed by fate, In Ramadan, brand new starts.
The sun has set, the day is done, A time for family, love, and cheer, Each moment feels like so much fun, Throughout the month, year after year.
Kindness spreads from door to door, With gifts and smiles, our spirits soar,Helping others, and so much more, Ramadan joy we all adore.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

ComprehensionPrintablesIdoWedoYoudo.pdf

The teacher and students will practice reading comprehension strategies within small groups or as a whole group. The teacher will be sure to have students identify skills that help in the understanding of the text. (See Below)
Before Reading (Pre-Reading Strategies)
Activating Prior Knowledge – Connect the text to what students already know.
Predicting – Make predictions based on the title, pictures, or prior knowledge.
Previewing the Text – Skim headings, images, and captions to get an overview.
Setting a Purpose for Reading – Define why they are reading (e.g., to learn, to enjoy, to find information).
During Reading (Active Reading Strategies)
Asking Questions – Encourage “who, what, where, when, why, how” questions.
Visualizing – Create mental images of what is happening in the text.
Making Connections – Text-to-self, text-to-text, and text-to-world connections.
Summarizing – Identify key details and main ideas in a few sentences.
Using Context Clues – Decode unfamiliar words based on surrounding text.
Making Inferences – Read between the lines to understand hidden meanings.
Rereading for Clarity – Go back and read again to improve understanding.
Identifying Main Idea and Supporting Details – Find the central message and key facts.
Monitoring Comprehension – Stop and reflect on understanding; use "fix-up" strategies if confused.
After Reading (Post-Reading Strategies)
Summarizing and Retelling – Recount the story in their own words.
Drawing Conclusions – Use text evidence to form opinions or conclusions.
Answering Comprehension Questions – Respond to literal, inferential, and critical thinking questions.
Discussing and Sharing – Participate in partner or group discussions about the text.
Making Connections to Real Life – Relate the story to personal experiences.
Creating a Graphic Organizer – Use Venn diagrams, story maps, or flowcharts to organize ideas.
Writing a Response – Reflect through writing (e.g., journal entry, letter, or opinion piece).

Grammar:

Refer to Anchor Chart

Warm-Up (3 min):
Review guide words from yesterday (quick game: find the guide words for "jump").
Watch short YouTube video on reading dictionary entries.
Practice (10 min):
Teacher models breaking down a dictionary entry: “Happy (hap·py) adj. Feeling or showing joy.”
Group challenge: Each group receives a word and must break down its entry (word, pronunciation, part of speech, definition).
Students create their own “fake” dictionary entry for a made-up word.

7 Characteristics of Living Things
Growth
Respiration - breathing
Sensitivity - Response to environment.
Having babies/seeds - Reproduction.
Excretion.
Eating and drinking - Nutrition.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Vocabulary
Living, nonliving, sun, energy

Visual Thinking (5 minutes)
Display a mix of pictures.
Teacher prompts:
“Which of these are alive?”
“How do you know?”
Students justify answers. Record student ideas on board.
Living vs Nonliving (7 minutes)
Explain:
Living things grow, need food, water, and air.
Nonliving things do not grow or need food.
Show short video clip - What Living Things Need to Survive (e.g., BBC Bitesize / National Geographic Kids).
Ask: “What do all living things seem to need?”
Introduce: sun = main energy source.
Picture Sort (10 minutes)
Students can work in pairs.
Spread picture cards.
Sort into Living / Nonliving.
Place on T-chart.
Question: “Why did you place this here?”
Energy Flow (5 minutes)
Draw/show: Garde 5 Food Chain.pptx
☀ → 🌱 → 🐇 → 🦊

Think–Pair–Share (2 minutes)
Question:“What would happen if the sun disappeared?”
Exit Ticket (1 minute)
Draw:
One living thing
One nonliving thing
Optional sentence:“The sun gives energy to ___.”

meta.standards:
Investigate and understand the living (animate) and nonliving (inanimate) world around them.
Understand the relationship between living things and the environment in which they live.
meta.assessment:
Observe sorting accuracy
Oral explanations
Exit ticket drawings

Support
Picture-only cards
Partner support
Sentence frame: “The sun gives energy to ___.”
Challenge
Write 2 sentences explaining why the sun is important.

Vocabulary
Producer, consumer, decomposer
Review (5 minutes)
Ask:“What gives plants energy?”“How do animals get energy?”
Explain (10 minutes) Components of a FOOD CHAIN and the definition:
Producers make their own food (plants).
Consumers eat plants or animals.
Decomposers break down dead things.
Display pictures.
Students repeat definitions aloud.
Guided Sorting (10 minutes)
The Food Chain for Kids | What is a food chain? |  Come learn about producers, consumers and more!
Watch video - discuss points and then rewatch to consolidate on the details of the different parts of a food chain.
Partner Share (3 minutes)
Explain one part of a food chain to a partner.
Quick Check (2 minutes)
Name 2 consumers - which type are they? Are they different or in the same sub-group of consumers?

meta.standards:
Know the major classifications of living things.
Know about the effect of food chains in a variety of environments.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Warm-Up: (whole group)
The teacher will begin the lesson by briefly reviewing the reading and comprehension skills that were learned in previous classes. This may include identifying characters, setting, plot, and understanding the sequence of events in a story.

Practice:
Reading and comprehension activity: The teacher will provide each group of students with a text that is appropriate for their reading level. This may be a short story, a chapter from a book, a poem, or an informational text. The students will read the text in their groups, discussing the characters, setting, plot, and main ideas. The teacher will circulate around the room, offering support and guidance as needed..  (Can also be done in Guided Reading)

Wrap Up:
The teacher will summarize the main points of the lesson, reinforcing the importance of reading comprehension and summarizing as essential skills for understanding a text. The teacher may review the different strategies for summarizing and how they help identify the main ideas of a text.

Writing:

This week students will be able to understand and compare the ABAB and AABB rhyme schemes, creating their own AABB poems about Eid - 3 sessions

What is Poetry?
Session 1
(10 mins) ABAB Quick Review:
Start with a choral reading of a short ABAB poem from last week's lessons (or one of the examples I provided).
Ask quick questions: "What's the rhyme scheme? How do you know? Where are the rhyming words?"
(15 mins) Introducing AABB:
Display the AABB structure visually (e.g., using colored markers or letters on the board):
Line 1: A (red)
Line 2: A (red)
Line 3: B (blue)
Line 4: B (blue)

Explain: "In AABB, the first and second lines rhyme, and the third and fourth lines rhyme. It's like two rhyming pairs right next to each other."
Read aloud a short, simple AABB poem about Eid (see resource ideas - in first column)).  Can label the rhyme scheme together as a class.

(5 mins) AABB Identification Practice:
Give students a very short poem. Ask them to identify if it is an AABB scheme, can they find the rhyming pairs?
records.label_note: ABAB Poems: Eid's Bright DayThe morning sun begins to glow, (A)We wear our clothes so new and bright, (B)Eid Mubarak, the greetings flow, (A)Filled with joy and pure delight. (B) Prayers and SweetsWe go to mosque to say our prayer, (A)Sweet treats are shared with every friend, (B)A joyful time beyond compare, (A)A celebration without end. (B) Gifts of EidThe gifts are wrapped with ribbon tied, (A)We hug our family, close and tight, (B)With love and laughter side by side, (A)On this special day, so bright! (B) AABB Poems: Eid's Happy TimeEid is here, so bright and grand, (A)A time to cheer throughout the land. (A)We share our food and give a treat, (B)With smiles and love on every street. (B) Eid GreetingsEid Mubarak, we all say, (A)Happy Eid to everyone today! (A)The mosque is full, we give and pray, (B)Hoping for a blessed day. (B) Eid JoyThe lights are on, so colorful and grand, (A)Celebrating Eid, hand in hand. (A)We visit friends and family too, (B)Sharing happiness in all we do. (B) (Sheet available)

meta.standards:
CCW.5.2.D Use precise language and domain-specific vocabulary to inform about or explain the topic.
CCW.5.4 Produce clear and coherent writing in which the development and organization are appropriate to task, purpose, and audience.
CCL.5.1.D Recognize and correct inappropriate shifts in verb tense.
EE5.1.2.A Introduce a topic and write to convey information about it, including one or more facts or details.
EE5.1.2.B Provide a closing statement related to the topic.
L.5.4.C (Homophones support) Consult reference materials (e.g., dictionaries, glossaries, thesauruses) to find the pronunciation and determine or clarify the precise meaning of key words and phrases.
L.5.5.C (Antonyms) Use the relationship between particular words (e.g., synonyms, antonyms, homographs) to better understand each of the words.
RL.5.2 (Poetry) Determine a theme of a story, drama, or poem from details in the text, including how characters respond to challenges; summarize the text.
RL.5.4 (Poetry) Determine the meaning of words and phrases as they are used in a text, including figurative language such as metaphors and similes.
RL.5.5 (Poetry) Explain how a series of chapters, scenes, or stanzas fits together to provide the overall structure of a story, drama, or poem.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

ComprehensionPrintablesIdoWedoYoudo.pdf

The teacher and students will practice reading comprehension strategies within small groups or as a whole group. The teacher will be sure to have students identify skills that help in the understanding of the text. (See Below)
Before Reading (Pre-Reading Strategies)
Activating Prior Knowledge – Connect the text to what students already know.
Predicting – Make predictions based on the title, pictures, or prior knowledge.
Previewing the Text – Skim headings, images, and captions to get an overview.
Setting a Purpose for Reading – Define why they are reading (e.g., to learn, to enjoy, to find information).
During Reading (Active Reading Strategies)
Asking Questions – Encourage “who, what, where, when, why, how” questions.
Visualizing – Create mental images of what is happening in the text.
Making Connections – Text-to-self, text-to-text, and text-to-world connections.
Summarizing – Identify key details and main ideas in a few sentences.
Using Context Clues – Decode unfamiliar words based on surrounding text.
Making Inferences – Read between the lines to understand hidden meanings.
Rereading for Clarity – Go back and read again to improve understanding.
Identifying Main Idea and Supporting Details – Find the central message and key facts.
Monitoring Comprehension – Stop and reflect on understanding; use "fix-up" strategies if confused.
After Reading (Post-Reading Strategies)
Summarizing and Retelling – Recount the story in their own words.
Drawing Conclusions – Use text evidence to form opinions or conclusions.
Answering Comprehension Questions – Respond to literal, inferential, and critical thinking questions.
Discussing and Sharing – Participate in partner or group discussions about the text.
Making Connections to Real Life – Relate the story to personal experiences.
Creating a Graphic Organizer – Use Venn diagrams, story maps, or flowcharts to organize ideas.
Writing a Response – Reflect through writing (e.g., journal entry, letter, or opinion piece).
Grammar:

Refer to Anchor Chart and Brainpop Jr Video

Warm-Up (3 min):
Write the word "happy" on the board. Ask:
"Can you think of another word for happy?"
"What’s the opposite of happy?"
Introduce the thesaurus as a tool to find synonyms & antonyms.
Practice (10 min):
Complete a Kahoot as a way to informally assess students.
Thesaurus Treasure Hunt: In pairs, students:
Find synonyms for 3 given words.
Find antonyms for 2 given words.
Use one word in a sentence.

Poetry | Rhyme Scheme, Rhythm, Repetition

Writing:

Session 2: AABB Poem Building: Word Brainstorming
(10 mins) Eid Brainstorm:
As a class, brainstorm words and phrases related to Eid. Write them on the board. Encourage students to think about:
Eid greetings ("Eid Mubarak," "Eid Saeed")
Eid traditions (prayers, gifts, food, family, celebrations)
Eid feelings (joy, gratitude, happiness, peace)

(15 mins) Rhyming Pair Match:
Divide the class into small groups.
Give each group a set of index cards. On some cards, write Eid-related words from the brainstorm. On other cards, write rhyming words.
Groups work together to match the rhyming pairs.
Example: "Gift" and "Lift," "Pray" and "Day," "Greet" and "Treat"

(5 mins) Sharing Rhyming Pairs:
Each group shares a few of the rhyming pairs they found. Add these to the list on the board
records.label_note: ABAB Poems: Eid's Bright DayThe morning sun begins to glow, (A)We wear our clothes so new and bright, (B)Eid Mubarak, the greetings flow, (A)Filled with joy and pure delight. (B) Prayers and SweetsWe go to mosque to say our prayer, (A)Sweet treats are shared with every friend, (B)A joyful time beyond compare, (A)A celebration without end. (B) Gifts of EidThe gifts are wrapped with ribbon tied, (A)We hug our family, close and tight, (B)With love and laughter side by side, (A)On this special day, so bright! (B) AABB Poems: Eid's Happy TimeEid is here, so bright and grand, (A)A time to cheer throughout the land. (A)We share our food and give a treat, (B)With smiles and love on every street. (B) Eid GreetingsEid Mubarak, we all say, (A)Happy Eid to everyone today! (A)The mosque is full, we give and pray, (B)Hoping for a blessed day. (B) Eid JoyThe lights are on, so colorful and grand, (A)Celebrating Eid, hand in hand. (A)We visit friends and family too, (B)Sharing happiness in all we do. (B) (Sheet available)

Warm-Up (3 min):
Write the word "bat" on the board. Ask:
"What does 'bat' mean?" (Flying animal? Baseball bat?)
Explain that homonyms are words that sound the same but have different meanings.
Practice (10 min):
Read A Lime, A Mime, A Pool of Slime aloud.
Watch the YouTube video on homonyms.
Group activity:
Each group gets 3 homonyms (e.g., bark, wave, ring).
They write two definitions & draw a picture for each meaning.

Writing:
Session 3: AABB Poem Writing: Guided
(10 mins) Review & Model:
Quickly review the AABB rhyme scheme again.
Model writing a single AABB stanza on the board, using words and phrases from the brainstorm list.
Example:
Eid Mubarak, a joyful day, (A)
Let's laugh and sing and play. (A)
With family near, we give a greet, (B)
Enjoying special Eid treat (B)
(15 mins) Guided Writing:
Work together as a class to write a longer AABB poem about Eid.
Guide them through the process, line by line:
"Let's start with a line about [specific Eid activity]. What words can we use?"
"What word rhymes with that word? Let's write that on the second line."
"Now let's think about [another Eid aspect]. How can we start that third line?"
"What rhymes with that?"
Write the poem on the board as it is created, emphasizing the AABB rhyme scheme.
(5 mins) Reading and Sharing:
Read the completed poem aloud as a class.
Ask for volunteers to share what they liked best about the poem.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Parts of a Plant
Vocabulary
Root, Stem, Leaf, Flower, Plant

Show a real plant  image.
Ask students:
“What do you notice about this plant?”
“What parts can you see?”
Record ideas.
2. Explicit Teaching
Introduce the plant parts using a diagram.
Explain functions:
Roots
Hold the plant in the ground
Absorb water and nutrients
Stem
Supports the plant
Moves water to leaves
Leaves
Make food using sunlight
Flower
Helps the plant reproduce
Ask quick check questions:
“Which part makes food?” “Which part holds the plant in the soil?”

3. Guided Practice – Labeling
Steps:
 Label root, stem, leaf, flower.
Teacher asking:
“What does the leaf do?”
“Why are roots important?”

4. Quick Knowledge Check
Students draw a plant and label the parts.
Students show drawings and give teacher feedback.

5. Exit Question
Ask:
“Which plant part makes food?”
Students respond orally.

meta.standards:
Know about the functions of the major parts of a plant.
Investigate and understand the living (animate) and nonliving (inanimate) world around them.
meta.assessment:
Student responses
Verbal answers
meta.differentiation:
Support
Pre-labeled diagram with word bank
Visual reference chart
Extension
Students give statements such as:
“Leaves help plants because __________.”

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Plant Reproduction & Life Cycle
Vocabulary
Life cycle, Seed, Seedling, Adult plant, Reproduction

Ask:
“Which part of the plant helps it reproduce?”
Review: flower

2. Direct Instruction
Show plant life cycle diagram.
Explain stages:
Seed
Seedling
Adult plant
Flower produces seeds
Explain that plants reproduce by making seeds.

3. Video
Show a short life cycle video.
Ask students to watch for:
“What happens after the seed grows?”

4. Guided Activity
Students are shown life cycle cards.
Students make suggestions on which step is next…
“What comes after the seed?”

5. Discussion
Students volunteer to explain the life cycle.

 6. Exit Ticket
Students draw plant cycle:

meta.standards:
Know about ways in which plants reproduce.
Gather evidence from a variety of sources.
meta.assessment:
Sequencing
Exit drawing
meta.differentiation:
Support
Sequencing cards
Visual support
Extension
Students write a sentence describing the life cycle.
Example:
“A seed grows into a plant that makes more seeds.”

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Animal Life Cycles
Vocabulary
Life cycle, Metamorphosis, Adult, Offspring

Ask:
“Do animals stay the same as they grow?”
Show baby animal pictures.

Can access this learning through the story of The Very Hungry Caterpillar

2. Direct Instruction
Introduce butterfly life cycle:
Egg
Caterpillar
Chrysalis
Butterfly
Explain metamorphosis = major change during growth.

3. Video
Short butterfly life cycle video.
Students watch for stages.

4. Guided Activity – Life Cycle Wheel
Students look at a butterfly life cycle

5. Compare Discussion
Ask:
“How is a butterfly different from a human life cycle?”

6. Exit Question (2 minutes)
“What is metamorphosis?”

meta.standards:
Know the major classifications of living things.
Investigate and understand the living world around them.
meta.assessment:
Life cycle wheel accuracy
Oral responses
meta.differentiation:
Support
Pre-labeled stages
Visual prompts
Extension
Students compare two life cycles:
Butterfly vs human.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Warm-Up: (whole group)
The teacher will begin the lesson by briefly reviewing the reading and comprehension skills that were learned in previous classes. This may include identifying characters, setting, plot, and understanding the sequence of events in a story.

Practice:
Reading and comprehension activity: The teacher will provide each group of students with a text that is appropriate for their reading level. This may be a short story, a chapter from a book, a poem, or an informational text. The students will read the text in their groups, discussing the characters, setting, plot, and main ideas. The teacher will circulate around the room, offering support and guidance as needed..  (Can also be done in Guided Reading)

Wrap Up:
The teacher will summarize the main points of the lesson, reinforcing the importance of reading comprehension and summarizing as essential skills for understanding a text. The teacher may review the different strategies for summarizing and how they help identify the main ideas of a text.

Warm-up (3 min):
Quick Brainstorm: Ask students to think of words that sound the same but have different meanings (e.g., "two" and "too"). Write their responses on the board.
Show a short video on homophones.
Practice (10 min):
Matching Game: Give students index cards with homophone pairs (e.g., "sea" and "see"). Have them find their partner and define their words.
Sentence Sort: Display sentences on the board with missing homophones, and students fill in the blanks correctly.
Writing:
Overall Goal: Students will understand the structure and purpose of Haiku poetry and be able to write their own simple Haiku poems.

 Introduction to Haiku
Session 1: What is Haiku? (30 minutes)

Haiku Poetry for Kids

(15 mins) Haiku Introduction:
Watch video - Explain that Haiku is a type of poem that comes from Japan. It often focuses on nature and capturing a moment in time.
Write the word "Haiku" on the board.
Explain the syllable structure:
Line 1: 5 syllables
Line 2: 7 syllables
Line 3: 5 syllables
Write this structure visually on the board (e.g., using dots or dashes for each syllable).
Read aloud a simple Haiku poems - Count the syllables together as a class:

      Rain
Soft rain is falling
Washing the world with new life
Green plants rise above

(5 mins) Quick Check: Ask students to repeat the Haiku syllable structure (5-7-5).

(10 mins) Work through slides from the PPT link below with task at the end (paper version of task made available)
HAIKU poems.ppt
records.label_note: Example Haiku: Rain Soft rain is falling Washing the world with new life Green plants rise above

Guided Reading:

ComprehensionPrintablesIdoWedoYoudo.pdf

The teacher and students will practice reading comprehension strategies within small groups or as a whole group. The teacher will be sure to have students identify skills that help in the understanding of the text. (See Below)
Before Reading (Pre-Reading Strategies)
Activating Prior Knowledge – Connect the text to what students already know.
Predicting – Make predictions based on the title, pictures, or prior knowledge.
Previewing the Text – Skim headings, images, and captions to get an overview.
Setting a Purpose for Reading – Define why they are reading (e.g., to learn, to enjoy, to find information).
During Reading (Active Reading Strategies)
Asking Questions – Encourage “who, what, where, when, why, how” questions.
Visualizing – Create mental images of what is happening in the text.
Making Connections – Text-to-self, text-to-text, and text-to-world connections.
Summarizing – Identify key details and main ideas in a few sentences.
Using Context Clues – Decode unfamiliar words based on surrounding text.
Making Inferences – Read between the lines to understand hidden meanings.
Rereading for Clarity – Go back and read again to improve understanding.
Identifying Main Idea and Supporting Details – Find the central message and key facts.
Monitoring Comprehension – Stop and reflect on understanding; use "fix-up" strategies if confused.
After Reading (Post-Reading Strategies)
Summarizing and Retelling – Recount the story in their own words.
Drawing Conclusions – Use text evidence to form opinions or conclusions.
Answering Comprehension Questions – Respond to literal, inferential, and critical thinking questions.
Discussing and Sharing – Participate in partner or group discussions about the text.
Making Connections to Real Life – Relate the story to personal experiences.
Creating a Graphic Organizer – Use Venn diagrams, story maps, or flowcharts to organize ideas.
Writing a Response – Reflect through writing (e.g., journal entry, letter, or opinion piece).

Grammar:

Refer to Anchor Chart and Brainpop Jr Video

Warm-up (3 min):
Riddle Time: Give students homophone-based riddles (e.g., “I have a pair, but I’m not a shoe. What am I?” – Pear).
Practice (10 min):
Homophone Sketch: Students work in pairs to draw pictures representing homophones and write sentences to go with them.
Seesaw or Writing Activity: Assign a homophone mini-quiz on Seesaw where students choose the correct homophone in sentences.

Writing:

Session 2:
 Counting Syllables (30 minutes)

Activities:

(10 mins) Syllable Review:

Explain that syllables are the "beats" in a word.

Demonstrate how to clap out the syllables in a few words (e.g., "Sun-shine," "Flow-er," "Win-ter").

Have students practice clapping out syllables in various words related to nature.

(15 mins) Syllable Sort:

Give students a set of index cards with words related to nature written on them.

Divide students into small groups.

Have students sort the words by the number of syllables (1 syllable, 2 syllables, 3 syllables, etc.).

(5 mins) Syllable Game:

Play a quick game of "Syllable Bingo." Call out a number (e.g., "2 syllables"). Students cover a word on their bingo card with that many syllables.

Grammar:

 Homographs- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dtv4XkUi5R4

Warm-up (3 min):
Act It Out: Write the word “bow” on the board. Ask a student to act out two meanings (e.g., take a bow, tie a bow). Discuss how one word can have multiple meanings.
Practice (10 min):
Mentor Text: Read a passage with homographs (e.g., from "Amelia Bedelia" books).
Sorting Activity: Provide a list of words (e.g., “lead,” “tear,” “row”) and have students sort them based on different meanings.

AND/OR

Writing:

Session 3:
Haiku Creation: Brainstorming Nature Observations (30 minutes)

Activities:

(10 mins) Nature Walk (Imaginary or Real):

If possible, take students on a short, silent nature walk outside the classroom (to the school garden, a nearby tree, etc.). Have them pay close attention to what they see, hear, smell, and feel.

If a real nature walk is not possible, guide students through an imaginary nature walk. Ask them to close their eyes and imagine they are walking through a forest, a garden, or a field. Ask questions: "What do you see? What colors do you notice? What sounds do you hear? What smells are in the air? How does the air feel on your skin?"

(15 mins) Sensory Brainstorm:

After the nature walk (real or imaginary), have students brainstorm words and phrases that describe their observations.

Focus on sensory details: sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and textures.

Write the brainstormed words and phrases on the board.

(5 mins) Quick Share: Have a few students share their favorite observations.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
What Are Fungi?
Key Vocabulary
Fungi (fungus); Mushroom; Microorganism; Cap; Stem
Watch video on fungi/mushrooms:
All About Fungi

Fungi: Why Mushrooms Are Awesome | Biology for Kids
Ask:
“Is fungus a plant or an animal?”
“Where have you seen this before?”
Explain that fungi are a different group of living things - the part we see of the fungi coming out of the ground are called mushrooms. Some fungi do not have mushrooms but all mushrooms are fungi because there is a part of them that decomposes dead things in the ground to make food to eat.
“Which word is the group name?”
Choices: (a) mushroom or (b) fungus
Answer: fungus
Explain key facts:
Fungi are living organisms.
They do not make their own food like plants.
Many fungi grow on dead or decaying things.
Look at a mushroom diagram:
The parts - Cap & Stem
Discuss: “Why do fungi grow on dead things?”
Introduce the idea of decomposition.

meta.standards:
Know about the nature, functions, and effects of microorganisms.
Know about the major classifications of living things.
meta.objectives:
To identify fungi as a type of living organism.
To describe the basic parts of a mushroom.
meta.assessment:
Labeling
Oral responses
meta.differentiation:
Support
Visual reference sheet
Extension - Students write one fact about fungi.

Fungi as Decomposers
Key Vocabulary
Decomposer; Decay; Recycle; Soil

Review food chain roles:
Producer
Consumer
Decomposer
Ask:
“Which role do mushrooms play?”
Explain:
Fungi break down dead plants and animals.
This process:
returns nutrients to soil
helps plants grow again
Draw simple diagram:

Ask & Discuss:
“What would happen if there were no decomposers?”
Connect to ecosystem balance.
Finish off:
“What is the fungi’s job in nature?”

meta.standards:
Understand the relationship between living things and their environment.
Know about the functions and effects of microorganisms.
meta.objectives:
To explain the role of fungi in ecosystems.
To identify fungi as decomposers.
meta.assessment: Exit responses

Support

ExtensionStudents draw a mini food chain including decomposers.

Explain:

Life cycle simplified:

Ask:
“Where might spores travel?”
Students can suggest wind, animals, water.

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:

Warm-Up: (whole group)
The teacher will begin the lesson by briefly reviewing the reading and comprehension skills that were learned in previous classes. This may include identifying characters, setting, plot, and understanding the sequence of events in a story.

Practice:
Reading and comprehension activity: The teacher will provide each group of students with a text that is appropriate for their reading level. This may be a short story, a chapter from a book, a poem, or an informational text. The students will read the text in their groups, discussing the characters, setting, plot, and main ideas. The teacher will circulate around the room, offering support and guidance as needed..  (Can also be done in Guided Reading)

Wrap Up:
The teacher will summarize the main points of the lesson, reinforcing the importance of reading comprehension and summarizing as essential skills for understanding a text. The teacher may review the different strategies for summarizing and how they help identify the main ideas of a text.

Warm-up (3 min)
Show 3-4 pictures (e.g., a cat under a chair, a book on a table). Ask:
Where is the object?
What word tells us its location?
Students turn & talk with a partner.
2. Practice (10 min)
Watch BrainPop Jr. Prepositions (first 3 minutes).
Read Behind the Couch (Seesaw) and highlight prepositions.
Activity: Classroom scavenger hunt
Call out prepositions (under, next to, behind, between) and have students place objects accordingly.
Example: Put your pencil case under your chair!
Have a few students describe where objects are using prepositions.

Writing:
Overall Goal: Students will finalize their chosen poems (ABAB, AABB, or Haiku), create neat and presentable copies, and rehearse for a final performance that showcases their understanding of different poetic forms.

Finalizing and Performing ALL Poems (ABAB/AABB/Haiku)

Session 1: Revising and Editing ALL Poems (30 minutes)

Activities:

(10 mins) Review Poetry Types:
Quickly review the key features of each poetry type:
ABAB: Rhyming first and third lines, second and fourth lines.
AABB: Rhyming first and second lines, third and fourth lines.
Haiku: 5-7-5 syllable structure, focus on nature.
(15 mins) Focused Peer Review:

Students get into small groups. Have them exchange drafts of their chosen poems.
Review should depend on what they're reading:
ABAB/AABB: Did they follow the right scheme, were their rhyme words correctly spelt? Did they use imagery and creativity?
Haiku: Did they follow the 5-7-5 structure? Did they use nature themes and creativity?
Provide simple checklists (adjust according to the type of poem being reviewed):
(Students to use these sheets to evaluate the poem being looked at:
ABAB and AABB Poem Evaluation Rubric.docx

Haiku - Poem Evaluation Rubric .docx

ABAB/AABB poems-
Does the poem follow the AABB or ABAB rhyme scheme? (Yes/No)
Are the rhymes clear and effective? (Yes/No)
Does the poem create a vivid picture? (Yes/No)
What do you like best about this poem?

Haiku-

Does the poem follow the 5-7-5 structure? (Yes/No)
Is this a poem about nature? (Yes/No)
Does the poem create a vivid picture? (Yes/No)
What do you like best about this poem?

(5 mins) Individual Revision:
Students use the feedback to revise their poems.

Guided Reading:

ComprehensionPrintablesIdoWedoYoudo.pdf

The teacher and students will practice reading comprehension strategies within small groups or as a whole group. The teacher will be sure to have students identify skills that help in the understanding of the text. (See Below)
Before Reading (Pre-Reading Strategies)
Activating Prior Knowledge – Connect the text to what students already know.
Predicting – Make predictions based on the title, pictures, or prior knowledge.
Previewing the Text – Skim headings, images, and captions to get an overview.
Setting a Purpose for Reading – Define why they are reading (e.g., to learn, to enjoy, to find information).
During Reading (Active Reading Strategies)
Asking Questions – Encourage “who, what, where, when, why, how” questions.
Visualizing – Create mental images of what is happening in the text.
Making Connections – Text-to-self, text-to-text, and text-to-world connections.
Summarizing – Identify key details and main ideas in a few sentences.
Using Context Clues – Decode unfamiliar words based on surrounding text.
Making Inferences – Read between the lines to understand hidden meanings.
Rereading for Clarity – Go back and read again to improve understanding.
Identifying Main Idea and Supporting Details – Find the central message and key facts.
Monitoring Comprehension – Stop and reflect on understanding; use "fix-up" strategies if confused.
After Reading (Post-Reading Strategies)
Summarizing and Retelling – Recount the story in their own words.
Drawing Conclusions – Use text evidence to form opinions or conclusions.
Answering Comprehension Questions – Respond to literal, inferential, and critical thinking questions.
Discussing and Sharing – Participate in partner or group discussions about the text.
Making Connections to Real Life – Relate the story to personal experiences.
Creating a Graphic Organizer – Use Venn diagrams, story maps, or flowcharts to organize ideas.
Writing a Response – Reflect through writing (e.g., journal entry, letter, or opinion piece).

Grammar:

Refer to Anchor Chart
Warm-up (3 min)
Ask: What time do you wake up? When do you eat lunch?
Model sentences:
I wake up at 6:30 AM.
We have recess after lunch.
Students turn & talk: Describe one daily routine.
2. Practice (10 min)
Watch YouTube: Prepositions .
Game: "Preposition Charades"
Teacher calls out a phrase (before school, during recess).
A student acts it out, and others guess the correct preposition.

Writing:
Session 2: Polishing Presentation (30 minutes)

(5 mins) Brainstorm Presentation Options:

Quickly brainstorm creative ways to present the poems:

Writing neatly on decorated paper.

Typing and printing with creative fonts/layouts.

Creating a small poster with artwork.

Writing on decorative cards.

(20 mins) Create Final Copies:

Students create their polished, final copies.

Encourage neatness, creativity, and consideration of the poem's theme.

(5 mins) Display and Admire:

Students will display their finished poems..

Everyone takes a moment to admire the varied presentations.

Guided Reading:
Review Previous Lesson

Grammar:
Warm-up (3 min)
Show images of movement (e.g., a dog jumping onto a bed). Ask:
What is happening in the picture?
What word shows movement?
2.Practice (10 min)
Watch BrainPop: Prepositions of Movement (first 3 minutes).
Activity: Mini obstacle course
Students take turns giving directions using prepositions (Walk through the chairs, jump onto the mat).
Sentence-building:
Students use a sentence starter (The cat jumped...) and complete it with a preposition of movement.
Preposition Relay:
Write prepositions on cards (under, beside, before, after, through, into).
A student picks a card and acts it out while others guess.
Picture Prompt Writing:
Show a picture (a dog under a table).
Students write a sentence describing it with a preposition.
Preposition Story Challenge:
Students write a 5-sentence mini-story using at least 3 prepositions.

Writing:

Session 3: Rehearsal and Performance Tips (30 minutes)

Objective: Students will rehearse their poem reading, focusing on clarity, expression, and connection with the audience.

Activities:

(10 mins) Performance Guidelines:

Discuss key elements of a good performance:

Clear pronunciation and volume

Expression and emotion (matching the poem's tone)

Eye contact (connecting with listeners)

Pacing (not too fast, not too slow)

Model effective reading techniques.

(15 mins) Rehearsal in Pairs:

Students practice reading their poems to a partner.

Partners provide feedback (did they speak clearly, did they express emotion, etc.).

(5 mins) Class Reflection:

Ask students what they found most challenging/rewarding about the rehearsal process. What are they excited about for the performance?

Explain:
Fungi reproduce using spores.
Life cycle simplified:

Ask:
“Where might spores travel?”
Students can suggest wind, animals, and water.

Gather evidence from a variety of sources.
Know about ways organisms reproduce.
meta.objectives:
To understand how fungi reproduce.
To identify stages in the fungus life cycle.
meta.assessment: Oral responses
meta.differentiation:
Support
Give starters to help with their responses
ExtensionStudents explain how spores travel.

meta.standards:
Reading Literature Standards (Theme, Structure, Point of View)
RL.5.2 – Determine a theme of a story, drama, or poem from details in the text, including how characters respond to challenges.
RL.5.5 – Explain how a series of chapters, scenes, or stanzas fits together to provide the overall structure of a text.
RL.5.6 – Describe how a narrator’s or speaker’s point of view influences how events are described.
Essential Elements (EE):
EE.RL.5.1 – Quote or reference details from the text to support understanding.
EE.RL.5.2 – Determine the theme or central idea of a text.
EE.RL.5.6 – Identify how a narrator or speaker describes events in a text.

Language Standards (Grammar, Conventions, Word Meaning)
L.5.1.F – Use conjunctions, prepositions, and interjections to form and expand sentences.
L.5.2.A – Use punctuation to separate items in a series.
Essential Elements (EE):
EE.L.5.1.F – Use simple conjunctions to connect words or phrases.
EE.L.5.2.A – Use punctuation (e.g., commas) in simple sentences.

Skill Alignment: Context Clues & Cause and Effect
Context Clues (Vocabulary in Context):
Supports RL.5.2 (understanding theme through word meaning)
Supports RL.5.6 (interpreting narrator’s language and perspective)
Reinforced through L.5.1.F and L.5.2.A when analyzing sentence structure and meaning
Cause and Effect (Text Structure & Understanding Events):
Strongly aligned to RL.5.5 (understanding how events are structured)
Supports RL.5.2 (how events lead to theme development)
Reinforced through EE.RL.5.1 (identifying details that show relationships between events)
meta.assessment:
Writing- Sorting activity.
Reading- Informal Assessment of questions asked and completed during guided reading.
Grammar-The ability to complete IXL and SeeSaw successfully
meta.differentiation:
Writing- Struggling students use picture clues to categorize story types.
Advanced students create a new example of a story type.
Reading- Students write one new word they learned and explain the context clue used.
Use picture clues or sentence frames to support understanding.
Grammar- Students work independently or in small groups to apply context clues to new words in their texts.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Introduce the day's activity (5mins) -  exploring facts related to the Sun, Moon, and Earth. Explain that they will be working in groups to decide which facts they think are true, false, or uncertain.
Group Activity (20 minutes)
Divide into Groups: Split the class into small groups of 3-4 students. Provide each group with a set of fact cards.
Categorizing Facts:
Instruct each group to sort their fact cards into three categories:
True: Facts they know are correct
False: Facts they believe are incorrect.
Uncertain: Facts they are unsure about.
Group Discussion:
Encourage groups to discuss and justify their reasoning for each category.
Ask questions to prompt deeper thinking, such as: "How do you know this fact is true?" or "What evidence do you have?"
Reflection (& Assessment) (5 minutes)
Discuss any surprises or new learnings that came up during the activity.
Conclusion and Next Steps (5 minutes)
Summarize Key Points: Recap what was learned about the Sun, Moon, and Earth.
Revisit List: Explain that the lists will be used throughout the unit to track learning and questions.

meta.standards: To decide which facts they think are true, false, or uncertain about the Sun, Moon and Earth.
meta.assessment:
Observe and note student participation and reasoning during group discussions.
Review the class lists to assess understanding and misconceptions.
meta.differentiation:
Support: Provide additional guidance and examples to students who may need help categorizing facts. Pair them with peers who have stronger understanding for peer support.
Challenge: Encourage advanced students to research and present additional facts or evidence for the facts they categorized.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Introduction (10 minutes)
Discussion Starter: Begin by asking the class, "Can anyone name all the planets in our solar system?" Write down their responses on the whiteboard, including Pluto, and mention that it is now considered a dwarf planet.

Engage Curiosity: Pose the following questions to the class:
Which of the planets do you think are the biggest?
Which do you think are the smallest?
Do you know which planets are closest to the Sun and the Earth?

Exploration Activity (20 minutes)
Step 1: Planet Size Comparison (10 minutes)
Visual Aid: Display images, videos or models of the planets in order of size.
Exploring Our Solar System: Planets and Space for Kids - FreeSchool

The Planet Song - 8 Planets of the Solar System Song

The Planets of our Solar System Song [UPDATE] (featuring The Hoover Jam)

Interactive Discussion:
Ask students to say which planet is the biggest and which is the smallest.
Reveal the largest (Jupiter) and the smallest (Mercury) planets.
Discuss the size of Earth in comparison to these planets.
Step 2: Planetary Positions and Movements (10 minutes)
Demonstration:
Use a flashlight as the Sun and the globe as Earth. Show how Earth revolves around the Sun.
Explain that all planets orbit the Sun in a similar way.
Activity:
Use solar system resource - Planets resources.jpeg
Have students form a circle representing the solar system. Assign each student a planet and have them walk around the "Sun," demonstrating their orbits.
Discuss which planets are closest to the Sun (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars) and Earth.
Conclusion and Discussion (5 minutes)
Recap Key Points:
Briefly summarize the size and order of the planets.
Highlight how planets move around the Sun.
Discussion Option:
Address any questions about Pluto, explaining its status as a dwarf planet and what that means.
Reflection:
Ask students what they found most interesting about the planets.
Simple Activity (5 minutes)
Creative Drawing:
Have students draw their favorite planet and write one fact they learned about it underneath.
(Share these drawings with the class if time permits.)

meta.standards: To learn about the planets in our solar system, understand their relative sizes, positions, and movements, and identify which are closest to the Sun and Earth.
meta.assessment:
Distribute a worksheet that asks students to list the planets in order from the closest to the farthest from the Sun.
Include questions about which planets are the largest and smallest.
meta.differentiation:
Support: Provide a simplified version of the planet cards with key information highlighted.
Challenge: For advanced students, include additional facts about each planet's atmosphere and surface conditions.

Goal: Explore story examples and discuss their character traits using the character traits planning sheets.
Story 1 Example: "Giraffes Can't Dance" by Giles Andreae
Link: Giraffes Can't Dance - Giles Andreae
How it contributes: This story features Gerald the giraffe, whose character development is central to the story. Students can explore:
Gerald's key challenge (he can't dance like other animals)
How Gerald is different from other animals
Gerald's relationship with the cricket who helps him
How Gerald's strengths emerge by the end of the story
Story 2: "Ruby Finds a Worry" by Tom Percival
Link: 💥Ruby Finds a Worry | Ruby's Worry (Read Aloud books for children) | Emotions
How it contributes: This story follows Ruby's journey dealing with a "worry" that grows bigger the more she ignores it. It's perfect for discussing:
The challenges Ruby faces with her worry
How Ruby's relationships help her overcome her worry
Ruby's character strengths
Important events that change Ruby throughout the story
Activity Ideas to Pair with Stories:
Character Map: After reading one story, have students fill out the question template from your PDF for the main character
Compare Characters: Compare two characters from different stories using a Venn diagram
Character Strengths: Identify and list the strengths of a favorite character and how those strengths helped them face challenges

Writing:  https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/unit-6-writing-fiction-writing-t2bti1vx405hel9e/wish/0BA3ZpzqKjozanPb
Goal: The second day focuses on story settings - the where and when of a story. Let's explore stories that feature distinctive settings that significantly influence the plot and characters.
Story Example 1: "The Snowy Day" by Ezra Jack Keats
Link: The Snowy Day Read Aloud
How it contributes: This classic story features a vivid winter urban setting that becomes an essential part of the story. The snow-covered city streets and buildings create opportunities for the main character Peter's adventures. Students can explore:
How the snowy setting enables specific plot events (making snow angels, snowball fights)
How the setting creates a particular mood
Why the setting isn't just a backdrop but an active element that shapes the story's possibilities
How the character interacts with the setting
Story Example 2: "Finding Nemo"
Link: Kids Book Read Aloud: Finding Nemo (Disney) ll bedtime stories ​ 📚(Alternative to "Saving Coral Reefs")
How it contributes: This book takes readers to the underwater world of coral reefs. Students can discuss:
How the unique underwater setting creates specific challenges for the characters
How this setting requires readers to imagine a world very different from their everyday experiences
How the distinctive setting creates specific moods (wonder, concern)
How the setting determines what can and cannot happen in the story
Story Example 3: "Last Stop on Market Street" by Matt de la Peña
Link: Last Stop on Market Street Read Aloud
How it contributes: This award-winning book follows a boy and his grandmother through an urban setting. Students can analyze:
How the city setting influences the characters' journey
The different mini-settings within the larger urban environment
How the setting reveals socioeconomic aspects of the characters' lives
How the main character's perspective on the setting changes throughout the story

Writing:  https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/unit-6-writing-fiction-writing-t2bti1vx405hel9e/wish/YDgnZezyNbDpWwrA

Goal: The third day focuses on how characters encounter and solve problems, a fundamental aspect of story structure.
Story Example 1: "Enemy Pie" by Derek Munson
Link: Enemy Pie Read Aloud
How it contributes: This story presents a clear problem: the main character has an "enemy" in his new neighbor. Students can explore:
How the main character's father offers a surprising solution - make an "enemy pie"
How the solution unexpectedly leads to friendship
The clear problem-solution structure central to narratives
How the resolution changes the character's perspective
Story Example 2: "The Oldest Student: How Mary Walker Learned to Read" by Rita Lorraine Hubbard
Link: The Oldest Student: How Mary Walker Learned to Read read-aloud
How it contributes: This true story follows Mary Walker, who learned to read at the age of 116 after facing lifelong obstacles to education. Students can analyze:
The clear problems (inability to read, limited opportunities) that Mary faces
How the character overcomes these challenges through determination and perseverance
How Mary uses her strengths to address complex problems
The emotional impact of the solution to a lifelong problem
Story Example 3: "After the Fall: How Humpty Dumpty Got Back Up Again" by Dan Santat
Link: After the Fall Read Aloud
How it contributes: This creative sequel to the classic nursery rhyme shows how Humpty Dumpty deals with his fear after his famous fall. Students can discuss:
The central problem (fear of heights) that develops after Humpty's accident
The steps Humpty takes to overcome his fear
How the character grows and changes through solving his problem
The metaphorical aspects of the story's problem-solution structure

records.cell_text:
🌌 SESSION 1:
The Amazing Solar System
Planet Pop Quiz (10 mins)
Ask: “Can you name all the planets?” 🪐
Display a simple Solar System poster or slide with all 8 planets - you can use your Solar System Box.
Brief discussion:
Which planet do you think is the biggest? Smallest?
Which is closest to the Sun? Which is closest to Earth?
Main Activity: Create a Solar System Scale Model (25 mins)

Planet Diameters and Model Scale Table.pdf
Instructions:
In groups, give students planet size data:

PlanetDiameter (km)
Mercury 4,879 ( 5000 Km - 0.5 cm)
Venus12,104 (12000 Km - 1.2cm)
Earth12,742 (13000 Km - 1.3cm)
Mars6,779 (7000 Km - 0.7cm)
Jupiter139,820 (140000 Km - 14cm)
Saturn116,460 (116000 Km - 11.6cm
Uranus50,724 (51000 Km - 5.1cm)
Neptune 49,244 (49000 Km - 4.9cm)
Scale (e.g., 1 cm = 10,000 km) and work on  cutting out circles for each planet.

Go outside to the playground or hallway, place the Sun (big ball or big yellow circle), and let students arrange their planets based on guessed distances.

Then, research actual distances from the Sun and rearrange using the scale:

Mercury – 58 million km (60 cm)
Venus – 108 million km (10.8 - 11cm)
Earth – 150 million km (15 cm)
Mars – 228 million km (22.8 - 23cm)
Jupiter – 778 million km (77.8 - 78cm)
Saturn – 1.4 billion km (140cm)
Uranus – 2.9 billion km (290cm)
Neptune – 4.5 billion km (450cm)

Reflect: Discuss the relative differences between the planets.

Extension Discussion:
What Happened to Pluto?
Ask: “Why do you think Pluto is no longer called a planet?”

Watch a short video like “Why Pluto Is Not a Planet” (YouTube Kids-friendly):
Why is Pluto not a planet?

Research task: What makes something a planet? What is a dwarf planet?
records.label_note: نشيدة الكواكب الشمسية 🌍🔥☀️راح تحفظها اذا سمعتها😉 The Planet Song - 8 Planets of the Solar System Song for Kids | KidsLearningTube The Planets Song - The Kiboomers Preschool Songs & Nursery Rhymes About The Solar System

meta.objectives: To understand the planets’ relative size and distance from the Sun and create a scale model of the solar system.
meta.assessment: Observation: Are you using the resources correctly (compasses [pin, paper strip & pin], rulers, scissors)?
meta.differentiation:
Support:
Give a simplified version of the data with only the planet name and one number to scale.

Challenge:
Ask higher-ability learners to calculate additional scales (e.g., 1 cm = 20,000 km).

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
SESSION 1: Does the Sun Move?
Begin with a "Sun Chase" chat (5–10 mins): Ask students, "Have you ever noticed how your shadow changes during the day? Why do you think that happens?" Play Crash Course Kids: Following the Sun (Ep. 8.2), which tracks how a lamppost shadow changes length and direction throughout the day and connects it directly to Earth's spin. Alternatively, Crash Course Kids: Earth's Rotation & Revolution (Ep. 8.1) gives the bigger picture of rotation and day/night. Discuss how light travels in straight lines and what happens when objects block it.
Next, lead the Shadow Investigation (30 mins): In small groups, have students plant a stick in the ground or observe a pre-existing shadow (like a swing set or playground equipment). Every 30 minutes, draw the shadow's outline, measure its length and direction, and note the time. If outdoors isn't possible, replicate the activity indoors using a flashlight moved in an arc to simulate the sun's apparent path — Science Friday has an excellent three-person version of this at sciencefriday.com/educational-resources/sunlight-and-shadows. PBS LearningMedia also offers an interactive globe model showing how shadows behave at different locations on Earth at pbslearningmedia.org — Observing Shadows on Earth.
Afterward, guide a reflection talk (10 mins): "Did the shadow move?" "Did it get longer or shorter?" "Why do you think that happened?" Connect to the key idea that the Earth spins on its axis, causing the Sun to appear to move across the sky — the Sun isn't going anywhere, we are!
Discussion prompts:
What would happen to shadows if the Sun didn't seem to move?
How might our daily routines change if the Earth stopped spinning?
Can you think of ways ancient people used shadows to tell time?
If you came back tomorrow at the same times, would the shadows be in the same places — why or why not?
For extended reading, assign from these free titles on Epic:
Me and My Shadow by Buffy Silverman — introduces what shadows are and how they form when objects block sunlight, perfect as a pre- or post-investigation read
Day and Night by Robin Nelson — a simple introduction to how Earth's rotation creates day and night, ideal for lower readers or ELL students
Does the Sun Sleep? by Martha E. H. Rustad — explores sun, moon, and star patterns with audio narration built in, great for reluctant readers
The Sun Is Kind of a Big Deal by Nick Seluk — available as an audiobook on Epic, a fun and humorous look at everything the Sun does for Earth, accessible for younger or ELL learners
You can also assign the full Sun and Shadows collection on Epic or the Day and Night — Earth's Rotation collection for students who want to explore further.
records.label_note: نشيدة الكواكب الشمسية 🌍🔥☀️راح تحفظها اذا سمعتها😉 The Planet Song - 8 Planets of the Solar System Song for Kids | KidsLearningTube The Planets Song - The Kiboomers Preschool Songs & Nursery Rhymes About The Solar System

meta.objectives: To know shadows change during the day and the Sun's position appears to move.
meta.assessment:
Data Collection: Are you recording observations accurately?
Discussion: Can you explain why shadows change length/direction?
meta.differentiation:
Support:
Pair students who need help with a peer to observe shadows together.

Challenge:
Ask more advanced learners to relate shadow movement to time of day or suggest improvements to their experiment.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
🌍 SESSION 3: Day, Night & Seasons

🟡 1. Quick Starter – What’s Happening to the Sun? (5 mins)
🧪 Intro Activity: Spin the Earth! (10 mins)
Use a globe and flashlight to show:
Day and night (spin the globe).

Marker on home country: Watch it go from light to dark.

📊 Main Activity: Sunrise & Sunset Investigation (25 mins)
Materials:
Sunrise/sunset data for June & December (home and other countries)
Graph paper or digital chart tool
Globe/map
Instructions:
Give each group data for different countries.
Plot longest and shortest days on a bar chart or line graph.
Locate countries on a globe – What do you notice?
Discussion prompts:
Are all places bright or dark at the same time?
Why do some places have longer summers or darker winters?
Extension task:
Research “Midnight Sun” and “Polar Night” (e.g., in Norway or Antarctica) and create a poster about life there!

🧠 Reflection (10 mins)
Ask:
What causes day and night?
What causes seasons?
What happens when the Earth spins? Tilts?
records.label_note: نشيدة الكواكب الشمسية 🌍🔥☀️راح تحفظها اذا سمعتها😉 The Planet Song - 8 Planets of the Solar System Song for Kids | KidsLearningTube The Planets Song - The Kiboomers Preschool Songs & Nursery Rhymes About The Solar System

meta.objectives: To understand how Earth’s movement causes day/night and seasons.
meta.assessment: Practical Demonstration: Observe participation in the globe/torch activity—do they understand how day/night works?Comparison and Reasoning: Can you explain seasonal differences between countries?
meta.differentiation:
Support:
Provide pre-labeled diagrams or sentence starters for children to explain day/night.
Use a simplified globe with only key countries marked.

Challenge:
Ask advanced students to research the "midnight sun" or polar night and explain why they happen.
Let them explore how Earth's tilt affects shadow length throughout the year.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
FOR SESSION 3’s MAIN ACTIVITY
General Information:
The sunrise and sunset times are influenced by the Earth's tilt on its axis and its orbit around the Sun. This results in varying day lengths throughout the year.
During the summer solstice (around June 20th or 21st in the Northern Hemisphere), the Northern Hemisphere experiences its longest day, while the Southern Hemisphere has its shortest day. The opposite occurs during the winter solstice (around December 21st or 22nd).
Locations closer to the Equator have less variation in day length throughout the year compared to places at higher latitudes.
Daylight Saving Time (DST) can also affect the observed sunrise and sunset times in some countries during certain periods. The data provided here does not account for DST unless specifically mentioned.
The times can vary by a few minutes depending on the exact longitude and latitude within a country or region.
This data should give you a good overview of the sunrise and sunset times in Qatar and a few other locations during June and December 2024. Remember that these are approximate times and can have minor variations based on the specific day and location.

Writing: Problem Solution with Characters

https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/unit-6-writing-fiction-writing-t2bti1vx405hel9e/wish/9kmlZVkGE7x0WpgV
Character Problem-Solution Analysis
These resources focus specifically on analyzing how characters solve problems through their goals, challenges, strengths, weaknesses, and what helps them overcome obstacles.
Story Example 1: "Salt in His Shoes" by Deloris Jordan and Roslyn M. Jordan
Link: Salt in his Shoes- Read Aloud
How it contributes: This story about young Michael Jordan's basketball journey directly addresses perseverance and overcoming challenges:
Character's Goal: Young Michael wants to grow taller and become a better basketball player
Challenges Faced: His short stature compared to other players, self-doubt, impatience
Weaknesses and Strengths: Weakness in size and confidence; strengths in natural talent and determination
Path to Achievement: Through his mother's wisdom, practice, and developing patience, Michael learns that perseverance and hard work matter more than physical height
Story Example 2: "The Dot" by Peter H. Reynolds
Link: 🟠 The Dot 📚 Kids Book Read Aloud Story
How it contributes: This story follows a girl who believes she can't draw but discovers her artistic ability:
Character's Goal: Initially none, then proving she can make art, and finally expressing herself
Challenges Faced: Self-doubt, lack of confidence, fear of failure
Weaknesses and Strengths: Weakness in self-belief; strength in willingness to try when encouraged
Path to Achievement: Through a teacher's guidance, starting with "just a dot," and gradually building confidence with each new creation
Story Example 3: "The Bad Seed" by Jory John
Link: The Bad Seed Read Aloud
How it contributes: This story follows a seed's journey from being "bad" to trying to be better:
Character's Goal: Initially none, then wanting to change and be better
Challenges Faced: Difficult past experiences, established negative identity, habits of bad behavior
Weaknesses and Strengths: Weakness in negative self-image; strength in self-awareness and desire to change
Path to Achievement: Through self-reflection, small daily decisions, and realistic expectations ("I'm still bad sometimes"), the seed makes gradual progress
Story Example 4: "The Most Magnificent Thing" by Ashley Spires
Link: 📐The Most Magnificent Thing
How it contributes: This story follows a girl attempting to build her great invention:
Character's Goal: To make "the most magnificent thing"
Challenges Faced: Repeated failures, frustration, anger, giving up temporarily
Weaknesses and Strengths: Weakness in handling frustration; strengths in creativity and persistence
Path to Achievement: Through taking a break, seeing her previous attempts with fresh eyes, and learning from failures to ultimately create something successful
These stories provide excellent examples of the character problem-solution cycle that third-grade students can analyze. Each story demonstrates how characters identify what they want, face challenges, work with their unique strengths and weaknesses, and find ways to overcome obstacles to achieve their goals.

Fiction Writing Lesson Plan - Day 4: Story Plan
The fourth day focuses on how stories are structured and planned, teaching students about plot development and narrative organization.
Story Example 1: "How to Catch a Star" by Oliver Jeffers
Link: How to Catch a Star Read Aloud
OR 📚 HOW TO CATCH A STAR - OLIVER JEFFERS
How it contributes: This story follows a boy's methodical attempts to catch a star, with each attempt building upon the previous one in a clear sequence. Students can explore:
The well-defined beginning (wanting a star), middle (multiple attempts to catch one), and end (finding a star in an unexpected way)
How the step-by-step progression makes the plot structure easy to identify
How authors plan their narratives with a logical sequence of events
How the character's goals drive the story forward
Story Example 2: "The Empty Pot" by Demi
Link: The Empty Pot Read Aloud
How it contributes: This Chinese folktale offers a clear, linear plot structure that's easy to analyze. Students can discuss:
The clear beginning (receiving seeds), middle (trying to grow flowers), and end (telling the truth)
The cause-and-effect relationships that move the story forward
How the story builds toward a meaningful conclusion
How the main character's actions and decisions influence the plot

Writing: Story Events
https://padlet.com/awsajprimary/unit-6-writing-fiction-writing-t2bti1vx405hel9e/wish/1xkVaqzAKMjRQl0e

These resources focus specifically on planning and sequencing events throughout a story's beginning, middle, and end structure.
Story Example 1: "A Bad Case of Stripes" by David Shannon
Link: A Bad Case of Stripes Read Aloud
How it contributes: This story has a clearly structured sequence of escalating events:
Beginning: Camilla Cream worries about what others think and refuses to eat lima beans; she wakes up covered in stripes
Middle: A series of increasingly bizarre transformations occur as doctors, specialists, and others try to cure her condition
End: An old woman helps Camilla admit she loves lima beans, which cures her condition
The events build logically, with each new attempt to cure Camilla creating a more complex situation until the final resolution
Story Example 2: "Sylvester and the Magic Pebble" by William Steig
Link: Sylvester and the Magic Pebble Read Aloud
How it contributes: This story has a classic event structure with clear cause-and-effect relationships:
Beginning: Sylvester finds a magic pebble and discovers its wish-granting powers
Middle: When frightened by a lion, Sylvester wishes to be a rock and becomes trapped in that form while his parents search for him
End: After a long period, his parents picnic on the rock that is Sylvester and place the magic pebble on him, allowing him to wish himself back to normal
The events demonstrate how a single choice can create complex consequences, and how the resolution requires specific circumstances
Story Example 3: "Those Shoes" by Maribeth Boelts
Link: 👟 Those Shoes 📚 Kids Book Read Aloud Story
How it contributes: This contemporary story follows a clear, relatable sequence of events:
Beginning: Jeremy wants expensive "those shoes" that everyone at school has, but his grandmother can't afford them
Middle: He searches thrift stores, finds a too-small pair, buys them anyway, tries to make them fit, and realizes they hurt too much to wear
End: Jeremy gives the shoes to a classmate who needs them even more than he does, and discovers the value of kindness and friendship
The events progress logically from desire to disappointment to discovery, with each event building emotional understanding
Story Example 4: "The Paperboy" by Dav Pilkey
Link: Read Aloud: The Paperboy by Dav Pilkey | Stories with Star
How it contributes: This story shows the planned sequence of a routine through clear chronological events:
Beginning: The paperboy wakes up before everyone else, even though it's difficult
Middle: He follows a specific sequence to prepare for his route (getting dressed, eating breakfast, folding papers, delivering papers)
End: He returns home, goes back to bed, and sleeps peacefully while everyone else wakes up
The events demonstrate how stories can be built around clear sequences and routines, with a circular structure that ends where it begins
These stories offer excellent examples of well-planned event sequences that third-grade students can analyze. Each story demonstrates how authors thoughtfully arrange events to create cohesive narratives with clear beginning, middle, and end structures.

Day 5: Story Dialogue and Event List
The fifth day focuses on developing effective dialogue between characters and creating a logical sequence of events in stories.
Story Example 1: "The Day the Crayons Quit" by Drew Daywalt
Link: The Day the Crayons Quit Read Aloud
How it contributes: This creative story features letters from different crayons to their owner, effectively serving as monologues that reveal each crayon's personality. Students can explore:
How each crayon has a distinct voice and way of speaking
How the "dialogue" (through letters) reveals character traits and feelings
The sequence of events as each crayon presents its case
How dialogue can move a story forward while revealing information about characters
Story Example 2: "The Recess Queen" by Alexis O'Neill
Link: The Recess Queen Read Aloud
How it contributes: This story about playground dynamics features clear dialogue between characters and a distinct sequence of events. Students can analyze:
How dialogue shows the personalities and relationships between characters
The difference between how Mean Jean and Katie Sue speak
The clear sequence of events from problem to resolution
How dialogue changes as characters develop throughout the story
Story Example 3: "Thank You, Mr. Falker" by Patricia Polacco
Link: Thank you, Mr Falker written by: Patricia Polacco - YouTube
How it contributes: This autobiographical story features meaningful conversations between the main character, her teacher, and other characters. Students can discuss:
How dialogue reveals emotions and relationships
The important conversations that mark turning points in the story
How to sequence the key events that show the character's journey
How dialogue can be used to show both conflict and support
These stories provide excellent examples of effective dialogue and clear event sequences that third-grade students can analyze and use as models for their own writing. The dialogue in these stories helps move the plot forward while revealing important information about the characters.
