records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
SESSION 1: Does the Sun Move?
Begin with a "Sun Chase" chat (5–10 mins): Ask students, "Have you ever noticed how your shadow changes during the day? Why do you think that happens?" Play Crash Course Kids: Following the Sun (Ep. 8.2), which tracks how a lamppost shadow changes length and direction throughout the day and connects it directly to Earth's spin. Alternatively, Crash Course Kids: Earth's Rotation & Revolution (Ep. 8.1) gives the bigger picture of rotation and day/night. Discuss how light travels in straight lines and what happens when objects block it.
Next, lead the Shadow Investigation (30 mins): In small groups, have students plant a stick in the ground or observe a pre-existing shadow (like a swing set or playground equipment). Every 30 minutes, draw the shadow's outline, measure its length and direction, and note the time. If outdoors isn't possible, replicate the activity indoors using a flashlight moved in an arc to simulate the sun's apparent path — Science Friday has an excellent three-person version of this at sciencefriday.com/educational-resources/sunlight-and-shadows. PBS LearningMedia also offers an interactive globe model showing how shadows behave at different locations on Earth at pbslearningmedia.org — Observing Shadows on Earth.
Afterward, guide a reflection talk (10 mins): "Did the shadow move?" "Did it get longer or shorter?" "Why do you think that happened?" Connect to the key idea that the Earth spins on its axis, causing the Sun to appear to move across the sky — the Sun isn't going anywhere, we are!
Discussion prompts:
What would happen to shadows if the Sun didn't seem to move?
How might our daily routines change if the Earth stopped spinning?
Can you think of ways ancient people used shadows to tell time?
If you came back tomorrow at the same times, would the shadows be in the same places — why or why not?
For extended reading, assign from these free titles on Epic:
Me and My Shadow by Buffy Silverman — introduces what shadows are and how they form when objects block sunlight, perfect as a pre- or post-investigation read
Day and Night by Robin Nelson — a simple introduction to how Earth's rotation creates day and night, ideal for lower readers or ELL students
Does the Sun Sleep? by Martha E. H. Rustad — explores sun, moon, and star patterns with audio narration built in, great for reluctant readers
The Sun Is Kind of a Big Deal by Nick Seluk — available as an audiobook on Epic, a fun and humorous look at everything the Sun does for Earth, accessible for younger or ELL learners
You can also assign the full Sun and Shadows collection on Epic or the Day and Night — Earth's Rotation collection for students who want to explore further.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
(moved from previous week)
🌍 SESSION 2: Day, Night & Seasons

🟡 1. Quick Starter – What’s Happening to the Sun? (5 mins)
🧪 Intro Activity: Spin the Earth! (10 mins)
Use a globe and flashlight to show:
Day and night (spin the globe).

Marker on home country: Watch it go from light to dark.
Ask students, "Why is it dark at night? Does the Sun disappear, or does something else happen?" Play Crash Course Kids: Earth's Rotation & Revolution (Ep. 8.1), which explains how rotation creates day and night and how Earth's tilt gives us seasons — all in under five minutes with engaging animations.
Next, lead the "Spin the Earth!" intro activity (10 mins): Using a globe and a flashlight, demonstrate day and night by slowly spinning the globe while the flashlight stays still. Place a small sticker on Qatar (or your students' home countries) and have them watch it move from light to dark. Then tilt the globe to show how Earth's 23.5° axis means different parts of the planet receive more or less direct sunlight at different times of year — this is what causes seasons, not distance from the Sun. NASA Space Place has an excellent child-friendly explanation of this common misconception at spaceplace.nasa.gov/seasons. For a video companion, Crash Course Kids: Seasons and the Sun (Ep. 11.1) walks through exactly how the tilt creates summer and winter in each hemisphere.
Then move into the Sunrise & Sunset Investigation (25 mins): Give each group sunrise and sunset data for their assigned country in both June and December (you can pull real data from timeanddate.com/sun). Have them calculate the length of the longest and shortest days, then plot these on a bar chart. Groups locate their countries on the globe and compare: What patterns do you notice? Are all places bright or dark at the same time? Why do some places have much longer summers or darker winters? Which countries are closest to the equator, and how does that affect their day length?
For an extension task, have students research the "Midnight Sun" and "Polar Night" phenomena — places like Tromsø, Norway or Antarctica where the Sun doesn't set for weeks or doesn't rise at all. Students can create a poster or short presentation about what daily life is like in these places. PBS's Science Trek: The Earth — A Tilt for All Seasons is a helpful short video for this.
Finish with a reflection talk (10 mins): "What causes day and night? What causes seasons? What would happen if the Earth wasn't tilted at all?"
Discussion prompts:
If you could live anywhere on Earth, would you choose a place with very long summer days or equal day and night all year? Why?
Why do you think Qatar doesn't experience big seasonal changes the way countries like the UK or Canada do?
If the Earth's tilt suddenly changed to 0°, what would happen to the seasons?
How might animals and plants in the Arctic cope with months of darkness?
For extended reading, assign from these free titles on Epic:
The Reasons for Seasons by Gail Gibbons — covers solstices, equinoxes, and how Earth's tilt creates the seasons, with clear diagrams suited to upper primary
Earth's Seasons and Cycles by Christine Dugan — explains Earth's orbit, rotation, and seasonal cycles
Day and Night by Robin Nelson — a simple introduction to how the Sun and Earth's rotation create day and night, ideal for lower readers
Me and My Shadow by Buffy Silverman — introduces shadows in a fun, accessible way that connects well to Session 1
You can also assign the full Sun, Earth, and the Seasons collection on Epic or the Day and Night — Earth's Rotation collection for students who want to explore further.

meta.standards:
Reading Literature (RL)
RL.5.4 – Determine the meaning of words and phrases as they are used in a text, including figurative language such as metaphors and similes. RL.5.9 – Compare and contrast stories in the same genre on their approaches to similar themes and topics.
EE.RL.5.4 – Determine the meaning of words and phrases in a literary text. EE.RL.5.9 – Compare characters, settings, or events in stories.
Reading Informational (RI)
RI.5.4 – Determine the meaning of general academic and domain-specific words and phrases in a text. RI.5.9 – Integrate information from several texts on the same topic.
EE.RI.5.4 – Determine the meaning of words and phrases in an informational text. EE.RI.5.9 – Compare details from two texts on the same topic.
Language (L)
L.5.3.A – Expand, combine, and reduce sentences for meaning, reader interest, and style. L.5.5.A – Interpret figurative language, including similes and metaphors, in context. L.5.5.B – Recognize and explain the meaning of common idioms, adages, and proverbs.
EE.L.5.5 – Demonstrate understanding of word relationships and figurative language
Writing (W) – Narrative Writing
W.5.3 – Write narratives to develop real or imagined experiences or events using effective technique, descriptive details, and clear event sequences. W.5.3.A – Orient the reader by establishing a situation and introducing a narrator and/or characters. W.5.3.B – Use narrative techniques, such as dialogue, description, and pacing. W.5.3.C – Use a variety of transitional words, phrases, and clauses. W.5.3.D – Use concrete words and phrases and sensory details to convey experiences and events precisely. W.5.3.E – Provide a conclusion that follows from the narrated experiences or events.
EE.W.5.3 – Write about events or personal experiences using details.

W.5.3.A – Orient the reader by establishing a situation and introducing a narrator and/or characters. W.5.3.B – Use narrative techniques, such as dialogue, description, and pacing. W.5.3.C – Use a variety of transitional words, phrases, and clauses. W.5.3.D – Use concrete words and phrases and sensory details to convey experiences and events precisely. W.5.3.E – Provide a conclusion that follows from the narrated experiences or events.
EE.W.5.3 – Write about events or personal experiences using details.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Recap on ideas covered from previous week and consolidate on seasons -
Watch: Earth's Tilt 1: The Reason for the Seasons

Finish off elements of session 2 from previous week that may not have been completed due to other things happening alongside preparing for MAP testing:

 Day, Night & Seasons

📊 Main Activity: Sunrise & Sunset Investigation (25 mins)
Materials:
Sunrise/sunset data for June & December (home and other countries)
Graph paper or digital chart tool
Globe/map
Instructions:
Give data for different countries.
Plot longest and shortest days on a bar chart or line graph.
Locate countries on a globe – What do you notice?
Discussion prompts:
Are all places bright or dark at the same time?
Why do some places have longer summers or darker winters?
Extension task:
Research “Midnight Sun” and “Polar Night” (e.g., in Norway or Antarctica) and create a poster about life there!

🧠 Reflection (10 mins)
Ask:
What causes day and night?
What causes seasons?
What happens when the Earth spins? Tilts?
The results show longer days in June for the Northern Hemisphere locations (Doha, London, NYC, Tokyo) and shorter days in December. The opposite is true for Sydney in the Southern Hemisphere. This variation is expected due to the Earth's tilt.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Light Detectives: How Does Light Travel?

Materials
For each group (approx. 4-5 students):
1 Light source (e.g., flashlight/torch – ensure batteries are working)
3-4 Small, safe mirrors (plastic or handheld cosmetic mirrors)
A small collection of objects:
Opaque: e.g., wooden block, thick cardboard shape, book
Translucent: e.g., piece of coloured plastic, thin tissue paper, maybe tracing paper
1 piece of White card or paper (as a target screen)
A room that can be partially or fully darkened.
(5 mins)
Thinking About Light
We know light helps us see. But how does light get from a lamp (point) or the Sun all the way to our eyes?"
"Remember talking about shadows? What makes a shadow?" (Something blocking the light). "Does this give us a clue about how light travels? If something blocks it, maybe light travels..." (Guide towards "straight").
Posing the Puzzle: "So, light seems to travel straight. But can we make light bend or turn corners? What do you think?" (Accept initial ideas). "Today, we're going to be Light Detectives and investigate!"
(20 mins)
 Explore & Explain: Light Maze Challenge
Group Up & Materials: Divide class into pre-arranged groups. Distribute the light source, mirrors, objects, and white card to each group.
The Challenge: "Here's your mission, Light Detectives! Place your white card somewhere nearby. Your challenge is to get the light from your flashlight to shine onto the white card, BUT you cannot point the flashlight directly at the card! You MUST use your mirrors and maybe the other objects to guide the light beam."
Safety First: Be gentle with the mirrors."
Darken the Room: Dim the lights as much as practical to make the light beams visible.
Investigation Time: Allow groups 10-15 minutes to experiment. Circulate and ask guiding questions:
"What happens when the light hits the mirror?" (It bounces off / changes direction). Introduce the word reflect.
"Can you make the light hit the card using just one mirror?"
"Now try using two mirrors. Can you make the light turn a corner?"
"What happens when the light beam hits the wooden block? Does it go through?" (No, it's blocked). Introduce opaque. "What do you see behind the block?" (A shadow).
"What happens when the light hits the coloured plastic/tissue paper?" (Some light goes through, it might be coloured/dimmer). Do introduce the word translucent.
"Does the light beam look as bright after bouncing off many mirrors?" (Maybe not).
Bringing it Together (Mid-Experiment Pause if needed): Briefly get class attention. "What are you using to make the light change direction?" (Mirrors!). "Is the light bending by itself, or is it bouncing off the mirrors?" (Bouncing/Reflecting).
(10 mins)
Sharing Findings & Space Connection
Light Back On/Gather Attention: Bring the lights back up slightly. Have groups stop experimenting and focus.
Feedback Discussion (using prompt questions):
"How were you able to change the way the light travelled?" (Using mirrors to reflect it).
"Could you make it bend around corners?" (Yes, by reflecting it!). Reinforce: Light travels straight until it hits something.
"Was the type of material important?" (Yes! Mirrors reflect, opaque blocks, translucent lets some through).
"Did you see shadows? What shape were they?" (Yes, same shape as the opaque object).
"Did the light beam change?" (Maybe got dimmer/weaker after reflecting many times or going further).
Connecting to the Moon: Show the image of the Moon. "Think about our Moon. Does it make its own light like the Sun or a lamp?" (No). "So how can we see it shining so brightly at night?"
The BIG Reveal: Show the Sun -> Moon -> Earth diagram. "It's just like your experiment! The Sun is the light source. Light travels in a straight line from the Sun, hits the Moon, and then reflects (bounces) off the Moon in straight lines towards our eyes on Earth! We see the Moon because it's reflecting sunlight."
Other Space Objects: "Planets and even comets that we see also shine because they are reflecting sunlight, just like the Moon!"
(5 mins)
Evaluate: Checking Understanding/Recap: "So, light travels in ______ lines. Mirrors ______ light. Opaque objects ______ light and make shadows. We see the Moon because it ______ sunlight." (Fill in blanks: straight, reflect, block, reflects).

To be able to::
State that light appears to travel in straight lines.
Demonstrate that mirrors can change the direction of light (reflection).
Distinguish between opaque objects (which block light and create shadows) and translucent objects (which let some light through).
Explain that we see the Moon because it reflects sunlight.
meta.assessment: Activity: Thumbs up/down: "Light travels straight?" (Up). "Mirrors bend light?" (Down - they reflect). "We see the Moon because it makes light?" (Down). "We can see the Moon because it reflects light" (Up).
meta.differentiation:

Provide diagrams showing simple one-mirror reflections as a guide.
Focus vocabulary on "bounce," "block," "goes through."

Challenge:
Challenge groups to hit the target using three mirrors.

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:/Grammar:
Warm-up (5 min): "What's the Word?" – Display a sentence with a missing word (e.g., “The girl was feeling ______ because she lost her toy.”) Students guess the missing word using clues in the sentence.
Main Lesson (15-20 min):
I Do (Teacher Model): Use a short paragraph with unfamiliar words and model how to identify meaning through context clues (definition, synonym, antonym, example).
We Do (Guided Practice): Read a new paragraph as a class. Pause to identify unfamiliar words and clues around them. Use a graphic organizer to track word, clues, and meaning.
You Do (Independent Practice): Students read a short text and underline two words they don’t know. Then write what they think the words mean using clues.

Writing: Day 1: Introduction to Fiction and Creating Characters
Description: Students will begin by exploring what fiction is, using the "Fiction Writing Overview" resource to discuss the elements of fiction and its purpose. The class will talk about what makes a story engaging, focusing on characters as a central element. Using the "Create a Character" worksheet, students will brainstorm and design their own fictional characters, considering traits, motivations, and interests. By the end of the lesson, each student will have a main character ready for their story.

W.5.3.A – Orient the reader by establishing a situation and introducing a narrator and/or characters. W.5.3.B – Use narrative techniques, such as dialogue, description, and pacing. W.5.3.C – Use a variety of transitional words, phrases, and clauses. W.5.3.D – Use concrete words and phrases and sensory details to convey experiences and events precisely. W.5.3.E – Provide a conclusion that follows from the narrated experiences or events.
EE.W.5.3 – Write about events or personal experiences using details.
meta.assessment: ☑️ Informal Assessment: Observe responses and reasoning.
meta.differentiation:
🔹ELLs/Struggling Readers: Provide texts with simpler vocabulary and visuals.
🔹Advanced: Challenge them with higher-level texts and ask them to create sentences using the new vocabulary.

Guided Reading:/Grammar:
Warm-up (5 min): “Word Hunt” – Give a simple word (e.g., “happy”). Ask students to brainstorm as many synonyms as they can in 1 minute.
Main Lesson (15-20 min):
I Do: Model how to use a dictionary to find the meaning, part of speech, and pronunciation. Then show how to use a thesaurus to find synonyms and antonyms.
We Do: In pairs, use dictionaries/thesauruses (online or print) to look up 2 target words.
You Do: Students receive 3 unfamiliar words from a short reading passage and find meanings/synonyms using tools. Create a mini word map.

ghost
gnawed
climber
wreath

island
scent
wrench
judge
fasten
wrist
doubt
knock
answer
knelt
sign

Writing Day 2: Setting the Scene
Description: Building on their character work, students will dive into the importance of setting in fiction. With guidance from the "Brainstorming Settings" worksheet, they will brainstorm and select where and when their stories will take place. Students will describe the places, times, and environments that will shape their stories, using sensory details to make their settings vivid. By the end of class, students will have a character and a setting, forming the foundation for their stories.

RI.5.4 – Determine the meaning of general academic and domain-specific words and phrases in a text. RI.5.9 – Integrate information from several texts on the same topic.
EE.RI.5.4 – Determine the meaning of words and phrases in an informational text. EE.RI.5.9 – Compare details from two texts on the same topic.

meta.assessment: Quick check: Ask, “What is one new word you found and a synonym or antonym for it?”
meta.differentiation:
🔹ELLs: Use illustrated dictionaries or bilingual tools.
🔹Advanced: Ask students to write a paragraph using the new synonyms.

records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:/Grammar:
Warm-up (5 min): Ask: “Have you ever read a story that reminded you of your own life? What was it?”
Main Lesson (15-20 min):
I Do: Read a short story (e.g., an excerpt from Because of Winn-Dixie) and model a text-to-self and a text-to-text connection.
We Do: Students listen to a second story or excerpt. As a class, discuss similarities between this story and another known text or personal experiences.
You Do: Students complete a graphic organizer with two types of connections: one to self, one to another book or character.

Writing: Day 3: Introducing the Story Problem and Planning
Description: Today, students will focus on the heart of any good story: the problem or challenge the main character faces. Using the "Planning Out the Problem" and "Goals and Challenges" sheets, students will determine what their character wants and what obstacles stand in the way. The class will discuss how problems drive stories forward and brainstorm possible solutions. Students will then begin laying out the basic structure of their story, using the "Fiction Story Plan" to organize their ideas.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
Guided Reading:/Grammar:
Warm-up (5 min): Watch a short news clip or read a current event paragraph. Ask, “What is this similar to in real life?”
Main Lesson (15-20 min):
I Do: Model how a story or article connects to a real-world event or issue. Use a Venn diagram to compare the story and the event.
We Do: Read a nonfiction excerpt or informational text. Guide students in identifying a connection to a world issue (e.g., environment, kindness, fairness).
You Do: Students write or draw a connection between the reading and something they’ve seen in the news or in real life. Also choose 3 new vocabulary words from the week and use them in a short paragraph.

scent
wrench

fasten
wrist
doubt

knelt

Writing: Day 4: Mapping Story Events and Beginning to Write
Description: With characters, settings, and problems in place, students will map out the sequence of events in their stories using the "Story Events List" resource. They’ll outline what happens at the beginning, middle, and end, ensuring their stories have a clear progression. Students will use this plan to start drafting the opening of their fiction stories, focusing on introducing their character, setting, and initial problem. By the end of the lesson, students will have a strong start on their first drafts and a plan to continue writing.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 1: Introduction to Percents

meta.standards:
Lesson Objective: Students will understand the meaning of percent as "per hundred," identify percents in real-life contexts, and represent percents using models and grids.
Common Core Standards:
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NBT.A.3: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.B.7: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 2: Connecting Percents and Decimals

meta.standards:
Lesson Objective: Students will convert between percents and decimals, and represent both using models.
Common Core Standards:
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NBT.A.3: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.A.1: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 3: Converting Percents and Fractions

meta.standards:
Lesson Objective: Students will convert between percents and fractions, including simplifying fractions and representing them visually.
Common Core Standards:
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.A.1: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.A.2: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 4: Ratios, Rates, and Percents in Context

meta.standards:
Lesson Objective: Students will compare and order fractions, decimals, and percents, and solve real-world problems involving ratios and rates.
Common Core Standards:
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.A.2: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.B.7: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 5: IXL Fractions, Decimals, Percents Mastery Check

meta.standards:
Lesson Objective: Students will demonstrate their understanding of converting and comparing fractions, decimals, and percents through an interactive IXL assessment.
Common Core Standards:
Review of all above standards as applied in IXL activities.
Activities:
Students complete assigned IXL skills for formative assessment and feedback.

records.cell_text:
Writing:
Writing Day 1 – Drafting the Beginning and Middle
Description: Students will begin writing the first draft of their stories, using their planning sheets for guidance. Today, the focus is on crafting a strong beginning that introduces the main character, the setting, and the story problem. Students will continue writing into the middle of their stories, developing the plot and showing how their characters respond to challenges. Encourage students to use action, dialogue, and description to bring their stories to life. Remind them not to worry about mistakes—today is all about getting ideas onto the page.

Guided Reading:
NWEA Review- https://jeopardylabs.com/play/nwea-prep-25
Readers Theater: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1x4ciYwdxpQBC-h_2Ik0U2OMwluPIL9egvFrtS6P4Na0/edit?usp=drive_link
Space Family: https://drive.google.com/file/d/19CzvnbQFOKMV3EXEiz7yUM51BC933t52/view?usp=drive_link
Walking Under the Stars: https://drive.google.com/file/d/12BHpKdvzTEqWno-KmF4pVnrLWF_zAc__/view?usp=drive_link

Warm-up: Watch a 1-minute Reader’s Theater clip. What makes it fun to watch?
Lesson:
Distribute scripts, assign roles, and begin reading in groups.
Focus on reading accuracy and voice variation.
Grammar:
Checklist: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1M2UPv2hjRYJUqtJ_wJZAEnh2VIZyjNEDyojV2xbCotI/edit?tab=t.0

Warm-up: Look at 2 sample flyers. Identify strong grammar and clear writing vs. mistakes.
Lesson:
Review key grammar points (punctuation, capitalization, sentence structure).
Brainstorm: What details does a great advertisement need? (Title, date, time, characters, call to action)
Students begin drafting their flyers in Canva.
🎭 Canva Flyer Template for Reader’s Theater
To create an engaging flyer for your Reader’s Theater performance, you can use Canva's customizable templates.
Steps to Access and Customize the Template:
Visit Canva's School Flyer Templates: Navigate to Canva's School Flyer Templates.Canva+7Canva+7Canva+7
Choose a Template: Browse through the available templates and select one that suits your event's theme.
Customize the Template: Click on the chosen template to open it in the Canva editor. Here, you can modify the text, colors, images, and other elements to fit your Reader’s Theater performance details.
Suggested Elements to Include:
Title: "Bringing Stories to Life: A Reader’s Theater Performance"
Date & Time: Specify when the performance will take place.
Location: Provide the venue details.
Cast List: Include names or roles of the performers.
Audience: Mention that the performance is for Pre-K to Grade 2 students.
Call to Action: Encourage attendance with phrases like "Join us for a magical storytelling experience!
Design Tips:
Visual Appeal: Use bright colors and playful fonts to attract the young audience.
Images: Incorporate illustrations or photos related to the stories being performed.
Clarity: Ensure all text is legible and information is easy to find.

Writing:

Writing Day 2 – Drafting the Middle and End

Description:
Students will continue and complete their first drafts, focusing on the middle and end of their stories. They should show how their character faces obstacles and works toward a solution, leading to a satisfying ending. Encourage students to check their story plan as they write to make sure they include all key events. Remind them to keep the action moving, add dialogue where it feels natural, and give their story a clear resolution.

Peer review grammar and clarity using a checklist.

Writing:

Revising Day 1 – Big Changes and Story Structure

Description:
Today, students will read through their first drafts with a focus on making “big changes” to improve the overall story. Using the "Revise and Reflect" worksheet and the "Story Elements Checklist," they’ll look for missing or unclear parts, rewrite sections for better flow, and strengthen character actions or dialogue. Peer or teacher conferences can help students identify areas for revision, such as unclear events, flat characters, or weak endings. Students will then begin rewriting to address these major areas.

Writing:

Revising Day 2 – Editing for Details and Final Touches
Description:
In this final writing session, students will focus on “small changes”—editing and polishing their stories. With the help of the "Story Elements Checklist," students will check for grammar, spelling, punctuation, and descriptive details. Encourage students to read their stories aloud to catch errors and awkward sentences. They’ll also add finishing touches, such as a creative title and, if time allows, a simple illustration. End with students sharing their polished stories in small groups or with the class, celebrating their growth as writers.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 1: Introduction to Percents

meta.objectives:
Lesson Objective: Students will understand the meaning of percent as "per hundred," identify percents in real-life contexts, and represent percents using models and grids.
Common Core Standards:
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NBT.A.3: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.B.7: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 2: Connecting Percents and Decimals

meta.objectives:
Lesson Objective: Students will convert between percents and decimals, and represent both using models.
Common Core Standards:
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NBT.A.3: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.A.1: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 3: Converting Percents and Fractions

meta.objectives:
Lesson Objective: Students will convert between percents and fractions, including simplifying fractions and representing them visually.
Common Core Standards:
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.A.1: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.A.2: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 4: Ratios, Rates, and Percents in Context

meta.objectives:
Lesson Objective: Students will compare and order fractions, decimals, and percents, and solve real-world problems involving ratios and rates.
Common Core Standards:
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.A.2: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions.
CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.5.NF.B.7: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
meta.differentiation: Each lesson includes opportunities for partner practice, common misperceptions, independent practice, and a challenge questions for differentiation.

records.subject: math
records.cell_text: Day 5: IXL Fractions, Decimals, Percents Mastery Check

meta.objectives:
Lesson Objective: Students will demonstrate their understanding of converting and comparing fractions, decimals, and percents through an interactive IXL assessment.
Common Core Standards:
Review of all above standards as applied in IXL activities.
Activities:
Students complete assigned IXL skills for formative assessment and feedback.

records.subject: reading
records.cell_text:
✏️ IXL Grade 5 Language Skills (with Skill Codes)
🔤 1. Capitalization & Punctuation
L.1 – Capitalize titles
L.2 – Capitalize proper adjectives
L.4 – Commas with a series
L.5 – Commas with introductory elements
L.6 – Commas with coordinate adjectives
L.7 – Use quotation marks correctly

🧠 2. Sentence Structure
L.9 – Identify complete sentences, fragments, and run-ons
L.10 – Combine sentences
L.11 – Use coordinating conjunctions

🔄 3. Verbs & Tense
L.12 – Form and use verb tenses
L.13 – Correct inappropriate shifts in verb tense

👤 4. Pronouns
L.14 – Use subject and object pronouns
L.15 – Pronoun-antecedent agreement

📝 5. Adjectives & Adverbs
L.16 – Compare adjectives and adverbs
L.17 – Choose between adjectives and adverbs

📍 6. Prepositions
L.18 – Identify prepositional phrases

📚 7. Vocabulary in Context (HIGH PRIORITY)
L.19 – Use context clues
L.20 – Determine meaning of idioms
L.21 – Use Greek and Latin roots
L.22 – Choose the best word for the sentence
L.23 – Synonyms and antonyms

🔤 8. Word Study
L.24 – Spell commonly confused words
L.25 – Identify homophones
L.26 – Use prefixes and suffixes

✍🏽 9. Editing & Revising (MAP-STYLE)
L.27 – Correct errors in capitalization and punctuation
L.28 – Revise sentences for clarity
L.29 – Choose the best way to rewrite a sentence
L.30 – Edit passages for grammar and usage

🎯 How I’d Use This (quick and effective)
For your MAP push:
Assign 2 skills per day
1 from Grammar (L.1–L.18)
1 from Vocabulary/Editing (L.19–L.30)
Focus heavy on:
L.9 (sentence types)
L.13 (verb tense shifts)
L.19 (context clues)
L.27–L.30 (editing)

Writing:
Writing Day 1 – Drafting the Beginning and Middle
Description: Students will begin writing the first draft of their stories, using their planning sheets for guidance. Today, the focus is on crafting a strong beginning that introduces the main character, the setting, and the story problem. Students will continue writing into the middle of their stories, developing the plot and showing how their characters respond to challenges. Encourage students to use action, dialogue, and description to bring their stories to life. Remind them not to worry about mistakes—today is all about getting ideas onto the page.

Focus on reading accuracy and voice variation.

Warm-up: Look at 2 sample flyers. Identify strong grammar and clear writing vs. mistakes.
Lesson:
Review key grammar points (punctuation, capitalization, sentence structure).
Brainstorm: What details does a great advertisement need? (Title, date, time, characters, call to action)
Students begin drafting their flyers in Canva.
🎭 Canva Flyer Template for Reader’s Theater
To create an engaging flyer for your Reader’s Theater performance, you can use Canva's customizable templates.
Steps to Access and Customize the Template:
Visit Canva's School Flyer Templates: Navigate to Canva's School Flyer Templates.Canva+7Canva+7Canva+7
Choose a Template: Browse through the available templates and select one that suits your event's theme.
Customize the Template: Click on the chosen template to open it in the Canva editor. Here, you can modify the text, colors, images, and other elements to fit your Reader’s Theater performance details.
Suggested Elements to Include:
Title: "Bringing Stories to Life: A Reader’s Theater Performance"
Date & Time: Specify when the performance will take place.
Location: Provide the venue details.
Cast List: Include names or roles of the performers.
Audience: Mention that the performance is for Pre-K to Grade 2 students.
Call to Action: Encourage attendance with phrases like "Join us for a magical storytelling experience!
Design Tips:
Visual Appeal: Use bright colors and playful fonts to attract the young audience.
Images: Incorporate illustrations or photos related to the stories being performed.
Clarity: Ensure all text is legible and information is easy to find.

Writing:

Writing Day 2 – Drafting the Middle and End

Description:
Students will continue and complete their first drafts, focusing on the middle and end of their stories. They should show how their character faces obstacles and works toward a solution, leading to a satisfying ending. Encourage students to check their story plan as they write to make sure they include all key events. Remind them to keep the action moving, add dialogue where it feels natural, and give their story a clear resolution.

Writing:

Revising Day 1 – Big Changes and Story Structure

Description:
Today, students will read through their first drafts with a focus on making “big changes” to improve the overall story. Using the "Revise and Reflect" worksheet and the "Story Elements Checklist," they’ll look for missing or unclear parts, rewrite sections for better flow, and strengthen character actions or dialogue. Peer or teacher conferences can help students identify areas for revision, such as unclear events, flat characters, or weak endings. Students will then begin rewriting to address these major areas.

Writing:

Revising Day 2 – Editing for Details and Final Touches
Description:
In this final writing session, students will focus on “small changes”—editing and polishing their stories. With the help of the "Story Elements Checklist," students will check for grammar, spelling, punctuation, and descriptive details. Encourage students to read their stories aloud to catch errors and awkward sentences. They’ll also add finishing touches, such as a creative title and, if time allows, a simple illustration. End with students sharing their polished stories in small groups or with the class, celebrating their growth as writers.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
(brought from previous week - CATCH UP)

Light Detectives: How Does Light Travel?

Materials
For each group (approx. 4-5 students):
1 Light source (e.g., flashlight/torch – ensure batteries are working)
3-4 Small, safe mirrors (plastic or handheld cosmetic mirrors)
A small collection of objects:
Opaque: e.g., wooden block, thick cardboard shape, book
Translucent: e.g., piece of coloured plastic, thin tissue paper, maybe tracing paper
1 piece of White card or paper (as a target screen)
A room that can be partially or fully darkened.
(5 mins)
Thinking About Light
We know light helps us see. But how does light get from a lamp (point) or the Sun all the way to our eyes?"
"Remember talking about shadows? What makes a shadow?" (Something blocking the light). "Does this give us a clue about how light travels? If something blocks it, maybe light travels..." (Guide towards "straight").
Posing the Puzzle: "So, light seems to travel straight. But can we make light bend or turn corners? What do you think?" (Accept initial ideas). "Today, we're going to be Light Detectives and investigate!"
(20 mins)
 Explore & Explain: Light Maze Challenge
Group Up & Materials: Divide class into pre-arranged groups. Distribute the light source, mirrors, objects, and white card to each group.
The Challenge: "Here's your mission, Light Detectives! Place your white card somewhere nearby. Your challenge is to get the light from your flashlight to shine onto the white card, BUT you cannot point the flashlight directly at the card! You MUST use your mirrors and maybe the other objects to guide the light beam."
Safety First: Be gentle with the mirrors."
Darken the Room: Dim the lights as much as practical to make the light beams visible.
Investigation Time: Allow groups 10-15 minutes to experiment. Circulate and ask guiding questions:
"What happens when the light hits the mirror?" (It bounces off / changes direction). Introduce the word reflect.
"Can you make the light hit the card using just one mirror?"
"Now try using two mirrors. Can you make the light turn a corner?"
"What happens when the light beam hits the wooden block? Does it go through?" (No, it's blocked). Introduce opaque. "What do you see behind the block?" (A shadow).
"What happens when the light hits the coloured plastic/tissue paper?" (Some light goes through, it might be coloured/dimmer). Do introduce the word translucent.
"Does the light beam look as bright after bouncing off many mirrors?" (Maybe not).
Bringing it Together (Mid-Experiment Pause if needed): Briefly get class attention. "What are you using to make the light change direction?" (Mirrors!). "Is the light bending by itself, or is it bouncing off the mirrors?" (Bouncing/Reflecting).
(10 mins)
Sharing Findings & Space Connection
Light Back On/Gather Attention: Bring the lights back up slightly. Have groups stop experimenting and focus.
Feedback Discussion (using prompt questions):
"How were you able to change the way the light travelled?" (Using mirrors to reflect it).
"Could you make it bend around corners?" (Yes, by reflecting it!). Reinforce: Light travels straight until it hits something.
"Was the type of material important?" (Yes! Mirrors reflect, opaque blocks, translucent lets some through).
"Did you see shadows? What shape were they?" (Yes, same shape as the opaque object).
"Did the light beam change?" (Maybe got dimmer/weaker after reflecting many times or going further).
Connecting to the Moon: Show the image of the Moon. "Think about our Moon. Does it make its own light like the Sun or a lamp?" (No). "So how can we see it shining so brightly at night?"
The BIG Reveal: Show the Sun -> Moon -> Earth diagram. "It's just like your experiment! The Sun is the light source. Light travels in a straight line from the Sun, hits the Moon, and then reflects (bounces) off the Moon in straight lines towards our eyes on Earth! We see the Moon because it's reflecting sunlight."
Other Space Objects: "Planets and even comets that we see also shine because they are reflecting sunlight, just like the Moon!"
(5 mins)
Evaluate: Checking Understanding/Recap: "So, light travels in ______ lines. Mirrors ______ light. Opaque objects ______ light and make shadows. We see the Moon because it ______ sunlight." (Fill in blanks: straight, reflect, block, reflects).
records.label_note: G5 Team, we need to talk about the End of Unit task - points were outlined from Week 4. Glass rooms booked for 29th May - need to confirm with Leadership what we are doing once we have agreed on what we actually wish to do. Possibly on (Sunday, 11.5.2025)

meta.objectives:
To be able to::
State that light appears to travel in straight lines.
Demonstrate that mirrors can change the direction of light (reflection).
Distinguish between opaque objects (which block light and create shadows) and translucent objects (which let some light through).
Explain that we see the Moon because it reflects sunlight.
meta.assessment: Activity: Thumbs up/down: "Light travels straight?" (Up). "Mirrors bend light?" (Down - they reflect). "We see the Moon because it makes light?" (Down). "We can see the Moon because it reflects light" (Up).
meta.differentiation:
Support:
Provide diagrams showing simple one-mirror reflections as a guide.
Focus vocabulary on "bounce," "block," "goes through."

Challenge:
Challenge groups to hit the target using three mirrors.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
What Do We Know About Stars?

Starter (5 mins)
Ask: “What comes to mind when you hear the word star?”

Stars in the Solar System | Science for Kids
A child-friendly introduction to stars, including the Sun and other stars in our galaxy.
Watch here

Brief whole-class discussion:
Record 3–5 key ideas on the board.

Star Life Cycle Worksheet
Students can label and learn about different stages in a star's life.
Star-life-cycle-worksheet.pdf

2. Group Mind Map Activity (10 mins)
In mixed-ability groups, children create a quick mind map titled: “Stars”.

Encourage at least 3–4 branches (e.g., what they are, what they do, questions, the Sun).

Support with visuals or sentence starters for those who need it.

📚 Vocabulary Support
Star Topic Words on Stars - Printable Vocabulary Cards Colorful cards featuring star-themed vocabulary to aid independent writing and discussions. Vocabulary for Stars

3. Class Sharing & Question Generation (10 mins)
Each group shares one idea or question from their mind map.

As a class, co-create a list of “Star Questions” on the board (e.g., Are all stars the same?).

4. Research Sprint (10 mins)
Children use books, pre-selected websites, or printed fact sheets to research 1–2 questions.

(Simplified texts to support and challenge questions for more able.).

5. Quick Review & Exit Task (5 mins)
Ask: “What’s one new thing you found out today?”
Children write or draw a quick fact card to place on the “Star Wall.”

meta.objectives:
To generate and research questions about stars.

To understand that the Sun is a star.
meta.assessment: Exit cards showing one accurate fact.
meta.differentiation:
Support:
Guided fact-finding sheet.
Challenge
Encourage forming and researching their own related question.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
The Life cycle of a Star
(Can be done over 2 sessions)
1. Hook (5 mins)
Show 1-minute animation or image sequence of the star life cycle.

🎥 Videos
Life Cycle of a Star | KS2 Science | STEM and Beyond An informative video explaining how stars are formed and their life cycle. How stars are formed and their life cycle
Life Cycle of a Star | KS2 Science (YouTube)
NASA eClips: Life Cycle of a Star (YouTube)

Ask: “Did you know stars could die? What did you notice?”

2. Lifecycle Overview (5 mins)
Brief explanation with visuals or flashcards of 4 simplified stages:

Life Cycle of a Star Labelling Worksheet (Teacher-Made)
A differentiated worksheet where students label the stages of a star's life cycle.
Life Cycle of a Star Sheet

Keep vocabulary accessible and visual.

3. Group Creative Task (25 mins)
In pairs or groups, children create a mini lifecycle exhibit:

Options:

Folded paper timeline

Simple poster with labels and facts

Mini-booklet with one stage per page

Digital slide (if ICT available)

Provide templates and vocabulary banks as needed.

4. Gallery Walk & Quick Assessment (5 mins)
Class walks around the room to view each group’s exhibit.

Teacher collects or observes the final product.

meta.objectives:
To understand that stars are born, live, and die.

To create a mini-presentation or model to show their understanding.
meta.assessment: Use sticky notes: One cool thing I learned (peer assessment).
meta.differentiation:
Support:
 Use vocabulary to create captions for each stage.

Challenge:
Add extra detail about star types or outcomes (e.g., black hole vs white dwarf).

meta.standards:
Reading Literature (RL)
RL.5.4 – Determine the meaning of words and phrases as they are used in a text, including figurative language such as metaphors and similes. RL.5.9 – Compare and contrast stories in the same genre on their approaches to similar themes and topics.
EE.RL.5.4 – Determine the meaning of words and phrases in a literary text. EE.RL.5.9 – Compare characters, settings, or events in stories.
Reading Informational (RI)
RI.5.4 – Determine the meaning of general academic and domain-specific words and phrases in a text. RI.5.9 – Integrate information from several texts on the same topic.
EE.RI.5.4 – Determine the meaning of words and phrases in an informational text. EE.RI.5.9 – Compare details from two texts on the same topic.
Language (L)
L.5.3.A – Expand, combine, and reduce sentences for meaning, reader interest, and style. L.5.5.A – Interpret figurative language, including similes and metaphors, in context. L.5.5.B – Recognize and explain the meaning of common idioms, adages, and proverbs.
EE.L.5.5 – Demonstrate understanding of word relationships and figurative language
Writing (W) – Narrative Writing
W.5.3 – Write narratives to develop real or imagined experiences or events using effective technique, descriptive details, and clear event sequences. W.5.3.A – Orient the reader by establishing a situation and introducing a narrator and/or characters. W.5.3.B – Use narrative techniques, such as dialogue, description, and pacing. W.5.3.C – Use a variety of transitional words, phrases, and clauses. W.5.3.D – Use concrete words and phrases and sensory details to convey experiences and events precisely. W.5.3.E – Provide a conclusion that follows from the narrated experiences or events.
EE.W.5.3 – Write about events or personal experiences using details.

Guided Reading:/Grammar/Writing:
Students will be assessed by the completion of their final project. (flyer, poem, and/or writing.

Support will be given as needed. (Sentence stems, paired collaborations or an extended assignment for those who are capable.)

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text: Review/Catch Up
records.label_note: No Exhibition this year

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Key Vocabulary
Rain, Soil, Plants, Water, Grow, Earth systems, Interact

What Helps Plants Grow? (5 minutes)
Show a plant, cup of soil, and spray bottle.
Ask:
“What does a plant need to grow?”
“Where does water come from?”
“What does soil do?”
Record responses
Explicit Teaching (10 minutes)
Assessment question picture.
Explain:
Rain gives water.
Soil holds water and nutrients.
Plants absorb water and nutrients from the soil.
Plants grow because rain and soil help them.

Teacher model:
“Rain gives water to the soil. The soil helps the plants grow.”
Create an anchor chart:

Build the Sentence (10 minutes)
Students repeat and complete sentence frames:
Rain gives __________.
Soil helps __________.
Plants use __________ to grow.
 Exit Ticket (5 minutes)
Oral response or written:
“How do rain, soil, and plants work together?”
records.label_note: No Exhibition this year

meta.objectives:
Explain how rain, soil, and plants work together.

Success Criteria
I can name the parts in the picture (rain, soil, plants).
I can explain how they help each other.
I can write one complete sentence about how they interact.
meta.standards:
Understand that Earth is made of different systems (water, land, air, and living things).
Describe how Earth’s systems work together to support life.
Gather evidence from pictures and observations.
meta.assessment:
Assessment Opportunities
Level of participation in discussion
Completed drawing and sentence
Exit response
meta.differentiation:
Support
Vocabulary bank with visual aids
Incomplete sentence prompts:
“Rain provides __________.”
“The soil helps __________.”
Challenge & Extension
Compose a pair of sentences detailing the connection between three systems - of rain, soil and plant.

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Key Vocabulary
Bar graph, Data, Most, Least, Oceans, Ice, Lakes, Rivers

Which Stack Is Taller? (5 minutes)
Create three stacks of cubes.
Ask:
“Which stack has the most cubes?”
“How do you know?”
Explicit Teaching (10 minutes)
Display the assessment graph.
Teach:
Read the labels.
Look at the height of each bar.
The tallest bar represents the greatest amount.
Identify:
Oceans = tallest bar
Ice = medium bar
Lakes and rivers = shortest bar
 Guided Practice (10 minutes)
Ask:
Which bar is tallest?
Which has the least?
Which category has the most water?
Students point out which is which.
Data Talk (10 minutes)
Students to give varying points about the graph using “data talk”:
The tallest bar shows the most
The least is shown by the shortest bar
There are more oceans than ice because…
Rivers are less than…
Exit Ticket (5 minutes)
Students answer:
“Where is most of Earth’s water found?”
Finish off after responses recorded:
Over 96%–97% of Earth's water is found in the oceans. This saline water covers roughly 71% of the Earth's surface. While the oceans are the largest reservoir, the remaining 3% is freshwater, mostly locked in glaciers and ice caps.
records.label_note: No Exhibition this year

meta.objectives:
Identify which bar shows the greatest amount.

Success Criteria
I can read a bar graph.
I can find the tallest bar.
I can tell which category has the most.
meta.standards:
Read and interpret simple graphs.
Compare quantities using visual data.
Understand that most of Earth’s water is found in the oceans.
meta.assessment:
Assessment Opportunities
Oral questioning
Exit sentence
meta.differentiation:
Support
Highlight the tallest bar
Provide sentence frame:
“The tallest bar is ______.”
Extension
Encourage students to compare all categories:
“Oceans have more water than ice and lakes.”

records.subject: explorers
records.cell_text:
Key Vocabulary
Recycle, Reduce, Reuse, Save water, Plant trees, Protect, Resources, Community
Sorting Good Choices (5 minutes)
Show pictures.
Students sort into:
Helps the Earth
Hurts the Earth
Explicit Teaching (10 minutes)
Discuss ways communities can protect resources:
Recycle paper and plastic
Save water
Plant trees
Pick up litter
Reuse materials
Create anchor chart.
Guided Practice (10 minutes)
Complete sentence frames:
Communities can __________.
We protect water by __________.
People can plant __________.

Independent Writing (10 minutes)
Students write two sentences.
Example:
Communities can recycle and save water. People can plant trees and clean parks.
 Exit Ticket (5 minutes)
Students share one action they can take at home.
records.label_note: No Exhibition this year

meta.objectives:
Identify actions communities can take to protect Earth’s resources.
Success Criteria
I can name ways to help the Earth.
I can explain how communities protect resources.
I can write two complete sentences.
meta.standards:
Recognize ways people conserve natural resources.
Describe actions that help protect Earth.
Communicate ideas in complete sentences.
meta.assessment:
Assessment Opportunities
Picture sorting
Completed writing
Exit sharing
meta.differentiation:
Support
Word bank with pictures
Sentence starters
Extension
Students explain why these actions are important.
